name: APPLICATIONS OF RENEWABLE ENERGY IN FOOD PROCESSING

topics.title: Overview of Renewable Energy Sources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT866 - APPLICATIONS OF RENEWABLE ENERGY IN FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Overview of Renewable Energy Sources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Renewable Energy Source (RES)** is defined as any form of energy derived from natural, inexhaustible resources that are replenished at a rate faster than or equal to their rate of consumption by human systems. According to the **International Energy Agency (IEA)** and the **MNRE (Ministry of New and Renewable Energy, Government of India)**, renewable energy is energy collected from resources which are naturally replenished on a human time scale, including sunlight, wind, rain, tides, waves, biomass, and geothermal heat.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the context of Food Process Engineering, renewable energy sources are those that can directly or indirectly (via thermal, mechanical, or electrical conversion) substitute conventional fossil fuels in unit operations such as drying, pasteurization, refrigeration, sterilization, and pumping within the food processing industry.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Explanation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Bank Account of Nature**
> Imagine Earth operates like a **bank account with two ledgers**:
> - **Current Account (Fossil Fuels):** You are withdrawing coal, petroleum, and natural gas that took **millions of years** to be deposited. Every withdrawal reduces the principal, and the balance is shrinking fast — eventually, the account goes bankrupt.
> - **Savings Account (Renewables):** Sunlight arrives every morning, wind blows every afternoon, and biomass regrows every season. As long as the Sun exists (another ~5 billion years), the principal never depletes — you only collect the *interest* (incident energy flux).
>
> **Food processing industries** historically over-drew from the Current Account. The shift to renewables is simply switching the energy budget of a dairy plant, rice mill, or bakery from the depleting ledger to the infinite one — without compromising the **product quality, throughput, or shelf-life**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Solar Constant (Isc)** = **1361 W/m²** (Total Solar Irradiance at the top of Earth's atmosphere, per WMO 2017 revised value)
- **Extra-terrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface (Gon)** ≈ **1361 W/m²** (at sun–Earth mean distance)
- **Global solar radiation on Earth's surface** ranges between **100 – 1000 W/m²** (depending on latitude, season, cloud cover)
- **Wind Power Density (WPD)** is measured in **W/m²**
- **Standard Calorific Values**:
  - Biomass (dry wood): ~**17 MJ/kg**
  - Biogas (methane): ~**35 MJ/m³**
  - Diesel (for comparison): ~**45 MJ/kg**
- **Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE)** is the standardized metric in **$/kWh or ₹/kWh** for cost comparison across technologies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition to memorize for KTU exams:**
> **Capacity Factor (CF)** = Actual energy generated over a year ÷ (Installed rating × 8760 hours). Solar PV typically has a CF of **15–25%**, while onshore wind has **25–40%**.

### 1.4 Why Renewable Energy in Food Processing?

Food processing is one of the most **energy-intensive** industrial sectors globally — consuming roughly **30% of the world's industrial energy** (FAO estimates). In India, food processing accounts for nearly **8–10% of total industrial energy consumption**. The primary energy-demanding operations are:

| Food Processing Unit Operation | Typical Energy Form Needed | Renewable Substitution Possible? |
|---|---|---|
| Drying (grains, fruits, fish) | Thermal (40 – 80 °C) | ✅ Solar, Biomass |
| Pasteurization / Sterilization | Thermal (72 – 121 °C) | ✅ Solar thermal, Biogas |
| Cold storage / Refrigeration | Electrical (compressors) | ✅ Solar PV + Battery |
| Milling / Grinding | Mechanical (rotary) | ✅ Solar PV-driven motors |
| Steam generation (boilers) | Thermal (>100 °C) | ✅ Biomass gasification, Biogas |
| Water pumping (irrigation) | Mechanical | ✅ Solar PV pumps |
| Baking / Roasting | Thermal | ✅ Solar concentrators, Biomass |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Renewable Energy Sources (KTU Critical Topic)

Renewable energy sources are classified into **five major families** based on the physical phenomenon exploited:

1. **Solar Energy** — direct conversion of sunlight (photovoltaic) or its heat (thermal).
2. **Wind Energy** — kinetic energy of moving air masses converted via turbines.
3. **Biomass / Bio-energy** — chemical energy stored via photosynthesis in plants and organic residues.
4. **Geothermal Energy** — thermal energy from the Earth's interior.
5. **Hydraulic Energy** — potential/kinetic energy of falling or flowing water.
6. **Ocean Energy** — Tidal, Wave, Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC).
7. **Hydrogen (Green)** — produced via electrolysis powered by renewables.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

#### A. Solar Energy Conversion Pathway
1. **Collection** → Sunlight strikes a surface (flat plate, concentrator, or PV cell).
2. **Absorption** → Photons with energy $\geq$ band gap excite electrons (PV) or heat a working fluid (thermal).
3. **Conversion** → DC electricity (PV) or hot water/air (thermal).
4. **Storage** → Batteries (electrical) or PCM/thermal mass (thermal).
5. **Utilization** → Powering food processing loads (motors, heaters, refrigerators).

#### B. Wind Energy Conversion Pathway
1. **Aerodynamic Capture** → Windstream impinges on airfoil-shaped blades.
2. **Lift Generation** → Pressure differential creates rotational torque.
3. **Mechanical Transmission** → Gearbox steps up rotation to generator speed.
4. **Electrical Generation** → Induction/PMG generator produces AC.
5. **Grid Synchronization / Local Use** → Direct mechanical drive (e.g., grain grinder) is also possible in off-grid food processing.

#### C. Biomass Energy Pathway
1. **Feedstock Preparation** → Drying, chipping, pelletizing agricultural residues (rice husk, bagasse, coconut shell).
2. **Thermochemical Conversion** → Combustion, Pyrolysis, Gasification.
3. **Biochemical Conversion** → Anaerobic digestion → Biogas; Fermentation → Bioethanol.
4. **Energy Output** → Steam, syngas, electricity, liquid biofuels.
5. **End Use** → Boiler feed for food industry (rice parboiling, dairy sterilization).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **These formulas are the most frequently tested in KTU Module 1. Memorize units along with symbols.**

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_T = I_b R_b + I_d (1 + \cos\beta)/2 + I (1 - \cos\beta)/2$ | $I_T$ = Total hourly radiation; $I_b$ = beam; $I_d$ = diffuse; $\beta$ = tilt | W/m² | Solar collector design |
| 2 | $P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3} C_p$ | $P$ = power; $\rho$ = air density; $A$ = swept area; $V$ = wind speed; $C_p$ = power coefficient | W | Wind turbine sizing |
| 3 | $C_p^{Betz} = \tfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ | Theoretical maximum efficiency of a wind turbine | dimensionless | Performance benchmark |
| 4 | $CF = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{P_{rated} \times 8760}$ | Capacity factor | dimensionless | Plant performance |
| 5 | $LCOE = \dfrac{\sum (CAPEX + OPEX)_t \, / \, (1+r)^t}{\sum E_{t} \, / \, (1+r)^t}$ | Levelized Cost of Energy | ₹/kWh | Economic comparison |
| 6 | $HHV_{biomass} = 0.3491\,C + 1.1783\,H + 0.1005\,S - 0.1034\,O - 0.0151\,N - 0.0211\,A$ | Dulong's formula — Higher Heating Value from ultimate analysis | MJ/kg | Biomass boiler design |
| 7 | $Q = m C_p \Delta T$ | Heat energy; $m$ = mass; $C_p$ = specific heat; $\Delta T$ = temp rise | J or kJ | Solar dryer sizing |
| 8 | $E_{biogas} = V_{biogas} \times CHV_{biogas}$ | Energy in biogas per unit volume | MJ/m³ | Anaerobic digester design |
| 9 | $\eta_{collector} = \dfrac{Q_u}{I_T \cdot A_c}$ | Useful heat gain over incident solar energy | dimensionless | Flat plate collector efficiency |
| 10 | $Payback = \dfrac{Total\, Investment}{Annual\, Cash\, Savings}$ | Simple payback period | years | Project feasibility |

> [!TIP]
> **Avoid markdown-breaking pipes:** Absolute value of $x$ is written as $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\vert x \vert$, never as $|x|$ inside table cells.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Food Processing Engineering

- **Solar Tunnel Dryers** in Kerala (coconut, pepper, cardamom, turmeric) use **$Q = m C_p \Delta T$** logic to size the collector area.
- **Rice Parboiling Units** powered by **rice husk gasifiers** are direct applications of **biomass energy** with **HHV calculations**.
- **Dairy cold chain** in rural India uses **solar PV refrigeration** based on **battery sizing and CF analysis**.
- **Coconut oil expellers** in coastal Kerala can be coupled to **wind-solar hybrid** systems for uninterrupted operation during monsoons (low solar, high wind).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Wind Power Available in a Stream Tube

We start from fundamental fluid mechanics. The kinetic energy per second (i.e., power) of an air mass $m$ moving at velocity $V$ is:

$$
P = \tfrac{1}{2} \dot{m} V^{2}
$$

The mass flow rate of air through a circular swept area $A$ is:

$$
\dot{m} = \rho A V
$$

Substituting into the power equation:

$$
P = \tfrac{1}{2} (\rho A V) V^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3}
$$

This is the **gross wind power** flowing through the rotor disc. However, a real turbine cannot extract **100%** of this power (which would require the air to come to a complete halt, violating continuity). A turbine extracts a fraction $C_p$ called the **power coefficient**:

$$
P_{turbine} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3} C_p
$$

**Betz Limit:** Theoretically, $C_p \leq 16/27 \approx 0.593$ (Albert Betz, 1919). Hence the **maximum extractable power per unit area** is:

$$
P_{max,per\,area} = \tfrac{8}{27} \rho V^{3} \approx 0.296 \rho V^{3}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the cubic relationship matters:** Doubling the wind speed **eight-folds** the available power. This is why wind farm siting in Kerala (Wayanad, Idukki hills with strong south-west monsoon winds) is so attractive.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Solar Collector Tilt Angle for a Tropical Location

The angle of incidence of solar radiation on a tilted surface depends on the **declination angle** $\delta$, the **latitude** $\phi$, the **hour angle** $\omega$, and the **surface tilt angle** $\beta$.

**Step 1 — Declination angle (Cooper's equation):**

$$
\delta = 23.45^{\circ} \cdot \sin\left(\dfrac{360}{365}(284 + n)\right)
$$

where $n$ is the day number of the year (1 Jan = 1).

**Step 2 — Solar zenith angle $\theta_z$ on a horizontal surface:**

$$
\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega
$$

**Step 3 — Angle of incidence on a south-facing tilted surface (Northern hemisphere):**

$$
\cos\theta_i = \sin\delta \sin(\phi - \beta) + \cos\delta \cos(\phi - \beta) \cos\omega
$$

**Step 4 — Optimum tilt for maximum annual yield:** For a fixed-tilt collector, the empirical rule is:

$$
\beta_{optimum} \approx \phi - 10^{\circ}\, \text{to}\, \phi - 15^{\circ}
$$

**Example:** For Trivandrum ($\phi \approx 8.5^{\circ}$ N), optimum tilt is essentially **horizontal ($\beta \approx 0°$)**, while for Srinagar ($\phi \approx 34°$ N), it is about **20–25°**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Biogas Yield from a Food Processing Waste

For an anaerobic digester treating food industry waste (e.g., dairy effluent, fruit pulp waste), the theoretical methane yield is derived from the **Buswell equation**:

$$
C_n H_a O_b + \left(n - \dfrac{a}{4} - \dfrac{b}{2}\right) H_2 O \rightarrow \left(\dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4}\right) CH_4 + \left(\dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{a}{8} - \dfrac{b}{4}\right) CO_2
$$

For a typical food waste with empirical formula $C_6 H_{10} O_5$ (representing carbohydrate-rich residue):

- $n = 6, \, a = 10, \, b = 5$
- Moles of $CH_4$ produced = $6/2 - 10/8 + 5/4 = 3 - 1.25 + 1.25 = 3$
- Moles of $CO_2$ produced = $3 + 1.25 - 1.25 = 3$

So the **biogas composition is 50% CH₄ + 50% CO₂** (theoretically), with a methane yield of **3 mol CH₄ per mol of substrate**.

**Energy in the methane produced** (at STP, molar volume = 22.4 L/mol, calorific value of methane = 55.5 MJ/kg = 39.8 MJ/m³):

$$
E_{methane} = n_{CH_4} \times 22.4 \times 10^{-3} \times 39.8 \quad \text{[MJ]}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Energy Sources vs. Food Processing Suitability

| Parameter | Solar PV | Solar Thermal | Wind | Biomass (Direct) | Biogas | Geothermal |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Form of energy | Electrical | Heat | Electrical/Mechanical | Heat | Heat/Mechanical | Heat |
| Capacity factor | 15–25 % | 20–30 % | 25–40 % | 60–85 % | 70–90 % | 80–95 % |
| Best food processing use | Cold chain, milling | Drying, pasteurization | Milling, pumping | Steam generation | Boiler, cooking | Pasteurization |
| Storage feasibility | Battery (medium) | PCM (high) | None (low) | Fuel stock (high) | Gas holder (high) | Seasonal (medium) |
| Kerala-specific viability | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★ (limited hot springs) |
| Capital cost (₹/kW) | 40,000–55,000 | 25,000–40,000 | 60,000–80,000 | 15,000–25,000 | 30,000–50,000 | 100,000+ |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize Kerala context:** Kerala's latitude (~8° N), tropical climate, and abundant agricultural residues (coconut, rice, spices) make **solar thermal + biomass hybrid systems** the most relevant renewable energy combination for food processing in the state — a frequently asked point in KTU ESE.

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — A Solar-Biomass Hybrid Food Drying System

```mermaid
graph TD

    SOLARCOLL[Solar Flat Plate Collector Array] -->|Hot Air 60 to 90 C| CHAMBER
    BIOBOIL[Biomass Back up Boiler] -->|Hot Air when solar insufficient| CHAMBER
    CHAMBER -->|Moisture laden air| EXH[Exhaust with Moisture]
    EXH --> FAN[Axial Fan powered by Solar PV]
    CHAMBER -->|Dried product| QC[Quality Check: Moisture Content less than 10 percent]
    QC --> PACK[Packaging]
    PACK --> STORE[Storage Warehouse]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1]**
> **Q1. Define renewable energy. List any four renewable energy sources with one example of food processing application for each.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks Distribution):**

- **Definition (1 Mark):** Renewable energy is energy obtained from natural sources that are replenished at a rate faster than or equal to the rate at which they are consumed. Examples: solar, wind, biomass, geothermal, hydro.
- **Four Sources with Food Processing Applications (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**
  1. **Solar** → Solar tunnel dryer for drying cardamom, turmeric, and fish.
  2. **Wind** → Windmill coupled to flour mill in hilly regions.
  3. **Biomass** → Rice husk fired boiler for parboiling rice.
  4. **Biogas** → Anaerobic digester in dairy processing producing cooking gas and steam.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2]**
> **Q2. Differentiate between renewable and non-renewable energy sources. State two advantages of using renewable energy in the food processing industry.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Renewable | Non-Renewable |
|---|---|---|
| Replenishment rate | Faster than consumption | Slower (geological timescale) |
| Carbon emissions | Near zero | High CO₂ |
| Examples | Solar, wind, biomass | Coal, petroleum, natural gas |
| Long-term availability | Inexhaustible | Finite, depleting |

**Two advantages in food processing (2 × 1 = 2 Marks):**
1. **Reduces operational cost** in the long run due to zero fuel cost (solar/wind) and lower LCOE.
2. **Decentralizes energy access** — rural food processing units (small dairy, rice mill, spice dryer) can be energy-independent, improving product quality and shelf-life.
3. *(Bonus point)* Reduces carbon footprint, qualifying for **carbon credit** benefits and green certification.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 1]**
> **Question A (14 Marks) — Attempt either A OR B**
>
> **A (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the various renewable energy sources and their applications in food processing industries. Mention the climatic suitability of each source for Kerala state. **`[7 Marks]`**
>
> **A (b)** A flat plate solar collector of area 2.5 m² receives a daily average global solar radiation of 5.5 kWh/m²/day. If the collector efficiency is 60%, calculate (i) the useful heat energy collected per day, and (ii) the mass of water that can be heated from 25 °C to 80 °C using this energy. Take $C_p$ for water = 4.18 kJ/kg·K. **`[7 Marks]`**

**Model Solution — A(a):**

- **Block diagram (3 Marks):** Draw a classification chart as in Section 4.1 — sources, sub-types, and applications.
- **Applications in food processing (2 Marks):** Mention specific Kerala-context applications:
  - Solar PV → Cold chain in fisheries (Kerala coast).
  - Solar Thermal → Copra drying, turmeric drying.
  - Biomass → Rice husk boilers in Kuttanad paddy processing.
  - Biogas → Dairy effluent treatment + cooking in cheese units.
  - Wind → Hybrid wind-solar spice dryers in Wayanad.
- **Kerala climatic suitability (2 Marks):** Kerala receives **4.5–5.5 kWh/m²/day** average solar radiation, has good south-west monsoon winds (5–7 m/s average in hills), and abundant **agricultural residue** (coconut, rice, areca). Tidal potential is high along the **Kozhikode–Kannur coast**.

**Model Solution — A(b):**

Given:
- $A_c = 2.5 \text{ m}^2$
- $I_{avg} = 5.5 \text{ kWh/m}^2\text{/day}$
- $\eta_c = 0.60$
- $\Delta T = 80 - 25 = 55 \text{ °C} = 55 \text{ K}$
- $C_p = 4.18 \text{ kJ/kg·K} = 1.16 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kWh/kg·K}$

**Step 1 — Total incident solar energy per day:**

$$
E_{incident} = I_{avg} \times A_c = 5.5 \times 2.5 = 13.75 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Useful heat collected (Part i):**

$$
Q_u = \eta_c \times E_{incident} = 0.60 \times 13.75 = 8.25 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

Converting to kJ: $8.25 \times 3600 = 29{,}700 \text{ kJ/day}$

**Step 3 — Mass of water heated (Part ii):**

$$
m_{water} = \dfrac{Q_u}{C_p \cdot \Delta T} = \dfrac{29{,}700}{4.18 \times 55} = \dfrac{29{,}700}{229.9} \approx 129.2 \text{ kg}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- (i) **8.25 kWh/day (or 29,700 kJ/day)** of useful heat
- (ii) **~129 kg of water** can be heated per day

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — 14-mark numerical:**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Forgetting to convert kWh → kJ before using $Q = m C_p \Delta T$ (loses 2 marks).
> - **Common mistake 2:** Using $C_p = 4.18 \text{ J/kg·K}$ instead of kJ — unit inconsistency leads to wrong magnitude.
> - **Common mistake 3:** Not clearly distinguishing between **incident energy** and **useful heat** — the 60% efficiency must be applied to the incident radiation, not the final temperature rise.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 1, Alternative Choice]**
> **Question B (14 Marks)**
>
> **B (a)** Explain the working principle of a biogas plant. Derive the energy content of biogas from a typical food industry waste having empirical formula $C_6 H_{10} O_5$. State the applications of biogas in food processing. **`[7 Marks]`**
>
> **B (b)** A wind turbine has a rotor diameter of 8 m installed at a location where the average wind speed is 9 m/s. Air density is 1.2 kg/m³. Calculate (i) the gross wind power flowing through the rotor, (ii) the maximum power extractable by the turbine using Betz limit, and (iii) the daily energy generation if the turbine operates at 25% of the Betz limit for 10 hours/day. **`[7 Marks]`**

**Model Solution — B(a):**

- **Working principle (3 Marks):** A biogas plant digests organic waste anaerobically in a sealed chamber. The four biological stages are **Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis**. Methanogenic bacteria (*Methanobacterium*) convert volatile fatty acids into **CH₄ + CO₂**, yielding 50–70% methane by volume.
- **Derivation of energy content (3 Marks):** Using the Buswell equation with $C_6 H_{10} O_5$:
  - 3 moles CH₄ + 3 moles CO₂ per mole of substrate.
  - Volume at STP: $3 \times 22.4 = 67.2$ L of CH₄.
  - Energy: $67.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 39.8 = 2.674$ MJ/mol of substrate.
- **Applications in food processing (1 Mark):** Steam generation in dairy, cooking fuel in bakery, electricity in rice mill via dual-fuel engine.

**Model Solution — B(b):**

Given:
- $D = 8 \text{ m} \Rightarrow A = \pi D^2 / 4 = \pi (8)^2 / 4 = 50.27 \text{ m}^2$
- $V = 9 \text{ m/s}$
- $\rho = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $C_p^{Betz} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$

**Step 1 — Gross wind power (i):**

$$
P_{gross} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A V^3 = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 50.27 \times (9)^3
$$

$$
P_{gross} = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 50.27 \times 729 = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 36{,}646.83 = 21{,}988 \text{ W} \approx 22 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum extractable power (Betz limit) (ii):**

$$
P_{Betz} = 0.593 \times 21{,}988 = 13{,}039 \text{ W} \approx 13.04 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Actual power (25% of Betz):**

$$
P_{actual} = 0.25 \times 13{,}039 = 3259.7 \text{ W} \approx 3.26 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Daily energy (iii):**

$$
E_{daily} = P_{actual} \times t = 3.26 \times 10 = 32.6 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Wind energy problems:**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Using diameter in $A = \pi D^2 / 4$ formula — frequently students forget $\pi$ or use $D$ instead of $D^2$.
> - **Common mistake 2:** Confusing **Betz coefficient (16/27)** with **maximum power fraction (8/27)** — Betz says $C_p \leq 16/27$, and the **maximum power** is $C_p \times P_{gross} = 0.593 \times P_{gross}$.
> - **Common mistake 3:** Assuming the turbine runs 24 hours — wind speeds vary, and the question typically specifies operating hours; here it is explicitly 10 hours/day.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Renewable Energy** = Replenishable, inexhaustible, low-carbon energy from natural flows (solar, wind, biomass, geo, hydro, ocean, H₂).
- [x] **Solar Constant** = **1361 W/m²** (WMO revised, 2017).
- [x] **Wind Power** $\propto V^3$ — cubic dependence on wind speed is critical for site selection.
- [x] **Betz Limit** = **16/27 ≈ 0.593** — theoretical max $C_p$.
- [x] **Capacity Factor** = Solar PV **15–25%**, Wind **25–40%**, Biomass/Biogas **60–90%**.
- [x] **Kerala's strength**: Solar thermal + Biomass (rice husk, coconut shell) for food processing.
- [x] **Buswell equation** governs theoretical biogas yield from organic substrates.
- [x] **Dulong's formula** for biomass HHV from ultimate (C, H, O, N, S, A) analysis.
- [x] **Food processing unit operations** linked to renewables: drying, pasteurization, refrigeration, milling, pumping, steam generation.
- [x] **LCOE** is the key economic comparison metric — biomass and solar in India are now cheaper than diesel-based generation.
- [x] **Kerala average solar radiation**: ~**4.5–5.5 kWh/m²/day** — ideal for solar drying.
- [x] **Mnemonic to remember 7 RES families**: **"S W B H G O H"** → Sun, Wind, Biomass, Hydro, Geo, Ocean, Hydrogen.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar Energy: Principles, Technologies (Solar Thermal, Photovoltaic), and Applications in Food Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT866 - APPLICATIONS OF RENEWABLE ENERGY IN FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Solar Energy: Principles, Technologies (Solar Thermal, Photovoltaic), and Applications in Food Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Solar Energy?

**Solar energy** is the radiant light and heat emitted by the Sun, harnessed through two principal pathways: **(i) Solar Thermal** (where sunlight is absorbed and converted into usable heat) and **(ii) Solar Photovoltaic (PV)** (where sunlight is directly converted into electricity via the photoelectric effect).

The Sun is essentially a **G-type main-sequence star** continuously performing hydrogen-to-helium fusion at its core, releasing energy at the rate governed by the **Stefan–Boltzmann law**. The Earth intercepts only a tiny fraction of this energy, yet it represents a virtually inexhaustible energy flux.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight – Solar Constant (I_sc)**
> The **solar constant** is defined as the rate at which solar energy is received per unit area on a surface perpendicular to the Sun's rays at the **mean distance of the Earth from the Sun**, outside the Earth's atmosphere.
>
> **I_sc = 1367 W/m²** (≈ 1.361 kW/m²)
>
> This is the **extraterrestrial irradiance** on a surface normal to the solar beam at the edge of the atmosphere.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The Sun as a "Cosmic Furnace"

Imagine the Sun as a **perpetual nuclear pressure cooker** operating at ~15 million Kelvin at its core. Every second, it fuses ~620 million tons of hydrogen into helium, converting ~4 million tons of mass into pure energy via **E = mc²**. From our planet's perspective, this energy travels ~150 million kilometers (1 AU) as electromagnetic radiation in ~8 minutes. When this "light parcel" strikes a black surface, it's like holding a dark-colored tray under a hot lamp — the surface warms up. **Solar thermal technology** captures this heat directly, while **PV technology** treats each incoming photon as a tiny "energy coin" that knocks electrons free from a silicon crystal lattice, generating electricity.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Solar constant | I_sc | **1367** | W/m² |
| Mean Earth–Sun distance | 1 AU | **1.496 × 10⁸** | km |
| Solar declination (max) | δ_max | ±**23.45** | degrees |
| Earth's axial tilt | ε | **23.45** | degrees |
| Speed of light | c | **3 × 10⁸** | m/s |
| Earth's rotation | 15°/hour | — | deg/hr |
| Air Mass at zenith (AM 1.5) | AM | **1.5** | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Air Mass (AM)** represents the path length of sunlight through the atmosphere relative to the path at zenith (directly overhead). The **AM 1.5 spectrum** is the standard reference for PV module rating (1000 W/m²), corresponding to a solar zenith angle of ~48.2°.

## 1.4 Spectral Composition of Sunlight at Earth's Surface

After atmospheric attenuation, the usable solar spectrum at ground level spans roughly **0.3 μm (UV) to 2.5 μm (NIR)**, with peak energy near **0.48 μm** (visible green-blue). About **99%** of solar energy is contained in the **0.3 – 2.4 μm** band, which is critical for selecting absorber coatings in solar thermal collectors and bandgap engineering in PV cells.

![Solar spectrum band illustration placeholder]
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Solar Geometry – The "Where is the Sun?" Framework

To design any solar system, one must first mathematically locate the Sun relative to a tilted collector on Earth. Three fundamental angles govern this geometry:

### 2.1.1 Solar Declination (δ)
The angular position of the Sun at solar noon with respect to the equatorial plane. It varies from **−23.45° (winter solstice)** to **+23.45° (summer solstice)**.

### 2.1.2 Hour Angle (ω)
The angular displacement of the Sun from the local meridian due to Earth's rotation (15° per hour). Negative in the morning, zero at solar noon, positive in the afternoon.

### 2.1.3 Latitude (φ) and Surface Tilt (β)
The collector's geographic latitude and its tilt angle from the horizontal.

## 2.2 Components of Solar Radiation

Terrestrial solar radiation is classified into three components:
- **Direct (Beam) Radiation (I_b)**: Travels in a straight line from the Sun.
- **Diffuse Radiation (I_d)**: Scattered by clouds, dust, and atmospheric molecules.
- **Reflected (Albedo) Radiation (I_r)**: Bounced from ground surfaces (snow, water, sand).

The **global radiation on a tilted surface** is the sum:
$$I_T = I_b \cdot R_b + I_d \cdot \left(\frac{1 + \cos\beta}{2}\right) + (I_b + I_d) \cdot \rho \cdot \left(\frac{1 - \cos\beta}{2}\right)$$

where R_b is the **tilt factor** (ratio of cos θ on tilted surface to cos θ on horizontal) and ρ is **albedo** (0.2 for grass, 0.8 for fresh snow).

## 2.3 Solar Thermal Technology – The "Heat Trap"

A **solar thermal collector** absorbs incoming shortwave solar radiation and converts it to longwave thermal energy. The performance hinges on the **absorber plate's spectral selectivity**: high absorptivity (α) in the solar band and low emissivity (ε) in the IR band.

### 2.3.1 Flat Plate Collector (FPC) Anatomy
1. **Transparent cover** (glass/tedlar) – reduces convective losses.
2. **Absorber plate** (selective coating, e.g., black chrome) – converts photons to heat.
3. **Fluid tubes/risers** – carry the working fluid (water, air, oil).
4. **Insulation** (mineral wool, polyurethane) – suppresses back/side losses.
5. **Casing** – protects from weather.

### 2.3.2 Concentrating Solar Collectors (CSC)
These use mirrors/lenses to focus direct beam radiation onto a small receiver, achieving temperatures of **150 °C – 1000+ °C**:
- **Parabolic Trough (PTC)**: Linear focus, 250–400 °C.
- **Parabolic Dish**: Point focus, 500–1500 °C.
- **Central Receiver (Power Tower)**: Field of heliostats, 600–1000 °C.
- **Fresnel Reflector**: Flat mirrors, 100–300 °C.

## 2.4 Photovoltaic (PV) Technology – The "Photon to Electron" Engine

A **solar cell** is essentially a large-area **pn-junction diode** made of semiconductor material (mostly crystalline silicon). When photons with energy ≥ bandgap (E_g) strike the cell:
1. Photons are absorbed in the depletion region.
2. Electron-hole pairs are generated.
3. The built-in electric field separates the charges.
4. Charges flow through an external circuit → **electric current**.

The **I-V characteristic** of an illuminated cell is the superposition of the dark diode current and the photogenerated current.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_{on} = I_{sc}\left(1 + 0.033 \cos\frac{360 n}{365}\right)$ | Extraterrestrial radiation on day *n* of the year | W/m² |
| 2 | $\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left[\frac{360(284 + n)}{365}\right]$ | Solar declination (Cooper's equation) | degrees |
| 3 | $\omega = 15^\circ (t_{solar} - 12)$ | Hour angle (t_solar in hours) | degrees |
| 4 | $\sin\alpha = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$ | Solar altitude angle | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\cos\theta_i = \sin\delta \sin(\phi - \beta) + \cos\delta \cos(\phi - \beta)\cos\omega$ | Angle of incidence (south-facing tilted surface) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $H_o = \frac{24 \times 3600}{\pi} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot E_o \cdot \left(\cos\phi \cos\delta \sin\omega_s + \omega_s \sin\phi \sin\delta\right)$ | Daily extraterrestrial radiation on horizontal | J/m²·day |
| 7 | $Q_u = A_c \, F_R \left[ S - U_L (T_{fi} - T_a) \right]$ | Hottel–Whillier equation (useful heat gain) | W |
| 8 | $F_R = \frac{\dot{m} c_p}{A_c U_L}\left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{A_c U_L F'}{\dot{m} c_p}\right)\right]$ | Heat removal factor | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\eta_c = \frac{Q_u}{A_c \cdot G_T}$ | Instantaneous thermal collector efficiency | % |
| 10 | $I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{q(V + I R_s)}{nkT}\right) - 1\right] - \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$ | Solar cell I–V equation (single-diode model) | A, V |
| 11 | $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} I_{mp}}{V_{oc} I_{sc}}$ | Fill factor of solar cell | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\eta_{PV} = \dfrac{V_{oc} I_{sc} FF}{G_T \cdot A_{PV}}$ | PV module conversion efficiency | % |
| 13 | $\text{STC reference: } G = 1000 \text{ W/m}^2, \ T_{cell} = 25^\circ\text{C}$ | Standard Test Conditions | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight for KTU Valuation**: In numerical problems, examiners expect students to **convert all angles from degrees to radians before feeding into trigonometric functions** (or keep them in degrees consistently). The solar declination formula above is the most tested numerical in Module 1.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Solar geometry** is the foundation of **fixed-tilt, seasonal-tilt, and tracking (1-axis/2-axis) collector design**.
- **Hottel–Whillier equation** is used daily by solar thermal engineers to size **FPC arrays for process heat, domestic water heating, and solar industrial steam**.
- **PV I–V equation** is the basis of **PVsyst, SAM, and Homer Pro** simulation tools for grid-tied and off-grid solar systems.
- In **food processing**, the same math underpins the design of **solar dryers, solar water heaters for CIP (clean-in-place) systems, and PV-powered cold chain equipment**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Solar Declination Angle (Cooper's Equation)

The declination δ is the angle between the Sun–Earth line and the equatorial plane. It can be expressed as a sinusoidal function of the day of the year.

Let the Earth be at any orbital position. Project the Sun–Earth vector onto the equatorial plane. The angle between the actual vector and its projection is δ.

Let *n* be the day of the year (1 = Jan 1, 365 = Dec 31). The declination is maximum (+23.45°) at summer solstice in the Northern Hemisphere, which occurs on **day n = 172** (≈ June 21). The argument of the sine function must equal **90°** when *n* = 172.

$$\delta(n) = 23.45^\circ \sin(\theta(n))$$

Let us set $\theta(n) = \dfrac{360}{365}(n - 81)$ to satisfy δ(81) = 0 (vernal equinox on March 22, day 81):

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\left(\frac{360(284 + n)}{365}\right)$$

> The constant 284 (= 365 − 81) shifts the argument so that the maximum occurs at day 172 and zero crossings at days 81 and 264. The factor 360/365 ≈ 0.9856°/day represents the Earth's mean orbital angular velocity.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Angle of Incidence on a Tilted Surface (South-Facing, γ = 0)

We need to derive the angle between the Sun's rays and the **normal to a tilted surface** facing south. Consider the geometry in the celestial coordinate system.

Let the unit vector along the Sun's direction be **ŝ** and the unit normal to the tilted surface be **n̂**. Then:

$$\cos\theta_i = \mathbf{\hat{s}} \cdot \mathbf{\hat{n}}$$

In spherical coordinates, with zenith along the local vertical, the Sun's direction has components:
- Azimuth from south: $\gamma_s$ (negative east, positive west)
- Zenith angle: $\theta_z = 90^\circ - \alpha$

The surface normal tilts by β from the vertical toward the south.

Expanding the dot product using standard 3-D vector representation:

$$\cos\theta_i = \cos\theta_z \cos\beta + \sin\theta_z \sin\beta \cos(\gamma - \gamma_s)$$

Substitute $\theta_z = \phi - \delta$ components (for south-facing at γ = 0, after manipulation using the identity for solar azimuth):

$$\cos\theta_i = \sin\delta \sin(\phi - \beta) + \cos\delta \cos(\phi - \beta)\cos\omega$$

This is the **classical KTU-tested result** for the angle of incidence on a south-facing tilted surface.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hottel–Whillier Equation (Steady-State Energy Balance on a Flat Plate Collector)

**Step 1: Useful Heat Gain**
Let Q_u be the net useful heat transferred to the working fluid per unit time. The steady-state energy balance on the absorber plate is:

$$Q_u = A_c \left[ S - U_L (T_{pm} - T_a) \right]$$

where $T_{pm}$ is the mean absorber plate temperature, $S$ is the absorbed solar radiation per unit area, and $U_L$ is the overall heat loss coefficient.

**Step 2: Introduce Heat Removal Factor F_R**
The fluid inlet temperature $T_{fi}$ is what the designer controls, not the plate mean temperature. Define the **heat removal factor** as:

$$F_R = \frac{\dot{m}c_p (T_{fo} - T_{fi})}{A_c \left[ S - U_L (T_{fi} - T_a) \right]}$$

**Step 3: Combine**
Substituting and rearranging:

$$Q_u = A_c \, F_R \left[ S - U_L (T_{fi} - T_a) \right]$$

This is the **Hottel–Whillier equation**, the cornerstone of FPC design.

**Step 4: Efficiency**
$$\eta_c = \frac{Q_u}{A_c G_T} = F_R \left[ \tau\alpha - \frac{U_L (T_{fi} - T_a)}{G_T} \right]$$

where $(\tau\alpha)$ is the effective transmittance–absorptance product of the cover/absorber system.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Fill Factor of a Solar Cell (Visual Derivation)

The **fill factor (FF)** is the ratio of the maximum power rectangle to the theoretical rectangle bounded by V_oc and I_sc on the I–V curve.

$$\text{FF} = \frac{V_{mp} I_{mp}}{V_{oc} I_{sc}}$$

For an ideal solar cell (no series resistance, infinite shunt resistance), an empirical relation (Green's approximation) is:

$$FF_0 = \frac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1}$$

where $v_{oc} = \dfrac{q V_{oc}}{n k_B T}$ is the normalized open-circuit voltage.

This shows that **FF increases with V_oc**, which itself rises with the logarithm of light intensity and decreases with temperature — a key reason PV modules lose ~0.4–0.5% efficiency per °C rise.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Solar Collector Types

| Parameter | Flat Plate (FPC) | Evacuated Tube (ETC) | Parabolic Trough (PTC) | Parabolic Dish |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Concentration ratio | 1× | 1× | 15–80× | 500–3000× |
| Operating temp. (°C) | 30–100 | 50–200 | 150–400 | 500–1500 |
| Tracks sun? | No (fixed) | No (fixed) | 1-axis | 2-axis |
| Uses diffuse radiation? | Yes | Yes | No (beam only) | No (beam only) |
| Typical food processing use | Water heating, low-temp drying | Pasteurization, medium-temp drying | Steam generation | Cooking, sterilization |
| Relative cost | Low | Medium | High | Very high |

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** For a location at latitude φ = 13° N (Trivandrum), on 14 October (n = 287), at solar noon (ω = 0), calculate the declination and the angle of incidence on a south-facing surface tilted at β = 15°.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Declination**
$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left[\frac{360(284 + 287)}{365}\right] = 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left[\frac{360 \times 571}{365}\right]$$

Compute the argument: $\dfrac{360 \times 571}{365} = 563.18^\circ$. Subtract 360°: $203.18^\circ$. Then $\sin(203.18^\circ) = -0.4033$.

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \times (-0.4033) = -9.46^\circ$$

**Step 2: Angle of Incidence at Solar Noon (ω = 0)**

$$\cos\theta_i = \sin\delta \sin(\phi - \beta) + \cos\delta \cos(\phi - \beta)$$

Plug in: δ = −9.46°, φ = 13°, β = 15°. So (φ − β) = −2°.

$$\cos\theta_i = \sin(-9.46^\circ)\sin(-2^\circ) + \cos(-9.46^\circ)\cos(-2^\circ)$$
$$\cos\theta_i = (-0.1644)(-0.0349) + (0.9864)(0.9994)$$
$$\cos\theta_i = 0.00574 + 0.98582 = 0.99156$$

$$\theta_i = \cos^{-1}(0.99156) = 7.53^\circ$$

> **Interpretation:** The collector is nearly perpendicular to the Sun's rays — a well-designed tilt for Trivandrum in October.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Solar Radiation Classification & Attenuation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[SUN CORE FUSION REACTION] --> S1[EXTRATERRESTRIAL RADIATION AT TOP OF ATMOSPHERE]
    S1 --> S2[DIRECT BEAM RADIATION I_B]
    S1 --> S3[DIFFUSE RADIATION I_D SCATTERED BY ATMOSPHERE]
    S1 --> S4[REFLECTED ALBEDO RADIATION I_R FROM GROUND]
    S2 --> S5[GLOBAL RADIATION ON TILTED SURFACE I_T]
    S3 --> S5
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6[SOLAR THERMAL HEAT CAPTURE]
    S5 --> S7[PHOTOVOLTAIC ELECTRICITY GENERATION]
    S6 --> S8[FOOD DRYING HEATING COOKING]
    S7 --> S9[FOOD COLD CHAIN PUMPING GRINDING]
```

## 4.2 Flat Plate Solar Collector — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SUN1[SUNLIGHT] --> CV[TRANSPARENT GLASS COVER]
    CV --> AP[SELECTIVE ABSORBER PLATE]
    AP --> RT[FLOW TUBE RISER NETWORK]
    RT --> WTI[COLD WATER INLET]
    RT --> WTO[HOT WATER OUTLET]
    AP -.-> IN[BACK INSULATION LAYER]
    AP -.-> SIDE[SIDE INSULATION]
    WTI --> WTO
    AP -- CONVECTIVE LOSS Q_C --> CV
    AP -- RADIATIVE LOSS Q_R --> ENV[AMBIENT ENVIRONMENT]
    AP -- BACK LOSS Q_B --> IN
```

## 4.3 PV System — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PVS[SUNLIGHT PHOTONS] --> PVC[PV ARRAY OF SERIES PARALLEL MODULES]
    PVC --> CHGC[MPPT CHARGE CONTROLLER]
    CHGC --> BATT[DEEP CYCLE BATTERY BANK DC STORAGE]
    BATT --> INVC[PURPOSE INVERTER DC TO AC]
    INVC --> ACL[AC LOAD FOOD PROCESSING MACHINERY]
    PVC -.-> DCL[DC LOAD DC PUMPS LED LIGHTS]
    BATT -.-> BATC[BATTERY PROTECTION BMS]
```

## 4.4 Solar Food Processing — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SRC[SOLAR RESOURCE I_T] --> A1[SOLAR THERMAL BRANCH]
    SRC --> A2[SOLAR PV BRANCH]
    A1 --> B1[FLAT PLATE COLLECTOR WATER HEATING]
    A1 --> B2[EVACUATED TUBE COLLECTOR PASTEURIZATION]
    A1 --> B3[PARABOLIC TROUGH STEAM GENERATION]
    A1 --> B4[SOLAR TUNNEL DRYER FRUITS FISH GRAINS]
    A2 --> C1[PV PUMP IRRIGATION PROCESS WATER]
    A2 --> C2[PV REFRIGERATION MILK VEGETABLE COLD ROOM]
    A2 --> C3[PV GRINDER MILL SPICES PULSES]
    A2 --> C4[PV ICE MAKING FISH DAIRY]
    B1 --> OUT1[PROCESS HEAT CIP BOILER FEED]
    B2 --> OUT2[PASTEURIZED MILK FRUIT JUICE]
    B3 --> OUT3[STEAM FOR COOKING STERILIZATION]
    B4 --> OUT4[DEHYDRATED FOODS SHELF STABLE]
    C1 --> OUT5[WATER SUPPLY FOR PROCESSING]
    C2 --> OUT6[CHILLED STORAGE EXTENDED SHELF LIFE]
    C3 --> OUT7[POWDERED SPICES FLOUR]
    C4 --> OUT8[ICE FOR FISH PRESERVATION]
```

## 4.5 Solar Dryer Schematic (Cabinet Type) for Food Processing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AIRA[AMBIENT AIR INLET] --> FANB[DC FAN DRIVEN BY PV]
    FANB --> COLL[SOLAR AIR HEATER COLLECTOR]
    COLL --> HOTA[HOT AIR 50 TO 80 DEG C]
    HOTA --> DRYCH[DRYING CHAMBER WITH TRAY RACKS]
    DRYCH --> FDP[FOOD PRODUCT SPREAD ON MESH TRAYS]
    FDP --> MOIST[MOISTURE EVAPORATION]
    MOIST --> EXH[EXHAUST MOIST AIR VIA CHIMNEY]
    EXH --> AMBOUT[AMBIENT OUTLET]
    FDP -.-> DRYPD[DEHYDRATED PRODUCT]
    COLL -.-> BYP[BYPASS DAMPER FOR TEMPERATURE CONTROL]
    BYP -.-> HOTA
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. Define the *solar constant*. Mention its standard value and explain why it is not truly "constant" in practice. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
The **solar constant** is the rate of solar energy received per unit area on a surface perpendicular to the Sun's rays at the mean Earth–Sun distance, **outside the Earth's atmosphere**, under standard conditions.
**Standard value: I_sc = 1367 W/m²** (also commonly cited as ~1361 W/m² per WMO 1981).
It is not truly constant because:
- The Earth's orbit is **elliptical** (eccentricity 0.0167), so the actual extraterrestrial irradiance varies by ±3.3% throughout the year (Earth-Sun distance varies between 1.471 × 10⁸ km and 1.521 × 10⁸ km).
- **Solar cycles** (11-year sunspot cycle) modulate solar output by ±0.1%.

### Question 2. Differentiate between a *flat plate collector* and a *concentrating solar collector*. State any three differences. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Flat Plate Collector (FPC) | Concentrating Collector (CSC) |
|---|---|---|
| Concentration ratio | 1× (no concentration) | 10× to 3000× |
| Solar radiation used | Direct + diffuse (works in cloudy weather) | Direct beam only (needs DNI) |
| Operating temperature | 30–100 °C | 150–1500 °C |
| Tracking required | Not required (fixed tilt) | 1-axis or 2-axis tracking mandatory |
| Cost | Low | High (precision optics + tracking) |
| Typical food use | Water heating, low-temp drying | Steam generation, sterilization |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [14 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **flat plate solar collector**. Define the terms *absorptivity*, *transmittance*, and *emissivity* in the context of selective surfaces. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (refer to Section 4.2 diagram):**
A flat plate collector consists of:
1. **Transparent cover(s)** – 1 to 3 sheets of low-iron glass or polymer (tedlar), thickness 3–4 mm. Reduces convective loss by trapping a stagnant air layer; transmits ~90% of incoming shortwave radiation.
2. **Absorber plate** – copper or aluminum sheet coated with a **selective surface** (e.g., black chrome, nickel–pigmented alumina, or cermet). Typical thickness 0.2–1 mm. Carries fluid tubes/risers on the underside.
3. **Fluid tubes/risers** – copper tubes bonded to the absorber, headers at top and bottom; working fluid is water, glycol mixture, or air.
4. **Insulation** – 50–100 mm mineral wool or polyurethane foam at the back and sides; thermal conductivity < 0.04 W/m·K.
5. **Casing** – galvanized iron or aluminum enclosure; weather-sealed.

**Working Principle:**
- Shortwave solar radiation passes through the transparent cover with transmittance τ.
- The selective absorber plate absorbs a fraction α of this radiation, heating up.
- The heated plate transfers heat to the working fluid via conduction (through the riser bond) and convection (fluid flow).
- Longwave (IR) emission from the plate is suppressed by the **selective surface** (low ε in the IR band).
- Useful heat gain: $Q_u = A_c F_R \left[ S - U_L(T_{fi} - T_a) \right]$, where $S = G_T \tau\alpha$.

**Selective Surface Definitions:**
- **Absorptivity (α):** fraction of incident solar radiation (0.3–2.5 μm) absorbed by the surface. For good selective coatings, α ≥ 0.95.
- **Transmittance (τ):** fraction of incident solar radiation that passes through the transparent cover. For low-iron glass, τ ≈ 0.91–0.95.
- **Emissivity (ε):** fraction of longwave thermal radiation (5–50 μm) emitted by the surface at a given temperature. For good selective coatings, ε ≤ 0.10–0.15 at 100 °C.
- A good **spectral selective surface** has **high α / low ε** ratio (e.g., α = 0.95, ε = 0.10 → ratio 9.5).

**(b)** Explain the **Hottel–Whillier equation** for a flat plate solar collector. Derive the expression for instantaneous thermal efficiency and discuss the effect of *fluid inlet temperature* on collector efficiency. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Energy balance on the absorber plate (steady state):**
$$A_c \cdot S = Q_u + A_c \cdot U_L (T_{pm} - T_a)$$

where $T_{pm}$ = mean absorber plate temperature.

**Step 2: Define Heat Removal Factor F_R:**
$$F_R = \frac{\dot{m}c_p(T_{fo} - T_{fi})}{A_c [S - U_L(T_{fi} - T_a)]} = \frac{\dot{m}c_p}{A_c U_L}\left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{A_c U_L F'}{\dot{m}c_p}\right)\right]$$

where F' is the **collector efficiency factor** (a function of plate-to-fluid heat transfer coefficient and fin efficiency).

**Step 3: Rearrange to Hottel–Whillier equation:**
$$Q_u = A_c F_R \left[ S - U_L(T_{fi} - T_a) \right]$$

**Step 4: Divide by (A_c · G_T) to get instantaneous efficiency:**
$$\eta_c = \frac{Q_u}{A_c G_T} = F_R \left[ \tau\alpha - \frac{U_L(T_{fi} - T_a)}{G_T} \right]$$

**Step 5: Interpretation – Effect of Fluid Inlet Temperature:**
- The plot of $\eta_c$ vs. $\dfrac{(T_{fi} - T_a)}{G_T}$ is a **straight line** with:
  - **Intercept** = $F_R (\tau\alpha)$ on the y-axis.
  - **Slope** = $-F_R U_L$ (negative).
- Higher $T_{fi}$ → higher x-axis value → **lower efficiency**.
- Therefore, **collectors perform best when fluid inlet is cold** (e.g., early morning or winter operation).
- For food processing applications like pre-heating wash water, this means **low feed-water temperature maximizes thermal gain**.

**Standard test conditions (ASHRAE 93):** $\eta_c$ is measured with $G_T = 800 \pm 50$ W/m², ambient temperature 20–30 °C, and zero wind.

---

### Question B [14 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **principle of photovoltaic energy conversion** in a silicon solar cell. With a labeled diagram, describe the I–V characteristics and define *short-circuit current, open-circuit voltage, maximum power point, and fill factor*. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:**
A crystalline silicon solar cell is a **pn-junction diode** with a thin p-type layer (~0.2–0.5 μm) on top of an n-type wafer (~200–300 μm). When photons of energy **hν ≥ E_g** (E_g of Si = 1.12 eV) are absorbed:
- Each photon generates an **electron–hole pair** in the depletion region.
- The **built-in electric field** (created by ionized dopants at the junction) separates the charges — electrons to the n-side, holes to the p-side.
- Connecting an external load allows the charges to flow, producing a **photocurrent I_ph** proportional to light intensity.

**Single-diode equivalent circuit:** $I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{q(V + IR_s)}{nkT}\right) - 1\right] - \dfrac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}$

**I–V Characteristics (refer to standard plot):**
- **Short-circuit current (I_sc):** current when V = 0; directly proportional to light intensity G_T.
- **Open-circuit voltage (V_oc):** voltage when I = 0; depends logarithmically on G_T and decreases with cell temperature.
  $$V_{oc} = \frac{nkT}{q}\ln\!\left(\frac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1\right)$$
- **Maximum Power Point (MPP):** point (V_mp, I_mp) on the I–V curve where the product P = V·I is maximum.
- **Fill Factor (FF):** $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} I_{mp}}{V_{oc} I_{sc}}$; for good cells, FF = 0.70–0.85.
- **Conversion efficiency:** $\eta_{PV} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{G_T \cdot A} = \dfrac{V_{oc} I_{sc} FF}{G_T \cdot A}$.

**Effect of Temperature and Irradiance:**
- As T rises by 1 °C → V_oc drops by ~2 mV, I_sc rises slightly → **net efficiency falls by 0.4–0.5%/°C**.
- As G_T increases → I_sc increases linearly, V_oc rises logarithmically → **η rises logarithmically**.

**(b)** Discuss the various **solar thermal and photovoltaic applications in food processing**. Give at least two examples of each with quantitative benefits over conventional systems. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

### A. Solar Thermal Applications in Food Processing

**1. Solar Crop Dryers (Cabinet, Tunnel, and Greenhouse Types)**
- **Principle:** Heated air from a solar air heater passes through a drying chamber, evaporating moisture from food products (grains, spices, fruits, fish, coconut).
- **Quantitative benefits:**
  - **Drying temperature** maintained at **50–80 °C** (vs. open-sun drying at ambient 30–35 °C).
  - **Drying time reduced** by 30–60% compared to open sun drying.
  - **Product quality improvement:** uniform drying, lower contamination (dust, birds, insects), better color retention.
  - For **chillies**: moisture content reduced from 75% to 10% (wb) in 12–15 hours vs. 25–30 hours in open sun.
  - For **fish (in coastal Kerala)**: shelf life extended from 1 day to 2–3 weeks.
  - **Payback period:** 1.5–3 years for a 100 m² solar tunnel dryer.

**2. Solar Water Heating for Food Process Plants**
- **Principle:** Flat plate or evacuated tube collectors pre-heat water used for cleaning, blanching, and CIP (clean-in-place) operations.
- **Quantitative benefits:**
  - Water heated from 25 °C to 60–80 °C **without electricity or LPG**.
  - For a small food processing unit needing **500 LPD of 60 °C water**, an FPC system of ~4 m² saves ~3000 kWh/year (≈ ₹30,000/year electricity cost).

**3. Solar Pasteurization of Milk and Fruit Juices**
- Concentrating collectors (parabolic trough) heat milk/juice to **72 °C for 15 s** (HTST pasteurization) without fossil fuel.

**4. Solar Steam Generation for Cooking/Sterilization**
- Used in **dairy, spice processing, and ready-to-eat food** plants.

### B. Solar Photovoltaic Applications in Food Processing

**1. Solar PV Water Pumping for Irrigation/Processing**
- **Principle:** PV array directly drives a DC or AC submersible pump via MPPT controller.
- **Quantitative benefits:**
  - For a 1 HP (0.75 kW) pump, a 1.2 kWp PV array can lift ~50,000 L/day from 7 m depth.
  - Replaces diesel pumps; **saves ~1500 liters of diesel per year** for a typical farm.
  - **Diesel pump unit cost:** ₹40/hour; **Solar PV pump:** ₹0/hour (after capital cost recovery in ~3 years).

**2. Solar PV Refrigeration and Cold Storage**
- **Principle:** PV array powers a DC vapor-compression refrigeration unit or a solar-thermal adsorption chiller.
- **Quantitative benefits:**
  - **Milk cooling:** chilling from 35 °C to 4 °C within 2 hours after milking; preserves freshness.
  - **Cold rooms (5–10 °C):** capacity 5–20 tonnes; used for fruits, vegetables, fish, and dairy. Reduces post-harvest losses (currently 15–40% in India).

**3. Solar PV Milling/Grinding**
- Powers small AC mills for spices, pulses, and grains; ideal for rural processing centers.

**4. Solar PV Ice-Making Machines**
- Critical for **fisheries** (especially in Kerala's coastal areas); produces 50–500 kg of ice/day.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Students often write the **angle of incidence** formula for a *horizontal* surface (cos θ_z) when the question asks for a *tilted* surface. KTU examiners specifically test the **tilted form**: $\cos\theta_i = \sin\delta \sin(\phi - \beta) + \cos\delta \cos(\phi - \beta)\cos\omega$.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** In Hottel–Whillier derivations, students confuse the **plate mean temperature T_pm** with the **fluid inlet temperature T_fi**. The final usable form of the equation is in terms of T_fi (the controllable variable).
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** For PV I–V characteristics, students forget to state **STC** (G = 1000 W/m², T_cell = 25 °C, AM 1.5). V_oc, I_sc, and FF are reported **only at STC**.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** In numerical problems, students forget to convert angles from **degrees to radians** when using the sine/cosine functions if they're working in calculator radian mode.
> 5. **Common Mistake 5:** When asked for *applications in food processing*, students write generic answers like "for drying." KTU expects **specific food items** (e.g., chillies, turmeric, cardamom, fish, coconut, rice, milk) and **quantitative benefits** (temperature, moisture content, payback period).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 – Solar Energy – Last-Minute Revision Bullets**

- **Solar constant = 1367 W/m²** (or 1361 W/m², both acceptable).
- **AM 1.5 spectrum** is the standard for PV rating at **1000 W/m²**.
- **Declination formula (Cooper):** $\delta = 23.45° \sin\!\left[\dfrac{360(284 + n)}{365}\right]$.
- **Hour angle:** $\omega = 15°(t_{solar} - 12)$ — **+ve after noon, −ve before noon**.
- **Angle of incidence on tilted south-facing surface:** $\cos\theta_i = \sin\delta \sin(\phi - \beta) + \cos\delta \cos(\phi - \beta)\cos\omega$.
- **Hottel–Whillier equation:** $Q_u = A_c F_R [S - U_L(T_{fi} - T_a)]$.
- **Collector efficiency** is **linear in (T_fi − T_a)/G_T** with negative slope $-F_R U_L$ and intercept $F_R (\tau\alpha)$.
- **Selective surface goal:** maximize $\alpha$ (solar band), minimize $\varepsilon$ (thermal IR band).
- **PV I–V equation:** $I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{q(V + IR_s)}{nkT}\right) - 1\right] - \dfrac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}$.
- **Fill Factor (FF)** = $V_{mp} I_{mp} / (V_{oc} I_{sc})$; **typical range 0.70–0.85**.
- **Temperature coefficient of P_max:** ~−0.4 to −0.5 %/°C for c-Si modules.
- **Concentration ratio:** FPC = 1×, ETC = 1×, PTC = 15–80×, Dish = 500–3000×.
- **Maximum operating temperature (FPC):** ~100 °C; (PTC): 400 °C; (Dish): 1500 °C.
- **Food processing applications of solar thermal:** drying, water heating, pasteurization, cooking, steam generation.
- **Food processing applications of solar PV:** water pumping, refrigeration, milk cooling, milling, ice-making, cold storage.
- **Solar dryer advantages over open-sun drying:** 30–60% faster drying, uniform quality, hygienic, lower contamination, higher market price.
- **Kerala-specific applications:** solar dryers for **cardamom, pepper, turmeric, coconut, and fish**; PV pumps for **coconut & paddy fields**; PV milk chillers for **dairy cooperatives**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
