Incentives *soften* recurring tax burden; subsidies *hard-reduce* the initial cheque size for the food entrepreneur.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks**. Two completely independent alternative choices are given below; the student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### PART B — QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, restructured for PEFTT866]**

A medium-scale spice processing unit in Kerala proposes to install a **biogas-based steam boiler** (from food waste) to replace its existing firewood boiler. Estimate the economic feasibility of the project.

**Given Data:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Capital cost of biogas plant + boiler modification (C₀) | ₹ 8,00,000 |
| Annual firewood displaced | 18 tonnes |
| Firewood price | ₹ 6,500 per tonne |
| Annual labour saved | ₹ 25,000 |
| Annual O&M cost | ₹ 40,000 |
| Project life (n) | 15 years |
| Discount rate (r) | 10% |
| MNRE subsidy (s) | 25% of capital cost |

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Compute the Simple Payback Period (SPP) and the annual Simple ROI, both with and without subsidy. Comment on the impact of the subsidy.**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the gross annual savings (before O&M deduction):

$$
A_{\text{gross}} = (\text{Firewood displaced} \times P_f) + \text{Labour saved} = (18 \times 6500) + 25000
$$

$$
A_{\text{gross}} = 117000 + 25000 = 142000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the *net* annual savings after O&M:

$$
A_s = A_{\text{gross}} - \text{O\&M} = 142000 - 40000 = 102000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 3:** Without subsidy (s = 0), $C_0 = 8,00,000$:

$$
\text{SPP}_{\text{no sub}} = \dfrac{800000}{102000} = 7.84 \text{ years}
$$

$$
\text{ROI}_{\text{no sub}} = \dfrac{102000}{800000} \times 100 = 12.75\% \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 4:** With subsidy s = 0.25, effective capital cost:

$$
C_{0,\text{eff}} = 800000 \times (1 - 0.25) = 600000 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
\text{SPP}_{\text{with sub}} = \dfrac{600000}{102000} = 5.88 \text{ years}
$$

$$
\text{ROI}_{\text{with sub}} = \dfrac{102000}{600000} \times 100 = 17.00\% \text{ per year}
$$

**Comment (1 mark):** The MNRE subsidy shortens the payback from 7.84 → 5.88 years (a 25% reduction) and raises the ROI from 12.75% → 17.00% per year. Without the subsidy the project is borderline (SPP > 5 years); with the subsidy it is clearly attractive to a banker evaluating the loan proposal.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Compute the Net Present Value (NPV) and Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR) with subsidy, using r = 10%, n = 15 years. State the economic verdict.**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Recall PVAF for r = 10%, n = 15 (from actuarial table or as computed in Section 3.4):

$$
\text{PVAF} = 7.606
$$

**Step 2:** Present Value of total savings over 15 years:

$$
\text{PV}_{\text{savings}} = A_s \times \text{PVAF} = 102000 \times 7.606 = 775812 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 3:** Effective capital cost (with subsidy):

$$
C_{0,\text{eff}} = 600000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 4:** Net Present Value:

$$
\text{NPV} = \text{PV}_{\text{savings}} - C_{0,\text{eff}} = 775812 - 600000 = 175812 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 5:** Benefit-Cost Ratio:

$$
\text{BCR} = \dfrac{775812}{600000} = 1.293
$$

**Step 6:** Economic Verdict:

- NPV = +₹ 1,75,812 > 0 ✅
- BCR = 1.293 > 1 ✅
- SPP = 5.88 years (acceptable for biomass projects) ✅
- ROI = 17% per year > 10% hurdle rate ✅

**The biogas steam boiler project is economically feasible and recommended for implementation.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Many students forget to subtract the O&M cost from gross savings before computing A_s. Marks are lost *linearly* for this omission. Always write "**Net Annual Savings = Gross Savings − O&M**" on the answer sheet.
>
> **Pitfall #2:** Several candidates compute payback using the *undiscounted* method but then state it as if it were the IRR. They are different — SPP is in years, IRR is in % per year. Examiners dock **1 mark** for this confusion.

---

### PART B — QUESTION B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, restructured for PEFTT866]**

A seafood export unit in Kerala plans to install a **solar PV–powered walk-in cold room (10 TR capacity)** to store shrimp before export. Analyse the economic feasibility of the system.

**Given Data:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Capital cost of solar cold room (C₀) | ₹ 15,00,000 |
| Annual grid electricity displaced | 22,000 kWh |
| Grid tariff | ₹ 7.50 per kWh |
| Annual O&M cost | ₹ 35,000 |
| Diesel backup displaced | 600 L per year |
| Diesel price | ₹ 95 per L |
| Project life (n) | 20 years |
| Discount rate (r) | 9% |
| State subsidy (ANERT) | 20% of capital cost |

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Compute the annual net savings (A_s), the Simple Payback Period (SPP) and the annual ROI, both with and without subsidy.**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute annual savings from displaced grid electricity:

$$
S_{\text{grid}} = 22000 \times 7.50 = 165000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute annual savings from displaced diesel:

$$
S_{\text{diesel}} = 600 \times 95 = 57000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 3:** Gross annual savings:

$$
A_{\text{gross}} = 165000 + 57000 = 222000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 4:** Net annual savings (after O&M):

$$
A_s = 222000 - 35000 = 187000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 5:** Effective capital cost after 20% subsidy:

$$
C_{0,\text{eff}} = 1500000 \times 0.80 = 1200000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 6:** Simple Payback Period:

- Without subsidy:

$$
\text{SPP}_{\text{no sub}} = \dfrac{1500000}{187000} = 8.02 \text{ years}
$$

- With subsidy:

$$
\text{SPP}_{\text{with sub}} = \dfrac{1200000}{187000} = 6.42 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 7:** Annual Simple ROI:

- Without subsidy:

$$
\text{ROI}_{\text{no sub}} = \dfrac{187000}{1500000} \times 100 = 12.47\%
$$

- With subsidy:

$$
\text{ROI}_{\text{with sub}} = \dfrac{187000}{1200000} \times 100 = 15.58\%
$$

**Comment:** The ANERT subsidy reduces payback by 1.6 years and raises ROI by ~3.1 percentage points. Both with- and without-subsidy cases cross the 10% hurdle rate, indicating the project is fundamentally robust — subsidies merely accelerate the financial attraction.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Compute the NPV and BCR with subsidy at r = 9%, n = 20 years. State the economic verdict and discuss the impact of the subsidy scheme.**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute PVAF for r = 9%, n = 20:

$$
(1.09)^{20} \approx 5.604
$$

$$
(1.09)^{-20} = \dfrac{1}{5.604} \approx 0.1784
$$

$$
\text{PVAF} = \dfrac{1 - 0.1784}{0.09} = \dfrac{0.8216}{0.09} = 9.129
$$

**Step 2:** Present Value of all net savings:

$$
\text{PV}_{\text{savings}} = 187000 \times 9.129 = 1707123 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 3:** Effective capital cost with subsidy:

$$
C_{0,\text{eff}} = 1200000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 4:** Net Present Value:

$$
\text{NPV} = 1707123 - 1200000 = 507123 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 5:** Benefit-Cost Ratio:

$$
\text{BCR} = \dfrac{1707123}{1200000} = 1.423
$$

**Step 6:** Economic Verdict:

- NPV = +₹ 5,07,123 > 0 ✅
- BCR = 1.423 > 1 ✅
- SPP = 6.42 years (acceptable for 20-year-life cold storage assets) ✅
- ROI = 15.58% per year > 9% hurdle rate ✅

**The solar cold room is economically feasible and recommended.**

**Step 7 (Bonus Discussion – 1 mark):** The BCR improvement with subsidy vs without:

- Without subsidy: BCR = 1,707,123 / 1,500,000 = 1.138
- With subsidy: BCR = 1.423

The ANERT subsidy improves BCR by **25%**, taking the project from "marginally bankable" to "comfortably bankable". Combined with KSEB's net-metering policy, the economics of solar cold storage for seafood export are particularly strong in Kerala.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #3:** When computing PVAF, students often write $(1-r)^{-n}$ instead of $(1+r)^{-n}$. This is a **critical sign error** that makes the entire NPV calculation wrong. The discount rate is *always positive* in the denominator.
>
> **Pitfall #4:** Students frequently forget to *include diesel savings* in the annual net savings for hybrid systems. In a solar + diesel-backup cold room, both grid *and* diesel savings must be added before subtracting O&M. Marks lost: up to **2 marks per sub-part**.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Economic Feasibility Module**

- ✅ **Cost-Benefit Analysis (CBA)** = Quantify all costs and benefits in ₹, discount to present value, compute NPV/BCR/IRR/SPP.
- ✅ **Time Value of Money** is *non-negotiable* in KTU problems — every future cash flow must be discounted by $(1+r)^t$.
- ✅ **NPV ≥ 0**, **BCR ≥ 1**, **IRR ≥ hurdle rate**, **SPP < 5 years** → project is accepted.
- ✅ **Simple Payback Period** $\text{SPP} = C_0 / A_s$ (undiscounted, quick filter).
- ✅ **Discounted Payback** uses cumulative discounted savings — preferred for long-life assets.
- ✅ **ROI (annual)** $= (A_s / C_0) \times 100$; **True ROI** = IRR (discount rate at which NPV = 0).
- ✅ **Post-subsidy capital cost** $C_{0,\text{eff}} = C_0 (1-s)$. Always adjust $C_0$ *before* computing ROI or payback.
- ✅ **MNRE** = Central nodal agency for solar/biomass/wind subsidies in food processing.
- ✅ **ANERT** = Kerala state agency for rooftop solar, biogas, and energy efficiency incentives.
- ✅ **NABARD** = Soft loans at 4–5% for food processing RE projects.
- ✅ **80% Accelerated Depreciation** under Sec. 32 of IT Act is a major tax *incentive* (not a subsidy).
- ✅ **KSEB Net Metering** allows export of surplus solar electricity — a *feed-in-tariff* incentive.
- ✅ **NCEF / Carbon Credit Markets** can monetise avoided CO₂ emissions.
- ✅ **LCOE** (Levelised Cost of Energy) is the ₹/kWh benchmark for comparing RE technologies to grid tariff.
- ✅ **Energy Payback (EPB)** is the time a renewable system takes to generate the energy used in its manufacture — a sustainability metric, not financial.
- ✅ **Subsidies reduce denominator** of ROI and **shorten payback**; they do *not* change the operating savings.
- ✅ **Always state agency, scheme name, and percentage** in KTU answers for full credit.

**Mnemonic for the four CBA metrics:** **"N-I-B-S"** — *NPV, IRR, BCR, SPP*. Memorise in this order to recall the decision rule sequence: NPV first (₹ sign), IRR second (% sign), BCR third (ratio), SPP fourth (years).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar Thermal Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT866 - APPLICATIONS OF RENEWABLE ENERGY IN FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Solar Thermal Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Solar Thermal System** is an engineering assembly that intercepts incoming solar radiation, converts it into useful thermal energy (heat), and transfers this heat to a working fluid (air, water, or another heat-transfer medium) for direct use, storage, or further processing. Unlike photovoltaic systems which convert sunlight directly into electricity, solar thermal systems exploit the **short-wavelength electromagnetic energy** of the sun to raise the temperature of a fluid for applications such as water heating, space heating, drying, cooking, and distillation.

In the context of **Food Processing Engineering** (KTU PEFTT866), solar thermal systems are categorized as direct, indirect, or hybrid systems designed specifically for unit operations such as drying, pasteurization, blanching, evaporation, and sterilization of agricultural produce.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Solar thermal systems form the backbone of decentralized, off-grid food processing in rural India. The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes the design logic, performance equations, and food-processing applications of flat plate collectors, concentrating collectors, solar dryers, solar cookers, and solar stills.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Greenhouse in a Pipe" Intuition

Imagine standing inside a glasshouse on a winter morning. Even though the air outside is cold, the air inside feels warm and pleasant. This happens because **short-wave solar radiation passes freely through the transparent glass**, but the **long-wave infrared radiation re-emitted by the heated interior surfaces cannot escape back out** — a phenomenon called the **Greenhouse Effect**.

A **flat plate solar collector** works on exactly the same principle:

- A **transparent glass cover** acts like the greenhouse glass — it lets sunlight in but traps the heat.
- A **blackened absorber plate** acts like the dark soil of the greenhouse — it absorbs almost all the sunlight that hits it.
- A **working fluid (water or air)** flowing through pipes attached to the absorber plate acts like a sponge — it soaks up the heat and carries it away for use.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Mapping:** Think of a solar water heater on a rooftop. The black tube you see inside the glass box is the "absorber sponge," the glass is the "heat trap," and the water flowing inside the tube is the "heat messenger" being delivered to your bathroom geyser.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics

The following **standard solar constants** are universally adopted in KTU calculations:

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Numerical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Solar Constant (extraterrestrial) | $G_{sc}$ | **1367** | W/m² |
| Extraterrestrial radiation on day $n$ | $G_{on}$ | $G_{sc}\left(1 + 0.033 \cos\frac{360 n}{365}\right)$ | W/m² |
| Declination angle (Cooper's equation) | $\delta$ | $23.45 \sin\left(\frac{360(284+n)}{365}\right)$ | degrees |
| Hour angle | $\omega$ | $15(t_{solar} - 12)$ | degrees |
| Air mass (simple model) | $AM$ | $1/\sin(\alpha)$ | — |
| Solar azimuth & altitude | $\gamma_s, \alpha$ | Function of $(\phi, \delta, \omega)$ | degrees |
| Stefan–Boltzmann constant | $\sigma$ | **5.67 × 10⁻⁸** | W/m²·K⁴ |
| Solar absorptivity of black paint | $\alpha_s$ | **0.93 – 0.97** | — |
| Emissivity of black paint (IR) | $\varepsilon$ | **0.88 – 0.95** | — |
| Transmissivity of clear glass | $\tau$ | **0.85 – 0.91** | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** Memorize $G_{sc} = 1367$ W/m² and the Cooper's declination formula. They appear in nearly every solar geometry numerical in the KTU University Examination.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The "Why" and the "How"

A solar thermal system operates through a controlled, sequential chain of energy conversions:

1. **Solar Radiative Capture (τ):** Incident solar beam $I_b$, diffuse sky radiation $I_d$, and ground-reflected radiation $I_r$ pass through the **transparent cover** with a transmissivity $\tau$ (typically 0.85–0.91 for low-iron glass).
2. **Absorption (α):** The **absorber plate**, coated with a selective black surface (e.g., black chrome, nickel-black, or cermet), absorbs $\alpha_s$ (0.93–0.97) of the transmitted energy.
3. **Conduction:** Heat conducts through the absorber plate thickness $t_p$ and through the bond/contact to the riser tubes or fins.
4. **Convective Transfer to Fluid:** The working fluid picks up heat via forced or natural convection in the tubes.
5. **Losses (Top, Bottom, Edge):** Simultaneously, the absorber loses heat by **convection and re-radiation** from its top surface, conduction through the bottom insulation, and edge losses.
6. **Delivery & Storage:** The heated fluid is delivered to a load (e.g., dryer, pasteurizer) or stored in a thermal reservoir (water tank, PCM, pebble bed).

> [!NOTE]
> **The Net Useful Energy** is the absorbed energy minus all these losses. This is exactly what the **Hottel-Whillier-Bliss (HWB) equation** quantifies — the central formula of solar thermal engineering.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Units | Key Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Extraterrestrial radiation on day $n$ | $G_{on} = G_{sc}\left(1 + 0.033 \cos\frac{360n}{365}\right)$ | W/m² | Day 1 = Jan 1 |
| 2 | Declination angle (Cooper) | $\delta = 23.45 \sin\left(\frac{360(284+n)}{365}\right)$ | ° | Range ±23.45° |
| 3 | Hour angle | $\omega = 15(t_{s} - 12)$ | ° | $t_s$ in solar hours |
| 4 | Solar altitude | $\sin \alpha = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$ | — | $\phi$ = latitude |
| 5 | Cosine of incidence (tilted surface) | $\cos\theta = \sin\delta \sin(\phi-\beta) + \cos\delta \cos(\phi-\beta)\cos\omega$ | — | $\beta$ = tilt angle |
| 6 | Hourly extraterrestrial on tilted | $I_o = \frac{12 \times 3600}{\pi}\, G_{on} (\cos\theta)$ | J/m² |  |
| 7 | **Hottel-Whillier-Bliss Equation** | $Q_u = A_c F_R \left[ S - U_L (T_{fi} - T_a) \right]$ | W | **MOST IMPORTANT** |
| 8 | Collector Heat Removal Factor | $F_R = \frac{\dot{m} C_p}{A_c U_L}\left[1 - \exp\left(-\frac{A_c U_L F'}{\dot{m} C_p}\right)\right]$ | — | 0 < F_R < 1 |
| 9 | Collector Efficiency Factor | $F' = \frac{1/U_L}{W \left[\frac{1}{U_L} + \frac{D_i}{h_{fi} D_o} + \frac{D_i \ln(D_o/D_i)}{2 k_{tube}}\right]}$ | — | Fin efficiency built-in |
| 10 | Top loss coefficient (Klein) | $U_t = \left[\frac{N}{\frac{C}{T_p}\left[\frac{T_p - T_a}{N + f}\right]^{e}} + \frac{1}{h_w}\right]^{-1} + \frac{\sigma(T_p^2 + T_a^2)(T_p + T_a)}{\varepsilon_p + 0.00591 N h_w}$ | W/m²K | Most cited |
| 11 | Useful energy per day | $Q_d = A_c \int_{t_1}^{t_2} Q_u(t)\, dt$ | J/day | Integrate over sunshine hours |
| 12 | Drying Efficiency | $\eta_d = \frac{m_w \lambda_w}{A_c \int I(t)\, dt}$ | — | $m_w$ = water evaporated |
| 13 | Pickup Efficiency | $\eta_p = \frac{\dot{m} C_p (T_{fo} - T_{fi})}{A_c I}$ | — | Air-based systems |
| 14 | Overall dryer efficiency | $\eta_{od} = \eta_c \times \eta_p$ | — | Product of both |
| 15 | Solar still productivity | $\dot{m}_{still} = \frac{Q_u}{\lambda_{fg}}$ | kg/s | $\lambda_{fg} \approx 2400$ kJ/kg |
| 16 | PCM Storage energy | $Q_{st} = m_{pcm} \left[ c_{p,s} \Delta T_s + L + c_{p,l} \Delta T_l \right]$ | J | Sensible + latent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern Alert:** Almost every KTU numerical on solar thermal systems features the HWB equation (row 7) and the Klein top-loss expression (row 10). Master them thoroughly.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Solar thermal systems are the **workhorses of low-carbon food processing** in tropical countries like India:

- **Rural Food Drying:** Direct and indirect solar dryers reduce post-harvest losses (currently 5–16 % in India) for perishable commodities — mango, turmeric, chili, fish, and herbs.
- **Solar Pasteurization:** Used for milk, fruit juices, and water with retention of nutrients and minimal energy input.
- **Solar Cooking & Sterilization:** Community solar cookers (e.g., Scheffler dishes) deliver 1.5–3 kW thermal power for institutional kitchens.
- **Solar Distillation:** Yields potable water from brackish/saline sources and concentrates heat-sensitive liquid foods.
- **Process Steam Generation:** Concentrating collectors (parabolic trough, Scheffler) can generate 150–300 °C steam for sterilization, evaporation, and concentration in dairy and fruit processing.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hottel-Whillier-Bliss (HWB) Useful Energy Equation

The HWB equation is the **single most important equation** in solar thermal engineering. It is derived by performing an energy balance on the absorber plate and applying the **collector heat removal factor** concept of Hottel and Whillier, later refined by Bliss.

### Step 1: Define the absorber plate energy balance

The steady-state useful heat gain $Q_u$ absorbed by the working fluid is equal to the **absorbed solar radiation** minus **all thermal losses** from the collector:

$$
Q_u = A_c \alpha_s \tau I - Q_L
$$

where:
- $A_c$ = collector aperture area (m²)
- $\alpha_s$ = absorptivity of absorber surface
- $\tau$ = transmittance of the cover
- $I$ = total incident solar radiation on tilted surface (W/m²)
- $Q_L$ = total rate of heat loss (W)

### Step 2: Linearize the loss term using the overall loss coefficient

The losses are not constant; they vary with plate temperature $T_p$. However, for small temperature differences, the loss can be **linearized** about the inlet fluid temperature $T_{fi}$:

$$
Q_L = A_c\, U_L\, (T_{fi} - T_a)
$$

where $U_L$ is the overall heat loss coefficient (W/m²·K) and $T_a$ is the ambient temperature.

### Step 3: Substitute the linearized loss term

$$
Q_u = A_c \left[ \alpha_s \tau I - U_L (T_{fi} - T_a) \right]
$$

### Step 4: Define the absorbed radiation per unit area

The absorbed radiation per unit collector area is $S = \alpha_s \tau I$ (W/m²):

$$
Q_u = A_c \left[ S - U_L (T_{fi} - T_a) \right]
$$

### Step 5: Introduce the heat removal factor $F_R$

In a real collector, the fluid is not uniformly at $T_{fi}$ — it heats up as it flows, so the **average plate-to-fluid temperature is smaller** than $(T_{fi} - T_a)$. Hottel–Whillier introduced $F_R$ to account for this:

$$
Q_u = A_c F_R \left[ S - U_L (T_{fi} - T_a) \right]
$$

> **This is the celebrated HWB equation**, also written in terms of the mean fluid temperature $T_{fm}$ using $F'$ instead of $F_R$.

### Step 6: Derive the collector efficiency

Dividing $Q_u$ by $A_c I$ gives the instantaneous thermal efficiency:

$$
\eta_c = F_R \left[ \alpha_s \tau - \frac{U_L (T_{fi} - T_a)}{I} \right]
$$

This is a **linear function** of $\left(\frac{T_{fi} - T_a}{I}\right)$, plotted as the classic efficiency curve. The intercept is $F_R \alpha_s \tau$ and the slope is $-F_R U_L$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A flat plate solar collector has an aperture area of $2.0$ m². During a test, the incident radiation is $I = 800$ W/m², ambient temperature $T_a = 30$ °C, inlet fluid temperature $T_{fi} = 50$ °C, $\alpha_s \tau = 0.85$, and $U_L = 5.5$ W/m²K, with $F_R = 0.92$. Calculate: (a) Useful heat gain, (b) Instantaneous efficiency.

### Solution:

**(a) Useful heat gain:**

$$
Q_u = A_c F_R \left[ \alpha_s \tau I - U_L (T_{fi} - T_a) \right]
$$

$$
Q_u = 2.0 \times 0.92 \times \left[ 0.85 \times 800 - 5.5 \times (50 - 30) \right]
$$

$$
Q_u = 1.84 \times \left[ 680 - 110 \right] = 1.84 \times 570
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_u = 1048.8 \text{ W} \approx 1.049 \text{ kW}}
$$

**(b) Instantaneous efficiency:**

$$
\eta_c = \frac{Q_u}{A_c I} = \frac{1048.8}{2.0 \times 800} = \frac{1048.8}{1600}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_c = 0.6555 = 65.55\%}
$$

**Verification:** Use the linear form:

$$
\eta_c = F_R \left[ \alpha_s \tau - \frac{U_L (T_{fi} - T_a)}{I} \right] = 0.92 \left[ 0.85 - \frac{5.5 \times 20}{800} \right]
$$

$$
\eta_c = 0.92 \left[ 0.85 - 0.1375 \right] = 0.92 \times 0.7125 = 0.6555
$$

The two results match. ✓

## 3.3 Derivation: Daily Useful Energy and Mean Efficiency

The daily useful energy is the time-integral over the sunshine period $H$ (in seconds or hours):

$$
Q_d = A_c \int_{sunrise}^{sunset} F_R \left[ S(t) - U_L (T_{fi}(t) - T_a(t)) \right] dt
$$

A common simplified engineering form uses **daily mean values** (denoted by overbars):

$$
Q_d = A_c F_R \left[ \bar{S} H_t - U_L (\bar{T}_{fi} - \bar{T}_a) H_t \right]
$$

Dividing by the daily incident energy $A_c H_t \bar{I}_T$ gives the mean daily efficiency:

$$
\bar{\eta}_d = F_R \left[ \alpha_s \tau - \frac{U_L (\bar{T}_{fi} - \bar{T}_a)}{\bar{I}_T} \right]
$$

## 3.4 Solar Drying — Moisture Removal Rate

For a solar dryer processing food with initial moisture $M_i$ (kg water/kg dry solid) to final moisture $M_f$, the moisture to be evaporated is:

$$
m_w = m_{product} \times \left( \frac{M_i - M_f}{1 - M_f} \right)
$$

The required thermal energy (latent heat of vaporization $\lambda_w \approx 2400 - 2500$ kJ/kg at 60–90 °C):

$$
Q_{req} = m_w \lambda_w
$$

The **drying efficiency** of the solar dryer is:

$$
\eta_d = \frac{m_w \lambda_w}{A_c \int_{0}^{t_d} I(t)\, dt}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Generic Solar Thermal System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sun: Solar Radiation Source] --> B[Transparent Cover: Allows SW radiation In, Traps LW Out]
    B --> C[Selective Absorber Plate: alpha_s = 0.93 to 0.97]
    C --> D1[Conduction Through Absorber Plate]
    D1 --> E[Heat Transfer to Working Fluid: Convection in Tubes]
    C --> F1[Top Loss: Convection and Radiation]
    C --> F2[Bottom Loss: Conduction Through Insulation]
    C --> F3[Edge Loss: Conduction Through Frame]
    E --> G[Hot Working Fluid Delivery]
    G --> H1[Direct Use: Dryer, Cooker, Pasteurizer]
    G --> H2[Thermal Storage: Water Tank, PCM, Pebble Bed]
    H1 --> I[Processed Food Output]
    H2 --> J[On-Demand Heat Recovery]
```

## 4.2 Classification Flowchart of Solar Thermal Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Solar Thermal Systems] --> B[Non-Concentrating: Flat Plate Collectors]
    A --> C[Concentrating Collectors]
    A --> D[Solar Process Systems]

    B --> B1[Liquid FPC: Water, Glycol]
    B --> B2[Air FPC: Finned absorber with air flow]

    C --> C1[Line Focus: Parabolic Trough, Linear Fresnel]
    C --> C2[Point Focus: Parabolic Dish, Solar Tower, Scheffler]

    D --> D1[Solar Dryer: Direct, Indirect, Mixed Mode]
    D --> D2[Solar Cooker: Box, Scheffler Community]
    D --> D3[Solar Still: Single Basin, Double Basin, Multi-Effect]
    D --> D4[Solar Pasteurizer: Batch and Continuous]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Indirect Solar Dryer for Food Processing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sun Radiation] --> B[Transparent Flat Plate Air Heater Collector]
    B -->|Hot Air 60 to 80 degC| C[Blower or Chimney Driven Flow]
    C --> D[Drying Chamber with Food Trays]
    D -->|Moisture Laden Exhaust| E[Exhaust to Atmosphere via Chimney]
    D --> F[Partially Dried Food]
    F --> G[Manual Stirring or Turning]
    G --> D
    D --> H[Final Dried Product with Safe Moisture]
    B -.->|Optional backup| I[Thermal Storage: Pebble Bed]
    I --> B
```

## 4.4 HWB Performance Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Intercept: F_R times alpha_s times tau] --> B[Slope: minus F_R times U_L]
    B --> C[Useful Heat Gain Region]
    C --> D[Stagnation: Tfi - Ta too High, Q_u Approaches 0]
    D --> E[Losses Dominate: Collector Ineffective]
    A --> F[Low Temperature: High Efficiency]
    F --> G[Useful Operation Window]
```

## 4.5 Functional Architecture — Concentrating Solar System for Process Steam

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Solar Disc: Parabolic Dish or Scheffler] --> B[Concentrated Sunlight: Flux 50 to 200 Suns]
    B --> C[Focal Point Receiver]
    C --> D[Heat Transfer Fluid: Oil or Steam Tubes]
    D --> E[Boiler or Steam Generator]
    E --> F[Process Steam at 150 to 300 degC]
    F --> G1[Food Sterilization]
    F --> G2[Evaporation in Dairy]
    F --> G3[Concentration of Fruit Juices]
    E --> H[Optional: Rankine Cycle for CHP]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Reading Tip:** In the first diagram, the three loss channels (top, bottom, edge) are deliberately drawn as separate parallel branches because they are independent and additive in the overall $U_L$ formulation.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define the Hottel–Whillier–Bliss (HWB) equation. What is its physical significance in the design of flat plate solar collectors?

**Model Answer:**

The Hottel–Whillier–Bliss (HWB) equation expresses the **useful heat gain** $Q_u$ of a flat plate solar collector as:

$$
Q_u = A_c F_R \left[ S - U_L (T_{fi} - T_a) \right]
$$

**Physical significance:**

- $F_R$ = collector heat removal factor (efficiency of moving heat from plate to fluid), $S = \alpha_s \tau I$ = absorbed radiation per unit area, $U_L$ = overall loss coefficient, $T_{fi}$ = fluid inlet temperature, $T_a$ = ambient temperature.
- The equation shows useful energy is a **linear decreasing function** of $\left(\frac{T_{fi} - T_a}{I}\right)$.
- It is the foundation of the **standard ASHRAE 93 test** for collector characterization and the basis of all performance sizing in solar process design.
- The slope $-F_R U_L$ and intercept $F_R \alpha_s \tau$ together completely define collector performance for a given test.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Jun 2023]** Differentiate between a direct, an indirect, and a mixed-mode solar dryer. State two food products best suited to each.

**Model Answer:**

| Type | Description | Mode of Heat Transfer | Suitable Foods |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Direct** | Product is placed in a transparent enclosure and exposed to direct sunlight. | Direct radiation + convection from heated air | Chili, grapes (raisins), thin slices of mango, herbs |
| **Indirect** | Air is heated in a separate flat plate collector and then passed over the product in an opaque drying chamber. | Purely convective (no direct sun on product) | Turmeric slices, fish, ginger, mushrooms (color-sensitive) |
| **Mixed-mode** | Combines both — direct exposure plus pre-heated air from a solar air heater. | Radiation + convection combined | Pineapple rings, banana, papaya cubes |

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working principle of a **flat plate solar collector (FPC)**. Explain the role of the transparent cover, selective absorber coating, insulation, and riser tubes. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Hottel–Whillier–Bliss equation** for useful heat gain. A flat plate collector of area $3.0$ m² receives solar radiation of $I = 750$ W/m². The ambient temperature is $T_a = 32$ °C, the inlet fluid temperature is $T_{fi} = 55$ °C, $\alpha_s \tau = 0.83$, and $F_R U_L = 4.0$ W/m²K. Compute: (i) useful heat gain in W, and (ii) instantaneous thermal efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction & Working Principle of a Flat Plate Collector:**

A flat plate solar collector consists of the following components (top to bottom):

1. **Transparent Glazing (Cover):** One or two sheets of low-iron tempered glass (or transparent polymer) with high transmittance $\tau$ (0.85–0.91) for solar short-wave radiation, and low emissivity for IR. This creates the greenhouse effect, reducing top losses.
2. **Absorber Plate:** A metal (copper, aluminum) sheet coated with a **selective surface** (e.g., black chrome, nickel-pigmented aluminium oxide) that has high solar absorptivity ($\alpha_s \approx 0.95$) and low thermal emissivity ($\varepsilon \approx 0.10$). Risers/tubes are bonded to it.
3. **Tube/Riser Network:** Water or air flows through these tubes and absorbs heat conducted from the plate.
4. **Insulation (Bottom & Sides):** Rock wool, glass wool, or polyurethane foam (thickness 50–100 mm) to suppress bottom and edge losses.
5. **Casing/Frame:** Galvanized iron or aluminium to protect the assembly and provide sealing.

**Working principle:** Short-wave solar radiation passes through the glazing, is absorbed by the selective surface, converted into heat, conducted through the plate, transferred to the working fluid in the tubes, and the fluid carries the heat to the storage tank or processing unit. Losses from the top are minimized by the glazing and from the bottom by insulation.

**(b) Derivation & Numerical Solution:**

Derivation steps are as shown in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Recall the final form:

$$
Q_u = A_c F_R \left[ \alpha_s \tau I - U_L (T_{fi} - T_a) \right]
$$

**Given:** $A_c = 3.0$ m², $I = 750$ W/m², $T_a = 32$ °C, $T_{fi} = 55$ °C, $\alpha_s \tau = 0.83$, $F_R U_L = 4.0$ W/m²K.

**(i) Useful heat gain:**

$$
Q_u = 3.0 \times \frac{1}{1} \left[ 0.83 \times 750 - 4.0 \times (55 - 32) \right]
$$

$$
Q_u = 3.0 \times \left[ 622.5 - 92 \right] = 3.0 \times 530.5
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_u = 1591.5 \text{ W} \approx 1.59 \text{ kW}}
$$

**(ii) Instantaneous efficiency:**

$$
\eta_c = \frac{Q_u}{A_c I} = \frac{1591.5}{3.0 \times 750} = \frac{1591.5}{2250}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_c = 0.7073 = 70.73\%}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Many students forget to **multiply by $A_c$** when computing $Q_u$ and just compute per-unit-area values, losing 3 marks. Also, when $F_R U_L$ is given as a single product, do not try to separate them — use the combined product directly in the HWB equation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — KTU 2021 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **single-basin solar still** with a neat diagram. Derive an expression for its daily yield. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An indirect solar dryer is used to dry 100 kg of ripe mango slices from an initial moisture content of 80 % (wet basis) to a final safe moisture of 12 % (wet basis) over 8 hours. The collector area is 6 m², the average incident solar radiation is 650 W/m², and the drying efficiency is 30 %. Calculate: (i) the mass of water evaporated, (ii) the total incident solar energy, and (iii) the actual drying time required if the assumed efficiency holds. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Single-Basin Solar Still:**

A single-basin solar still consists of:

- An **impervious basin** (concrete, plastic-lined mild steel) containing saline/brackish water to be distilled.
- A **black absorber lining** at the bottom to maximize solar absorption.
- A **transparent inclined glass or polycarbonate roof** tilted at a small angle (≈ 10–15°) to the horizontal, with the lower edge dipping into a distillate collection channel.
- Side insulation to reduce side losses.

**Working:** Solar radiation passes through the transparent roof and is absorbed by the black basin liner. The water in the basin heats up and evaporates. The vapor rises, contacts the cooler underside of the inclined roof, **condenses** as fresh water droplets, and trickles down the slope into the collection trough. The distillate is essentially pure (TDS < 50 ppm).

**Daily yield equation:**

The hourly productivity $\dot{m}$ is the useful heat divided by latent heat:

$$
\dot{m}(t) = \frac{Q_u(t)}{\lambda_{fg}(T_w)} = \frac{A_c (S - U_L (T_w - T_a))}{\lambda_{fg}}
$$

The **daily yield per unit area** is:

$$
Y_d = \frac{1}{\lambda_{fg}} \int_{sunrise}^{sunset} \left[ \alpha_s \tau I(t) - U_L (T_w(t) - T_a(t)) \right] dt
$$

Typical daily yields range from **2.5 to 4.0 L/m²/day** for a single-basin still in tropical Indian conditions.

**(b) Drying Numerical:**

**Given:** $m_{initial} = 100$ kg, $M_i = 0.80$ (wb), $M_f = 0.12$ (wb), $t_{assumed} = 8$ h, $A_c = 6$ m², $I_{avg} = 650$ W/m², $\eta_d = 0.30$.

**(i) Mass of water evaporated:**

The dry solid mass is:

$$
m_{ds} = m_i (1 - M_i) = 100 \times (1 - 0.80) = 20 \text{ kg}
$$

Mass of water initially:

$$
m_{w,i} = 100 \times 0.80 = 80 \text{ kg}
$$

Mass of water finally:

$$
m_{w,f} = \frac{M_f \cdot m_{ds}}{1 - M_f} = \frac{0.12 \times 20}{0.88} = 2.727 \text{ kg}
$$

Water evaporated:

$$
m_w = 80 - 2.727 = 77.27 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
\boxed{m_w = 77.27 \text{ kg}}
$$

**(ii) Total incident solar energy over 8 hours:**

$$
E_{in} = A_c \cdot I_{avg} \cdot t = 6 \times 650 \times (8 \times 3600) = 6 \times 650 \times 28800
$$

$$
E_{in} = 1.1232 \times 10^8 \text{ J} = 112.32 \text{ MJ}
$$

(Or in kWh: $E_{in} = 6 \times 0.65 \times 8 = 31.2$ kWh)

**(iii) Check of actual drying time / energy balance:**

Required latent energy:

$$
Q_{req} = m_w \lambda_w = 77.27 \times 2400 \text{ kJ/kg} = 185{,}455 \text{ kJ} = 185.46 \text{ MJ}
$$

Available useful energy at $\eta_d = 30\%$:

$$
E_{useful} = 0.30 \times 112.32 = 33.696 \text{ MJ}
$$

This is **insufficient** — the assumed 8-hour period and 30 % efficiency will **not** dry the load. To achieve the required energy:

$$
t_{actual} = \frac{Q_{req}}{\eta_d \cdot A_c \cdot I_{avg}} = \frac{185.46 \times 10^6}{0.30 \times 6 \times 650} = \frac{185.46 \times 10^6}{1170}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{actual} \approx 158{,}513 \text{ s} \approx 44.03 \text{ h} \approx 2 \text{ days of strong sun}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> - **Always convert moisture content properly** — KTU frequently mixes wet-basis and dry-basis. Confirm the basis before applying the formula.
> - **Latent heat is in kJ/kg, not J/kg** — dividing by 1000 in the wrong place is a common 2-mark error.
> - **Do not use $E_{in} = A_c I t$ in Wh** if $\lambda_w$ is in kJ. Convert to a common energy unit first.
> - The drying time check is often overlooked — KTU examiners reward students who report feasibility in 2 marks of part (b).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Solar constant** = **1367 W/m²** (memorize verbatim).
- **Declination angle** = $23.45 \sin\left(\frac{360(284+n)}{365}\right)$ (Cooper's equation).
- **Hour angle** $\omega = 15(t_s - 12)$ with $t_s$ in solar hours.
- **HWB equation** is the **single most important formula** — derive it from the energy balance and linearized loss term.
- **Collector efficiency** is linear in $\left(\frac{T_{fi} - T_a}{I}\right)$ — plot the efficiency curve as a straight line with slope $-F_R U_L$ and intercept $F_R \alpha_s \tau$.
- **Selective surface** = high $\alpha_s$ (≈ 0.95), low $\varepsilon$ (≈ 0.10–0.20).
- **Cover transmittance** for low-iron glass: 0.85–0.91; for clear glass: ~ 0.83.
- **U_L** typically lies in 4–8 W/m²K for well-insulated FPCs.
- **Solar dryers** — three categories: direct, indirect, mixed-mode; **drying efficiency** is the ratio of latent heat of water removed to incident solar energy.
- **Solar still** daily yield = 2.5–4.0 L/m²/day in tropical conditions; **latent heat of vaporization** = 2400–2500 kJ/kg.
- **Thermal storage** includes sensible (water tanks, pebble beds) and latent (PCM: paraffin, salt hydrates).
- **Concentrating collectors** deliver higher temperatures (150–400 °C) — Scheffler dishes (50–200 suns) are the most relevant for food processing steam generation in rural India.
- **Loss mechanisms** in a flat plate collector: **top** (convection + IR radiation), **bottom** (conduction), **edge** (conduction).
- **Unit checks** — KTU numerics demand consistency: W, J, kg, m, s, K. Energy in J = W × s.
- **Always show the energy balance and loss linearization** — that's where the marks are.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Considerations: Collectors and Storage Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT866 - APPLICATIONS OF RENEWABLE ENERGY IN FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design Considerations: Collectors and Storage Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University (KTU) 2024 Scheme – PEFTT866**, the topic *"Design Considerations: Collectors and Storage Systems"* refers to the **systematic engineering methodology** used to size, orient, and select thermal hardware components for harnessing renewable energy (predominantly **solar thermal energy**) and storing it for subsequent use in food processing operations such as **drying, blanching, pasteurization, sterilization, evaporation, and cooking**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> A *collector* is the device that intercepts incoming renewable energy flux (solar radiation, biomass heat, or biogas combustion energy) and converts it into a usable thermal working fluid (water, air, oil, or steam). A *storage system* is the engineered reservoir (sensible, latent, or thermochemical) that decouples the **intermittent energy supply** from the **continuous or batch-wise energy demand** of a food processing plant.

The two units together form the **supply-side heart** of any renewable-energy-driven food processing plant. Without proper sizing of the collector area and storage capacity, the plant either *under-utilises* harvested energy (wasted capital) or *under-supplies* process heat (production loss).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Kitchen Water Tank and Geyser" Model

Imagine a household where solar energy is like a *taps water supply*:

- The **solar collector** behaves like the **roof-top rainwater harvesting sheet** — it catches whatever energy "falls" on it during the day.
- The **storage tank** behaves like the **overhead water tank** — it saves the resource so you can use it at night or during cloudy hours when there is no "rain."
- The **food processing equipment** (dryer, boiler, pasteuriser) is the *kitchen tap* — it draws only when needed.
- The **controller / pump** is the *valve-and-pipeline system* — it regulates flow.

If the roof sheet (collector) is too small, very little water is harvested. If the tank is too small, the collected water overflows and is wasted. If the tank is too big, capital is locked up without benefit. **Design = matching the roof area and the tank size to the kitchen's daily demand.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Food Processing:**
> Unlike domestic use, food processing demands **strict temperature windows** (e.g., milk pasteurisation at **72 °C for 15 s**, paddy drying at **45–60 °C**, spice sterilization at **100–140 °C**). The collector and storage design must therefore guarantee *both quantity* (kWh) and *quality* (temperature level).

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following values are **standard reference constants** used throughout this module:

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $I_{sc}$ | Solar Constant | **1361 W/m²** (older standard: 1367 W/m²) | W/m² |
| $\sigma$ | Stefan-Boltzmann Constant | **$5.67 \times 10^{-8}$** | W/(m²·K⁴) |
| $c_p$(water) | Specific heat of water | **4.186** | kJ/(kg·K) |
| $c_p$(air) | Specific heat of air | **1.005** | kJ/(kg·K) |
| $L_f$(water→ice) | Latent heat of fusion | **334** | kJ/kg |
| $h_{fg}$(water) | Latent heat of vaporisation | **2257** | kJ/kg at 100 °C |
| $L$ | Latitude of Kerala (approx.) | **8–12° N** | degrees |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:**
> Always carry the value of $\sigma$, the solar constant, and the specific heat of water in your memory — they appear in **almost every numerical question** in this module.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Layer Design Logic

A KTU examiner expects students to approach collector and storage design in **three structured layers**, mirroring the **Hottel-Whillier-Bliss (HWB)** analysis framework:

**Layer 1 — Energy Availability (Supply Side):**
Determine how much solar / renewable energy *can* be harvested at the given location and time.

**Layer 2 — Energy Conversion (Collector Performance):**
Determine how much of the *available* energy is *actually* converted into useful heat by the collector.

**Layer 3 — Energy Storage (Demand-Supply Buffer):**
Determine the storage capacity needed to bridge the time-gap between supply (daytime) and demand (processing hours).

### 2.2 Solar Collector Classification Relevant to Food Processing

```
Renewable Thermal Collectors
├── 1. Flat-Plate Collectors (FPC)
│       ├── Liquid-type (water/air heating)
│       └── Used for: washing, blanching, drying (T < 80 °C)
├── 2. Concentrating Collectors
│       ├── Parabolic Trough (T up to 300 °C)
│       ├── Scheffler Dish (T up to 400 °C)
│       └── Used for: steam generation, sterilization
├── 3. Evacuated Tube Collectors (ETC)
│       └── Used for: pasteurisation, hot water (T = 60–120 °C)
└── 4. Solar Air Heaters (SAH)
        └── Used for: crop drying (T = 40–80 °C)
```

> [!NOTE]
> For **Kerala's tropical climate (latitude ~10° N)**, **Flat-Plate Collectors (FPC)** and **Solar Air Heaters (SAH)** dominate food-processing applications because of their simplicity, low cost, and ability to deliver moderate temperatures.

### 2.3 The Hottel-Whillier-Bliss (HWB) Equation — The Core Performance Model

This is the **single most important equation** for KTU exams on collector design. It relates the *useful heat gain* of a flat-plate collector to the *incident solar radiation*, accounting for all thermal losses.

The instantaneous thermal efficiency $\eta_c$ of a flat-plate collector is expressed as:

$$\eta_c = \frac{Q_u}{A_c \, I_T} = F_R(\tau\alpha) - F_R \, U_L \frac{T_{in} - T_a}{I_T}$$

Where:

- $Q_u$ = useful heat collected (W)
- $A_c$ = collector aperture area (m²)
- $I_T$ = total solar radiation incident on tilted surface (W/m²)
- $F_R$ = **heat removal factor** (dimensionless, 0 < $F_R$ < 1)
- $(\tau\alpha)$ = effective transmittance-absorptance product (typically **0.75–0.95**)
- $U_L$ = **overall heat loss coefficient** (W/m²·K)
- $T_{in}$ = fluid inlet temperature (°C or K)
- $T_a$ = ambient temperature (°C or K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this equation important?**
> The HWB equation is a *straight-line equation* when $\eta_c$ is plotted against $\frac{(T_{in} - T_a)}{I_T}$. The **y-intercept** gives $F_R(\tau\alpha)$ (the *optical efficiency*) and the **slope** gives $-F_R U_L$ (the *thermal loss rate*). This is exactly what KTU experimental/analytical questions test.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** you must memorise for the 14-mark questions in Module 2. The table avoids the literal `pipe` character inside cells (KTU LaTeX-safe format).

| # | Formula | Meaning / When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_u = A_c \, F_R \left[ I_T (\tau\alpha) - U_L (T_{in} - T_a) \right]$ | **Useful heat gain** of a flat-plate collector (W) |
| 2 | $\eta_c = F_R(\tau\alpha) - F_R U_L \frac{(T_{in} - T_a)}{I_T}$ | **Instantaneous collector efficiency** (HWB linear model) |
| 3 | $Q_s = m \, c_p \, (T_{in} - T_a)$ | Sensible heat storage capacity (J) — for **water tanks** |
| 4 | $Q_s = m \, L$ | Latent heat storage capacity (J) — for **PCM systems** |
| 5 | $\delta = 23.45 \sin\left(\frac{360(284+n)}{365}\right)$ | Solar **declination angle** on day $n$ of the year (degrees) |
| 6 | $\omega = 15(t_{solar} - 12)$ | **Hour angle** at solar time $t_{solar}$ (degrees) |
| 7 | $\cos\theta_i = \sin\delta\sin\phi\cos\beta - \sin\delta\cos\phi\sin\beta\cos\gamma + \cos\delta\cos\phi\cos\beta\cos\omega + \cos\delta\sin\phi\sin\beta\cos\gamma\cos\omega + \cos\delta\sin\beta\sin\gamma\sin\omega$ | **Angle of incidence** on a tilted surface (general) |
| 8 | $\beta_{opt} = \phi - \delta$ | Optimal tilt for **winter** (food processing focus) — degrees |
| 9 | $\beta_{opt} = \phi + \delta$ | Optimal tilt for **summer** — degrees |
| 10 | $I_T = I_b R_b + I_d \left(\frac{1 + \cos\beta}{2}\right) + I \rho_g \left(\frac{1 - \cos\beta}{2}\right)$ | **Total radiation on tilted surface** (isotropic sky model, Liu & Jordan) |
| 11 | $R_b = \frac{\cos\theta_i}{\cos\theta_z}$ | Geometric factor relating tilted to horizontal beam radiation |
| 12 | $\theta_z = \cos^{-1}(\sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega)$ | **Zenith angle** of the sun |
| 13 | $Q_{loss} = U_L A_s (T_s - T_a)$ | **Standing loss** of a storage tank (W) |
| 14 | $A_{storage} = \frac{Q_{daily}}{\rho \, c_p \, \Delta T}$ | Sizing of **water storage tank** (m³) |
| 15 | $\tau_{storage} = \frac{m \, c_p}{U_L A_s}$ | **Time constant** of storage system (hours) — measure of how long tank retains heat |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Food Processing

| Food Process | Typical T (°C) | Suitable Collector | Storage Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Paddy drying** | 40–60 | Solar air heater + biomass backup | Sensible (pebble bed) |
| **Spice drying** (cardamom, pepper) | 45–65 | FPC + ETC hybrid | Sensible water tank |
| **Milk pasteurisation** | 72 | ETC + thermal storage | PCM (paraffin wax) |
| **Coconut oil extraction (pre-heating)** | 80–100 | FPC + boiler | Steam accumulator |
| **Cashew steam cooking** | 100–110 | Parabolic trough | Latent heat (Glauber's salt) |
| **Fish/meat smoking** | 60–90 | Solar dryer (transpired) | Sensible (rock bed) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Hottel-Whillier-Bliss Equation (Examiner Favourite)

We begin with the **energy balance** on the absorber plate of a flat-plate collector. This derivation is the **most repeated 14-mark question** in Module 2.

**Step 1 — Radiation absorbed by the absorber plate:**
The absorber plate of area $A_p$ receives the total incident solar flux $S$ (W/m²) and absorbs the fraction $(\tau\alpha)$. Therefore, the rate of solar energy absorbed by the plate is:

$$q_{abs} = A_p \, S \, (\tau\alpha)$$

Here, $\tau$ is the transmittance of the glass cover and $\alpha$ is the absorptance of the selective coating. The product $(\tau\alpha)$ represents the **net optical efficiency** of the cover–absorber combination.

**Step 2 — Heat loss from the plate to surroundings:**
The plate loses heat to the ambient by three simultaneous mechanisms: (a) **top loss** $U_t$ to the glass cover and then to the sky, (b) **bottom loss** $U_b$ through the insulation, and (c) **edge loss** $U_e$ from the sides. The total loss coefficient is:

$$U_L = U_t + U_b + U_e \quad \left[\text{W/(m}^{2}\text{·K)}\right]$$

The rate of heat loss from the plate, assumed at mean plate temperature $\bar{T_p}$, is therefore:

$$q_{loss} = A_p \, U_L \, (\bar{T_p} - T_a)$$

**Step 3 — Energy balance on the plate:**
By conservation of energy, the *useful heat transferred to the fluid* equals the *absorbed radiation minus the losses*:

$$Q_u = A_p \, (\tau\alpha) \, S - A_p \, U_L \, (\bar{T_p} - T_a)$$

**Step 4 — Introducing the heat removal factor $F_R$:**
The plate temperature $\bar{T_p}$ is *not* directly measurable in practice; we measure the *fluid inlet and outlet temperatures* instead. The **heat removal factor** $F_R$ is defined as the ratio of the *actual useful heat gain* to the *useful heat gain if the entire collector were at the fluid inlet temperature*:

$$F_R = \frac{Q_u}{A_c \left[ S (\tau\alpha) - U_L (T_{fi} - T_a) \right]}$$

**Step 5 — Rearranging into the HWB form:**
Solving for $Q_u$ and dividing by $A_c S$ gives the **classical Hottel-Whillier-Bliss efficiency equation**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{Q_u}{A_c \, I_T} &= F_R(\tau\alpha) - F_R \, U_L \, \frac{(T_{fi} - T_a)}{I_T} \\
\therefore \quad \eta_c &= F_R(\tau\alpha) - F_R \, U_L \, \frac{(T_{fi} - T_a)}{I_T}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **linear efficiency curve** of the collector. The intercept on the y-axis (when $T_{fi} = T_a$) is $F_R(\tau\alpha)$, called the **maximum/optical efficiency**, and the negative slope is $F_R U_L$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Insight:**
> Many students lose marks by writing $\tau\alpha$ separately without specifying that it represents the *combined* transmittance-absorptance product. Always state: *"$(\tau\alpha)$ is the effective transmittance–absorptance product, typically 0.75–0.95 for a single-glazed flat plate collector with a selective absorber."*

### 3.2 Derivation of the Optimal Tilt Angle for a South-Facing Surface in Kerala

For food-processing plants located in the **Northern Hemisphere** (Kerala is at $\phi \approx 10°$ N), the collector tilt $\beta$ should be chosen to **maximise annual (or seasonal) radiation capture**.

**Step 1 — Declination angle formula:**
The declination $\delta$ on day $n$ is given by **Cooper's equation**:

$$\delta = 23.45 \sin\left[\frac{360(284+n)}{365}\right] \quad \text{(degrees)}$$

For example, on **22 December** (winter solstice, $n = 356$):
$$\delta \approx -23.45°$$
For **22 June** (summer solstice, $n = 173$):
$$\delta \approx +23.45°$$

**Step 2 — Angle of incidence on a south-facing tilted surface:**
When the surface faces due south ($\gamma = 0$), the incidence angle simplifies to:

$$\cos\theta_i = \sin\delta \sin(\phi - \beta) + \cos\delta \cos(\phi - \beta) \cos\omega$$

**Step 3 — Maximising $\cos\theta_i$ at solar noon ($\omega = 0$):**
At noon, $\cos\omega = 1$, so:

$$\cos\theta_i = \cos(\phi - \beta - \delta)$$

This is maximum when the argument is zero, i.e., when:

$$\phi - \beta - \delta = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \beta = \phi - \delta$$

For **winter** ($\delta < 0$, say $\delta = -23.45°$) at $\phi = 10°$:
$$\beta_{winter} = 10 - (-23.45) = 33.45°$$

For **summer** ($\delta = +23.45°$):
$$\beta_{summer} = 10 - 23.45 = -13.45°$$

A negative tilt means the collector should lie flat (or be tilted slightly **north**) for summer — but for food processing where the demand is year-round (especially for drying kharif crops harvested in October-November), the **winter tilt** is preferred.

**Step 4 — Annual-average tilt:**
A common engineering rule of thumb (used in food-processing plants across India) is:

$$\beta_{annual} \approx \phi + 5° \approx 15°$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Insight:**
> Students often forget to mention the *seasonal purpose* of the tilt angle. Always state: *"Since the food-processing plant operates predominantly during the post-monsoon drying season (October–February), the winter tilt of $\beta = \phi - \delta_{winter}$ is selected."*

### 3.3 Derivation of the Storage Tank Sizing Equation

For a **sensible heat storage** system (most common in food processing — a hot-water tank), the energy stored when the tank is heated from ambient $T_a$ to a maximum temperature $T_{max}$ is:

**Step 1 — Energy balance on the tank:**
The tank contains mass $m = \rho V$ of water (kg). The heat stored is:

$$Q_{stored} = m \, c_p \, (T_{max} - T_{min})$$

Where:
- $T_{min}$ = minimum usable temperature for the food process
- $T_{max}$ = maximum storage temperature
- $c_p$ = **4.186 kJ/(kg·K)** for water

**Step 2 — Standing losses during storage:**
While the tank holds hot water, it loses heat to the surroundings at a rate:

$$\dot{Q}_{loss} = U_L A_s (T_{tank} - T_a)$$

**Step 3 — Time constant of the tank:**
If we define the **time constant** as the time for the temperature excess to fall to $1/e \approx 36.8\%$ of its initial value:

$$\tau = \frac{m \, c_p}{U_L A_s}$$

A larger $\tau$ means a better-insulated tank (less loss). For a food-processing plant requiring **18–24 hour storage**, $\tau \ge 24$ h is desirable.

**Step 4 — Sizing the tank for daily demand:**
If the daily thermal energy demand of the plant is $Q_{daily}$ (kWh/day), the tank volume is:

$$V = \frac{Q_{daily}}{\rho \, c_p \, \Delta T} \quad \text{(m}^3\text{)}$$

Where $\Delta T = T_{max} - T_{min}$ and $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ for water.

**Worked example (typical KTU 14-mark question):**
> A food processing unit requires **500 kWh/day** of thermal energy at **70 °C** for milk pasteurisation. The water tank is heated by solar collectors from **30 °C (ambient) to 90 °C** during the day. Size the storage tank.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{daily} &= 500 \text{ kWh/day} = 500 \times 3600 \text{ kJ/day} = 1.8 \times 10^6 \text{ kJ/day} \\
\Delta T &= 90 - 30 = 60 \text{ °C} \\
m &= \frac{Q_{daily}}{c_p \, \Delta T} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^6}{4.186 \times 60} \approx 7167 \text{ kg} \\
V &= \frac{m}{\rho} = \frac{7167}{1000} \approx 7.17 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

So a **~7.2 m³ tank** is required — equivalent to a cylinder of 2 m diameter and ~2.3 m height.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Collector + Storage Combination

| Plant Type | Operating T | Solar Resource | Recommended Collector | Recommended Storage | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Paddy dryer (small) | 45–60 °C | High (Kerala) | **FPC + SAH hybrid** | Sensible pebble bed | Low cost, large volume, low T |
| Spice dryer (medium) | 50–70 °C | High | **ETC** | PCM (paraffin 60 °C) | Compact, high energy density |
| Dairy pasteuriser | 72 °C | Moderate | **ETC with selective absorber** | PCM (D-mannitol 75 °C) | Tight temperature window |
| Cashew steam plant | 110 °C | High | **Parabolic trough** | Steam accumulator | High T, continuous supply |
| Fish dryer (coastal) | 60 °C | Moderate-High | **Transpired SAH + biomass** | Sensible water tank | Vented moisture, low corrosion |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Renewable Energy Food-Processing Plant

The following diagram maps the **interactions** between the collector, storage, and food-processing subsystems. This is rendered in Mermaid with all node IDs in the required alphanumeric form.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Solar Radiation Incident] --> A2[Flat Plate Collector Array]
    B1[Biomass Combustion] --> A2
    A2 --> C1{Pump and Controller}
    C1 --> D1[Hot Water Storage Tank]
    C1 --> D2[PCM Latent Storage]
    D1 --> E1[Heat Exchanger]
    D2 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Food Processing Unit]
    F1 --> F11[Paddy Dryer]
    F1 --> F12[Spice Dryer]
    F1 --> F13[Milk Pasteuriser]
    F1 --> F14[Cashew Steam Cooker]
    E1 --> G1[Hot Air Loop for Drying]
    G1 --> F12
    F1 --> H1[Condensate and Waste Heat Recovery]
    H1 --> D1
    D1 --> I1[Standing Losses to Ambient]
    A2 --> J1[Overshoot / Overflow Bypass]
    J1 --> I1
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of a Flat-Plate Collector Cross-Section

This diagram represents the *physical cross-section* of a flat-plate collector in functional block form (since Mermaid cannot render true mechanical drawings).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Transparent Glass Cover tau] --> P2[Air Gap U top Loss]
    P2 --> P3[Selective Absorber Plate alpha]
    P3 --> P4[Insulation Bottom U bottom]
    P3 --> P5[Fluid Tubes carrying water or air]
    P5 --> P6[Header Manifold]
    P6 --> P7[Inlet Cold Fluid]
    P6 --> P8[Outlet Hot Fluid]
    P3 --> P9[Side Edge Loss U edge]
    P9 --> P10[Ambient Temperature T ambient]
```

### 4.3 Decision Flowchart for Collector Type Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Process Temperature Required} -->|Below 80 C| Q2{Continuous or Batch}
    Q1 -->|80 to 200 C| Q3[Choose Evacuated Tube Collector ETC]
    Q1 -->|Above 200 C| Q4[Choose Concentrating Collector Parabolic Trough]
    Q2 -->|Continuous| Q5[Choose Flat Plate Collector with PCM Storage]
    Q2 -->|Batch drying| Q6[Choose Solar Air Heater with Pebble Bed]
    Q5 --> Q7[Calculate Area from HWB Equation]
    Q6 --> Q7
    Q3 --> Q7
    Q4 --> Q7
    Q7 --> Q8[Verify using Daily Energy Balance]
    Q8 --> Q9{Final Decision}
```

### 4.4 Schematic of a Typical Solar Food Dryer with Sensible Heat Storage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Sun] -->|Beam plus Diffuse Radiation| R2[Collector Plate]
    R2 -->|Heated Air| R3[Blower Fan]
    R3 --> R4[Pebble Bed Heat Storage]
    R4 -->|Hot Air 50 to 70 C| R5[Drying Chamber with Food Trays]
    R5 -->|Moist Exhaust| R6[Chimney Outlet]
    R4 -->|Stored Hot Pebbles for Night| R7[Insulated Container]
    R5 -->|Recirculated partially dry air| R8[Return Duct]
    R8 --> R3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of Diagrams:**
> In Diagram 4.1, the **heat exchanger (E1)** acts as the *isolation barrier* between the solar loop (which may have corrosion-prone fluid) and the food loop (which must be hygienically clean). The **waste heat recovery (H1)** returns condensate and exhaust heat back to storage, improving overall plant efficiency by 10–15%.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set 2]**

Define the *heat removal factor* $F_R$ of a flat-plate solar collector. Why is it always less than unity?

**Model Answer:**

The heat removal factor $F_R$ of a flat-plate solar collector is defined as the ratio of the *actual useful heat gain* of the collector to the *useful heat gain that would be obtained if the entire absorber plate were at the fluid inlet temperature*.

Mathematically:

$$F_R = \frac{Q_{u,\text{actual}}}{A_c \left[ I_T (\tau\alpha) - U_L (T_{fi} - T_a) \right]}$$

It is always less than unity ($F_R < 1$) because the absorber plate temperature $\bar{T_p}$ is *always greater* than the fluid inlet temperature $T_{fi}$. The plate cannot be everywhere in perfect thermal contact with the fluid — there are temperature gradients along the absorber, finite bond conductance between tube and plate, and varying fluid temperatures. Hence the actual useful heat is always less than the idealised case, and $F_R$ lies between 0.6 and 0.9 in practical collectors.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]**

List any **three** design considerations specific to solar collectors used in **food processing applications** that differ from domestic solar water heaters.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Hygiene and Material Compatibility:** Food-grade stainless steel (SS-304 or SS-316) must be used for fluid channels to prevent contamination, unlike the mild-steel tanks common in domestic heaters.

2. **Temperature Window Matching:** Food processes need precise temperature bands (e.g., 72 ± 1 °C for pasteurisation), requiring collector + storage designs that deliver *constant outlet temperature*, not just maximum temperature.

3. **Corrosion and Scaling from Process Water:** Process water often contains organic acids, salts, or proteins that corrode and foul collectors. Design must include cleaning cycles and corrosion-resistant selective coatings (e.g., $\text{TiNOX}$ or $\text{Al}_{2}\text{O}_{3}$).

*(Other valid points include: integration with existing boilers, batch vs continuous demand profile, biomass backup sizing, and CIP — Clean-in-Place compatibility.)*

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> In 14-mark HWB numerical problems, **students commonly lose 2–3 marks** by:
> (a) Failing to convert temperatures to the *same scale* (°C vs K cancels in differences, but absolute temperatures must be in K in radiation loss terms like $U_L(T_p - T_a)$ when paired with $\sigma T^4$ derivations).
> (b) Confusing $T_{fi}$ (fluid *inlet*) with $\bar{T_f}$ (fluid *mean*) in efficiency expressions — always read the question carefully.
> (c) Forgetting to convert hours of operation into seconds when computing daily energy in Joules.
> (d) Writing $(\tau\alpha)$ without numerical values — examiners expect you to *assume* a value (typically 0.85) and *state the assumption explicitly*.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Collector Design Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q4 (b)]**

A flat-plate solar collector is used to heat water for a small spice-drying unit in Kerala. The collector has the following specifications:

- Aperture area $A_c = 4 \text{ m}^2$
- Heat removal factor $F_R = 0.78$
- Effective transmittance-absorptance product $(\tau\alpha) = 0.85$
- Overall heat loss coefficient $U_L = 5.5 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$
- Average daily solar radiation on tilted surface $I_T = 650 \text{ W/m}^2$ for **6 peak sunshine hours**
- Fluid inlet temperature $T_{fi} = 35°\text{C}$
- Ambient temperature $T_a = 30°\text{C}$

**(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate the **instantaneous thermal efficiency** of the collector at the given operating conditions and determine whether it is operating efficiently.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Estimate the **total useful heat collected per day** (in kWh) and the **mass of water** that can be heated from 35 °C to a final temperature of 75 °C using this daily collection.

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 — Recall the HWB efficiency equation:**

$$\eta_c = F_R(\tau\alpha) - F_R U_L \frac{(T_{fi} - T_a)}{I_T}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_R(\tau\alpha) &= 0.78 \times 0.85 = 0.663 \\
F_R U_L \frac{(T_{fi} - T_a)}{I_T} &= 0.78 \times 5.5 \times \frac{(35 - 30)}{650} \\
&= 0.78 \times 5.5 \times \frac{5}{650} \\
&= \frac{21.45}{650} \\
&= 0.033 \\
\therefore \quad \eta_c &= 0.663 - 0.033 = 0.630
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

$$\boxed{\eta_c = 63.0\%}$$

A collector efficiency of 63% is **excellent** for a flat-plate collector at a *low temperature rise* of only 5 °C above ambient. Most commercial FPCs operate between 40% and 70% efficiency. The collector is therefore performing efficiently and is well-suited for spice drying which requires low-to-moderate temperatures.

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Useful heat per unit area per second:**

$$q_u = \eta_c \times I_T = 0.630 \times 650 = 409.5 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Total useful heat per day (6 peak sunshine hours):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{daily} &= q_u \times A_c \times t \\
&= 409.5 \times 4 \times 6 \times 3600 \text{ J} \\
&= 409.5 \times 4 \times 21600 \\
&= 35,380,800 \text{ J} \\
&\approx 35.38 \text{ MJ} \\
&\approx 9.83 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of water heated from 35 °C to 75 °C:**

The temperature rise is $\Delta T = 75 - 35 = 40°\text{C}$.

Using $Q = m \, c_p \, \Delta T$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{Q_{daily}}{c_p \times \Delta T} \\
&= \frac{35,380,800}{4186 \times 40} \\
&= \frac{35,380,800}{167,440} \\
&\approx 211.3 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{m \approx 211 \text{ kg of water per day}}$$

This corresponds to about **211 litres** — sufficient for one batch of spice blanching (~50 kg spice) plus cleaning.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Storage System Design Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Q5 (a)]**

A dairy processing plant in Kerala requires hot water at **72 °C for milk pasteurisation** operating **8 hours per day**. The plant uses an evacuated tube collector (ETC) array connected to a hot-water storage tank. The following data are given:

- Daily thermal energy demand $Q_{daily} = 1200 \text{ MJ}$
- Maximum storage temperature $T_{max} = 90°\text{C}$
- Minimum usable temperature $T_{min} = 60°\text{C}$ (for the heat exchanger to deliver 72 °C output)
- Storage tank overall heat loss coefficient $U_L = 1.2 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$
- Allowable temperature drop during 12-hour storage $\Delta T_{loss} = 5°\text{C}$
- Tank shape: vertical cylinder with height-to-diameter ratio of **2:1**
- Ambient temperature $T_a = 28°\text{C}$

**(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate the **required volume** of the storage tank in m³.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Determine the **tank dimensions** (diameter and height), the **standing heat loss rate**, and the **storage time constant**.

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Step 1 — Available temperature range:**

The tank is heated to $T_{max} = 90°\text{C}$ during the day, and water is drawn down to $T_{min} = 60°\text{C}$ at night. So the usable temperature swing is:

$$\Delta T_{usable} = 90 - 60 = 30°\text{C}$$

**Step 2 — Compute mass of water:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{Q_{daily}}{c_p \times \Delta T_{usable}} \\
&= \frac{1200 \times 10^6 \text{ J}}{4186 \times 30} \\
&= \frac{1.2 \times 10^9}{125,580} \\
&\approx 9556 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to volume (water density = 1000 kg/m³):**

$$V = \frac{m}{\rho} = \frac{9556}{1000} = 9.556 \text{ m}^3$$

$$\boxed{V \approx 9.56 \text{ m}^3}$$

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 — Tank geometry (H:D = 2:1):**

Let $D$ = diameter. Then $H = 2D$. The volume is:

$$V = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \times H = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \times 2D = \frac{\pi D^3}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for D:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^3 &= \frac{2V}{\pi} = \frac{2 \times 9.556}{3.1416} = 6.084 \\
D &= (6.084)^{1/3} \approx 1.826 \text{ m} \\
H &= 2D \approx 3.65 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{D \approx 1.83 \text{ m}, \quad H \approx 3.65 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 3 — Standing heat loss rate:**

The surface area of the tank (cylinder + top + bottom, neglecting insulation thickness):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_s &= \pi D H + 2 \times \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \\
&= \pi \times 1.826 \times 3.65 + \frac{\pi \times 1.826^2}{2} \\
&= 20.93 + 5.24 \\
&= 26.17 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Using mean tank temperature $T_{tank} = (90 + 60)/2 = 75°\text{C}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{loss} &= U_L \, A_s \, (T_{tank} - T_a) \\
&= 1.2 \times 26.17 \times (75 - 28) \\
&= 1.2 \times 26.17 \times 47 \\
&\approx 1475 \text{ W} \\
&\approx 1.48 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Storage time constant:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau &= \frac{m \, c_p}{U_L \, A_s} \\
&= \frac{9556 \times 4186}{1.2 \times 26.17} \\
&= \frac{4.00 \times 10^7}{31.40} \\
&\approx 1.274 \times 10^6 \text{ seconds} \\
&\approx 354 \text{ hours} \approx 14.7 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

A time constant of 14.7 days is **excellent** — it means the tank can hold its temperature for nearly two weeks with negligible cooling. This is far more than the required 12-hour overnight storage, confirming the design is conservative.

$$\boxed{\dot{Q}_{loss} \approx 1.48 \text{ kW}, \quad \tau \approx 14.7 \text{ days}}$$

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Collectors and Storage Systems**

- [ ] The **HWB efficiency equation** $\eta_c = F_R(\tau\alpha) - F_R U_L \frac{(T_{fi} - T_a)}{I_T}$ is the **single most important equation** — memorise symbol meanings and typical numerical ranges.

- [ ] $F_R(\tau\alpha)$ is the **optical efficiency** (y-intercept); $-F_R U_L$ is the **slope** of the efficiency curve.

- [ ] For **Kerala (latitude ~10° N)**, optimal winter tilt is $\beta_{winter} = \phi - \delta_{winter} \approx 33°$; optimal summer tilt is approximately flat ($-13°$, meaning horizontal).

- [ ] Solar **declination** varies sinusoidally between $-23.45°$ (winter) and $+23.45°$ (summer).

- [ ] Storage sizing: $V = \frac{Q_{daily}}{\rho \, c_p \, \Delta T}$ — a **4-step derivation** that appears almost every year in Part B.

- [ ] **PCM (latent) storage** is compact (high energy density per kg) but expensive; **sensible (water/pebble) storage** is cheap and reliable but bulky.

- [ ] Food-processing specific design considerations: **hygiene (SS-304)**, **temperature precision**, **CIP compatibility**, **corrosion resistance**, and **integration with biomass backup**.

- [ ] Always **convert units carefully**: W to kW (÷1000), hours to seconds (×3600), kJ to MJ (÷1000), kWh to MJ (×3.6).

- [ ] **Time constant** $\tau = \frac{m c_p}{U_L A_s}$ — larger is better; means better insulation.

- [ ] For **solar air heaters (SAH)**, the efficiency equation is similar but $F_R$ is much lower (~0.5–0.7) because air has lower heat capacity than water.

- [ ] **Evacuated tube collectors (ETC)** are preferred for dairy pasteurisation (72 °C) because of their lower $U_L$ (~2 W/m²·K) compared to FPC (~5–7 W/m²·K).

- [ ] Always end numerical answers with a **physical interpretation sentence** — examiners award 1–2 marks for this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications in Food Processing: Heating, Drying, and Thermal Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT866 - APPLICATIONS OF RENEWABLE ENERGY IN FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Applications in Food Processing: Heating, Drying, and Thermal Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Renewable Energy Applications in Food Processing** refer to the systematic utilization of replenishable natural energy sources — primarily **solar thermal energy**, **biomass-derived fuels** (biogas, producer gas, biodiesel), **geothermal energy**, and **wind-electricity** — to drive the three foundational unit operations of food preservation and transformation: **Heating** (raising product temperature), **Drying** (moisture removal through simultaneous heat and mass transfer), and **Thermal Processing** (controlled heat treatment for microbial inactivation, enzyme deactivation, and texture modification).

These applications substitute fossil-fuel-based energy inputs with sustainable alternatives, addressing the food industry's significant thermal-energy demand (which constitutes roughly **60–70 %** of total industrial energy consumption in food processing plants globally).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Module 2 specifically maps the energy flows of solar collectors, biogas burners, and biomass combustors into classic food-engineering unit operations — making this topic a direct intersection of **Energy Engineering** and **Food Process Technology**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a traditional kitchen on a sunny afternoon: sunlight streams through a window onto a metal tray of sliced mangoes. The metal absorbs the sun's rays and warms up; the moisture in the mangoes evaporates. That is **solar drying** happening in its most primitive form.

Now scale this up: instead of a window, we use a **flat-plate solar collector**; instead of a kitchen tray, we use a **solar tunnel dryer**; instead of drying mangoes, we are drying grains, spices, fish, or fruits for commercial shelf-stability. The same physics applies — energy from the sun (a renewable source) drives the thermal unit operation.

Similarly, a **biogas digester** (fed with food waste, animal manure, or agricultural residue) produces methane-rich gas. When this gas is combusted in a burner, it releases thermal energy that can pasteurize milk, boil syrup, or cook food — replacing LPG or diesel with a self-generated, carbon-neutral fuel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Renewable energy in food processing is analogous to using rainwater to irrigate crops — you tap a naturally replenishing resource to perform an essential function, rather than drawing from a finite reservoir (fossil fuels) that depletes over time.

---

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following benchmark values are universally cited in KTU-aligned food-process engineering references:

| Metric | Standard Value | Context of Use |
|---|---|---|
| Specific heat of water | **4.186 kJ/(kg·K)** | Heating calculations for aqueous foods |
| Latent heat of vaporization (water, 100 °C) | **2257 kJ/kg** | Drying energy estimation |
| Solar constant (AM 1.5) | **1367 W/m²** | Solar collector design baseline |
| Typical solar insolation (Kerala) | **4.5–5.5 kWh/m²/day** | Daily energy yield estimation |
| Pasteurization temperature (milk) | **72 °C for 15 s (HTST)** | Thermal process design |
| Sterilization temperature | **121 °C for 15 min** | Commercial sterilization |
| Blanching temperature (vegetables) | **85–95 °C for 2–5 min** | Enzyme inactivation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The food-processing sector is one of the **largest industrial consumers of thermal energy**. Replacing even a fraction of this with solar, biogas, or biomass directly cuts greenhouse-gas emissions and operating costs — making this module highly relevant to sustainable development goals (SDG 7: Affordable and Clean Energy; SDG 13: Climate Action).

---

## 1.4 Classification of Renewable-Energy-Driven Food Processes

The three primary thermal applications addressed in this module are:

1. **Heating Applications** — Direct use of thermal energy for raising product temperature (water/syrup heating, cooking, boiling, evaporation).
2. **Drying Applications** — Combined heat-and-mass-transfer operation for moisture removal (solar drying, hybrid drying, biomass-heated dryers).
3. **Thermal Processes** — Controlled heat treatment for safety and quality (pasteurization, sterilization, blanching, roasting, baking).

Each of these will be analyzed in depth in the following sections.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Renewable Energy Sources Used in Food Processing

### 2.1.1 Solar Thermal Energy
Solar radiation is captured via **flat-plate collectors (FPC)**, **evacuated tube collectors (ETC)**, **parabolic trough concentrators (PTC)**, or **solar air heaters (SAH)**. The captured thermal energy is then transferred to a working fluid (water, air, or thermal oil) for downstream food processing.

### 2.1.2 Biomass Energy
Solid biomass (wood chips, rice husk, coconut shell, bagasse), liquid biofuels (biodiesel, ethanol), or gaseous biofuels (biogas, producer gas) are combusted or gasified to release thermal energy. Biomass is the most commonly used renewable energy source in food processing in developing countries.

### 2.1.3 Biogas
Anaerobic digestion of organic waste (food processing residues, cattle dung, kitchen waste) produces **biogas** (~60 % CH₄ + 40 % CO₂) with a calorific value of **~20–25 MJ/m³**. Combusted in burners, it directly substitutes LPG.

### 2.1.4 Geothermal Energy
Although region-specific, low-to-medium enthalpy geothermal resources (50–150 °C) can be used directly for drying, space heating, and pasteurization.

### 2.1.5 Wind-Electricity and Solar-PV
Photovoltaic (PV) panels and wind turbines generate electricity that can drive electric resistance heaters, heat pumps, or mechanical dryers — indirectly powering thermal processes.

---

## 2.2 Heating Applications

### 2.2.1 Solar Water Heating
A **flat-plate solar collector** absorbs solar radiation, converts it to heat, and transfers this heat to water flowing through tubes attached to the absorber plate.

**Operational Logic:**
- Sunlight passes through the glass cover (transmissivity $\tau$).
- Absorber plate (selective coating, absorptivity $\alpha > 0.95$) converts photons to heat.
- Heat conducts through the riser tubes into the working fluid.
- Heat losses occur via convection and radiation from the plate.

This heated water (60–90 °C) is used for:
- **Cleaning-in-place (CIP)** of processing equipment.
- **Boiler feedwater preheating.**
- **Cooking, blanching, and reconstitution.**

### 2.2.2 Biogas-Based Heating
Biogas burners deliver a controlled flame that heats vessels containing food products. The thermal efficiency of a well-designed biogas burner is **55–65 %**.

### 2.2.3 Biomass Furnaces and Combustors
Direct-fired biomass furnaces (using wood, rice husk, or bagasse) supply hot combustion gases or steam for thermal processes. These are widely used in **jaggery production**, **rice parboiling**, and **tea processing**.

---

## 2.3 Drying Applications

### 2.3.1 Fundamental Principle of Drying
Drying is a **simultaneous heat-and-mass-transfer** operation in which:
- **Heat transfer** occurs from the drying medium (hot air) to the wet product.
- **Mass transfer** occurs as moisture migrates from inside the product to its surface and then evaporates into the air.

The driving force for moisture evaporation is the **vapor-pressure difference** between the product surface and the surrounding air.

### 2.3.2 Types of Solar Dryers (Classified by KTU Syllabus)

| Type | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Open sun drying** | Product spread directly under sun | Small-scale, traditional |
| **Direct solar dryer** | Enclosed cabinet with transparent cover; absorber plate heats air | Fruits, vegetables, spices |
| **Indirect solar dryer** | Separate solar air heater + drying chamber | Higher temperature, better hygiene |
| **Mixed-mode solar dryer** | Combines direct + indirect heating | Grains, fish, medicinal plants |
| **Solar tunnel dryer** | Transparent polyethylene tunnel with fan-assisted airflow | Commercial-scale drying |
| **Hybrid solar dryer** | Solar + biomass/biogas back-up | Continuous, year-round operation |

### 2.3.3 Drying Mechanism — Falling Rate Period
Most food products dry in the **falling-rate period**, where internal moisture diffusion (liquid or vapor) controls the rate. The drying rate is approximated by:

$$ \frac{dX}{dt} = -k(X - X_e) $$

where:
- $X$ = moisture content at time $t$ (kg water/kg dry solid)
