- $X_e$ = equilibrium moisture content
- $k$ = drying constant (1/s)

---

## 2.4 Thermal Processes

### 2.4.1 Pasteurization
A mild heat treatment (below 100 °C) that destroys pathogenic vegetative cells and significantly reduces spoilage microorganisms, while preserving sensory and nutritional quality.

**Examples:**
- Milk: 72 °C / 15 s (HTST) or 63 °C / 30 min (LTLT)
- Fruit juice: 65–75 °C / 15–30 s
- Honey: 63–71 °C to delay crystallization

### 2.4.2 Sterilization
A more severe heat treatment (typically 121 °C for 15 min in retort) that destroys all vegetative cells and most spores, achieving **commercial sterility** for shelf-stable canned foods.

### 2.4.3 Blanching
A short heat treatment (85–95 °C / 2–5 min) applied to vegetables and fruits **before** freezing, drying, or canning. Its primary purposes are:
- **Enzyme inactivation** (peroxidase, catalase, polyphenol oxidase) to prevent quality loss.
- **Texture softening** for ease of packing.
- **Air removal** from intercellular spaces.

### 2.4.4 Cooking, Roasting, Baking
Renewable-energy-heated ovens (biomass-fired, solar ovens, biogas-heated) are used for bread baking, nut roasting, and grain puffing.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM ALERT:** The following equations are the most frequently tested in KTU ESE for this module. Memorize both the formula and the unit conventions.

| # | Formula | Description | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = mc_p \Delta T$ | Sensible heat for heating | $m$ = mass (kg), $c_p$ = specific heat (kJ/kg·K), $\Delta T$ = temperature rise (K) |
| 2 | $Q = m \lambda$ | Latent heat for phase change | $m$ = mass of water (kg), $\lambda$ = latent heat (kJ/kg) |
| 3 | $Q_{total} = m_f c_{pf} \Delta T + m_w \lambda$ | Combined heating + evaporation | $m_f$ = food mass, $c_{pf}$ = food specific heat, $m_w$ = water evaporated |
| 4 | $\eta_c = \frac{Q_u}{A_c \cdot I_t}$ | Instantaneous collector efficiency | $Q_u$ = useful heat (W), $A_c$ = collector area (m²), $I_t$ = solar irradiance (W/m²) |
| 5 | $\eta_d = \frac{m_w \lambda}{I_t \cdot A_c \cdot t}$ | Dryer efficiency | $m_w$ = water removed (kg), $t$ = drying time (s) |
| 6 | $\eta_{comb} = \frac{m_f \cdot CV_f}{m_b \cdot CV_b}$ | Combustion / burner efficiency | $CV$ = calorific value (kJ/kg or kJ/m³) |
| 7 | $\frac{dX}{dt} = -k(X - X_e)$ | Falling-rate drying kinetics | $X$ = moisture content (db), $k$ = constant (1/s) |
| 8 | $t = \frac{\ln\left(\frac{X_1 - X_e}{X_2 - X_e}\right)}{k}$ | Drying time (falling rate) | $X_1, X_2$ = initial/final moisture |
| 9 | $F_0 = 10^{\frac{T - 121}{z}} \cdot t$ | Sterilization value (lethality) | $T$ = process temperature (°C), $z$ = z-value (°C) |
| 10 | $P = \frac{\Delta H_v}{\Delta H_v + c_p(T_b - T_a)}$ | Pasteurization value | $\Delta H_v$ = latent heat of vapor |

**Critical Note on Symbols:** In the markdown table above, the vertical pipe symbol `|` has been replaced with `\vert` (e.g., $\frac{m_w \lambda}{\vert I_t \vert \cdot A_c \cdot t}$) wherever absolute value or modulus is intended, to preserve table syntax integrity.

---

| Grain drying | Solar + biomass hybrid | Rice, maize, wheat drying in Kerala/Tamil Nadu |

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Thermal Energy Required for a Drying Process

**Problem Statement:** A batch of food product with initial mass $m_1$ and moisture content $M_1$ (wet basis) is to be dried to final moisture content $M_2$ (wet basis). The drying medium enters at $T_1$ and leaves at $T_2$. Derive the total energy required considering both sensible heating of the product and evaporation of moisture.

### Step 1: Express Initial and Final Dry Solid Mass

The mass of bone-dry solid in the product is constant throughout drying. Initial mass of dry solid:

$$ m_s = m_1 (1 - M_1) $$

Similarly, the final dry solid mass equals:

$$ m_s = m_2 (1 - M_2) $$

### Step 2: Equate to Find Final Product Mass

Equating the two expressions for $m_s$:

$$ m_1 (1 - M_1) = m_2 (1 - M_2) $$

Therefore, the final mass is:

$$ m_2 = \frac{m_1 (1 - M_1)}{(1 - M_2)} $$

### Step 3: Determine Mass of Water to be Evaporated

The mass of water removed during drying is the difference between initial and final water content:

$$ m_w = m_1 M_1 - m_2 M_2 $$

Substituting $m_2$:

$$ m_w = m_1 M_1 - \frac{m_1 (1 - M_1) M_2}{(1 - M_2)} $$

This simplifies to:

$$ m_w = m_1 \left[ M_1 - \frac{(1 - M_1) M_2}{(1 - M_2)} \right] $$

### Step 4: Calculate Energy Required for Sensible Heating of Product

The product (dry solid + bound water) must be heated from ambient temperature $T_a$ to the drying temperature $T_p$:

$$ Q_{sensible} = m_1 c_{p,product} (T_p - T_a) $$

where $c_{p,product}$ is the specific heat of the wet food product, often approximated by:

$$ c_{p,product} = c_{p,solid} \cdot x_s + c_{p,water} \cdot x_w $$

with $x_s$ and $x_w$ being mass fractions of solid and water.

### Step 5: Calculate Energy Required for Moisture Evaporation

The latent heat required to vaporize the removed water at the drying temperature:

$$ Q_{latent} = m_w \lambda_{T_p} $$

where $\lambda_{T_p}$ is the latent heat of vaporization at the product temperature $T_p$. For water:

$$ \lambda_{T_p} \approx 2501 - 2.42 \cdot T_p \quad \text{(kJ/kg)} $$

### Step 6: Total Energy Required

$$ Q_{total} = Q_{sensible} + Q_{latent} = m_1 c_{p,product}(T_p - T_a) + m_w \lambda_{T_p} $$

This is the fundamental energy equation used in **solar dryer design** and **biomass-fired industrial dryer** sizing.

### Step 7: Numerical Evaluation Example

**Given:** $m_1 = 1000$ kg of grapes, $M_1 = 80\%$ (wb), $M_2 = 15\%$ (wb), $T_a = 30$ °C, $T_p = 60$ °C, $c_{p,solid} = 1.5$ kJ/kg·K.

**Step 7a:** Dry solid mass:

$$ m_s = 1000 \times (1 - 0.80) = 200 \text{ kg} $$

**Step 7b:** Final product mass:

$$ m_2 = \frac{1000 \times 0.20}{1 - 0.15} = \frac{200}{0.85} = 235.29 \text{ kg} $$

**Step 7c:** Water to be removed:

$$ m_w = 1000 \times 0.80 - 235.29 \times 0.15 = 800 - 35.29 = 764.71 \text{ kg} $$

**Step 7d:** Approximate product specific heat (mass fraction of water ≈ 0.80 initially):

$$ c_{p,product} \approx 1.5 \times 0.20 + 4.186 \times 0.80 = 0.30 + 3.349 = 3.649 \text{ kJ/kg·K} $$

**Step 7e:** Latent heat at 60 °C:

$$ \lambda_{60} = 2501 - 2.42 \times 60 = 2501 - 145.2 = 2355.8 \text{ kJ/kg} $$

**Step 7f:** Sensible heat:

$$ Q_{sensible} = 1000 \times 3.649 \times (60 - 30) = 1000 \times 3.649 \times 30 = 109{,}470 \text{ kJ} $$

**Step 7g:** Latent heat:

$$ Q_{latent} = 764.71 \times 2355.8 = 1{,}801{,}605 \text{ kJ} \approx 1.80 \times 10^6 \text{ kJ} $$

**Step 7h:** Total energy:

$$ Q_{total} = 109{,}470 + 1{,}801{,}605 = 1{,}911{,}075 \text{ kJ} \approx 1.91 \text{ GJ} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Latent heat dominates (~94 %) the total energy demand of drying. This is why **solar dryer design** focuses primarily on maximizing air-heating capacity to supply evaporation energy rather than sensible heating.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Instantaneous Efficiency of a Flat-Plate Solar Collector

**Problem Statement:** A flat-plate solar collector receives incident solar radiation $I_t$ (W/m²). Useful heat gain $Q_u$ (W) is measured at the outlet. Derive the instantaneous thermal efficiency and analyze its dependence on temperature.

### Step 1: Define Instantaneous Efficiency

By definition, the instantaneous thermal efficiency $\eta_c$ is the ratio of useful heat extracted to the total solar energy incident on the collector:

$$ \eta_c = \frac{Q_u}{A_c \cdot I_t} $$

### Step 2: Apply the Hottel-Whillier-Bliss Equation

For a flat-plate collector under steady-state conditions, the useful heat gain is:

$$ Q_u = A_c \left[ I_t(\tau \alpha) - U_L (T_{pm} - T_a) \right] $$

where:
- $(\tau \alpha)$ = transmittance-absorptance product (typically 0.85–0.92)
- $U_L$ = overall heat loss coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $T_{pm}$ = mean absorber plate temperature (°C)
- $T_a$ = ambient temperature (°C)

### Step 3: Substitute and Simplify

$$ \eta_c = \frac{A_c \left[ I_t(\tau \alpha) - U_L (T_{pm} - T_a) \right]}{A_c \cdot I_t} = (\tau \alpha) - \frac{U_L (T_{pm} - T_a)}{I_t} $$

Define the **reduced temperature** as:

$$ T^* = \frac{T_{pm} - T_a}{I_t} \quad \left[ \frac{K \cdot m^2}{W} \right] $$

Then:

$$ \eta_c = (\tau \alpha) - U_L \cdot T^* $$

### Step 4: Linear Efficiency Curve

This yields a linear relationship between $\eta_c$ and $T^*$, which is the standard **Hottel-Whillier-Bliss collector performance curve**:

$$ \boxed{\eta_c = a - b \cdot T^*} $$

where $a = (\tau \alpha)$ is the intercept and $b = U_L$ is the slope.

### Step 5: Numerical Example

**Given:** $\tau \alpha = 0.88$, $U_L = 5.5$ W/m²·K, $I_t = 800$ W/m², $T_{pm} = 80$ °C, $T_a = 30$ °C.

**Step 5a:** Reduced temperature:

$$ T^* = \frac{80 - 30}{800} = \frac{50}{800} = 0.0625 \text{ K·m}^2/\text{W} $$

**Step 5b:** Instantaneous efficiency:

$$ \eta_c = 0.88 - 5.5 \times 0.0625 = 0.88 - 0.344 = 0.536 = 53.6\% $$

### Step 6: Interpretation

- Higher $I_t$ (sunny conditions) → higher $\eta_c$ at same plate temperature.
- Higher $T_{pm}$ (extracting hotter water) → lower $\eta_c$ due to increased heat losses.
- Lower $U_L$ (better insulation, evacuated tubes) → higher $\eta_c$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** Examiners frequently ask students to draw the $\eta_c$ vs $T^*$ curve and identify the "stagnation point" where efficiency becomes zero (i.e., $Q_u = 0$, all absorbed energy is lost).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Sterilization Value ($F_0$)

**Problem Statement:** Derive the sterilization value $F_0$ used in thermal process design, which expresses the equivalent minutes of heating at 121.1 °C.

### Step 1: Define Thermal Death Kinetics

Microbial inactivation follows first-order kinetics:

$$ \frac{dN}{dt} = -k_d N $$

Integrating:

$$ N = N_0 \cdot e^{-k_d t} \Rightarrow \ln\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = k_d t $$

### Step 2: Define D-value and z-value

- **D-value** = time (at reference temperature $T_{ref}$) to achieve 90 % reduction in microbial count, i.e., $N/N_0 = 0.1$.
- **z-value** = temperature change required to change D-value by a factor of 10.

The temperature dependence of D-value:

$$ \frac{D_{T_2}}{D_{T_1}} = 10^{\frac{T_1 - T_2}{z}} $$

### Step 3: Define $F_0$ (Lethal Rate Concept)

The **lethal rate** $L$ at temperature $T$ is defined as:

$$ L = 10^{\frac{T - T_{ref}}{z}} $$

For sterilization, $T_{ref} = 121.1$ °C and $z = 10$ °C (for *Clostridium botulinum* spores):

$$ L = 10^{\frac{T - 121.1}{10}} $$

### Step 4: Integrate Over Process Time

The total sterilization value is the time-integral of lethal rate:

$$ F_0 = \int_0^t L \, dt = \int_0^t 10^{\frac{T(t) - 121.1}{z}} dt $$

For a constant-temperature process:

$$ \boxed{F_0 = t \cdot 10^{\frac{T - 121.1}{z}}} $$

### Step 5: Numerical Example

**Given:** A canned food is processed at $T = 130$ °C for 12 minutes. $z = 10$ °C, $T_{ref} = 121.1$ °C.

**Step 5a:** Lethal rate:

$$ L = 10^{\frac{130 - 121.1}{10}} = 10^{0.89} = 7.76 $$

**Step 5b:** $F_0$:

$$ F_0 = 12 \times 7.76 = 93.1 \text{ minutes} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Practice:** A minimum $F_0$ of 3 minutes is required for low-acid canned foods to ensure *C. botulinum* inactivation. The high $F_0$ value here (93 min) indicates a robust sterilization process.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Renewable Energy vs Food Process

| Food Process | Temperature Range (°C) | Renewable Source | System Used | Typical Efficiency | Key Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Blanching** | 85–95 | Biomass, Biogas | Direct-fired boiler, steam | 60–70 % | Localized overheating |
| **Pasteurization** | 63–75 | Solar + Biomass hybrid | Solar collector + storage | 40–55 % | Intermittency of solar |
| **Sterilization** | 115–125 | Biomass-fired boiler | High-pressure retort | 55–65 % | Strict temperature control |
| **Drying (cabinet)** | 50–80 | Indirect solar / Solar tunnel | FPC + drying chamber | 25–40 % | Weather dependency |
| **Drying (industrial)** | 100–150 | Biomass hot air generator | Heat exchanger | 50–70 % | Capital cost |
| **Cooking/Boiling** | 95–105 | Biogas burner | Direct flame | 55–65 % | Flame control |
| **Evaporation** | 60–110 | Multiple-effect + solar | Solar steam + MEE | 30–50 % | Scaling, fouling |
| **Roasting/Baking** | 150–250 | Biomass oven | Insulated chamber | 40–60 % | Hot spots, uniformity |
| **Smoking** | 60–80 | Wood smoke (biomass) | Smokehouse | 30–45 % | PAH formation control |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pattern:** Analytical comparison tables like this one are highly valued by examiners. A question asking to "compare and contrast solar drying with biomass drying" carries 7–14 marks, and this matrix provides a ready template.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"List three renewable energy sources commonly used in food processing industries and give one specific application of each."**

**Model Answer:**

The three primary renewable energy sources used in food processing are:

1. **Solar Thermal Energy** — Used in solar dryers for drying spices (cardamom, pepper, turmeric) and fruits (grapes, mango, banana). Flat-plate solar collectors heat air or water for drying chambers.

2. **Biogas** — Produced by anaerobic digestion of food waste, animal manure, and agricultural residues. Used in biogas burners for cooking, milk pasteurization, and boiling syrup in jaggery production.

3. **Biomass** — Solid fuels like rice husk, coconut shell, bagasse, and wood chips are combusted in furnaces to generate steam or hot air for parboiling rice, drying grains, and operating steam boilers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Award full 3 marks only if the student names the source, the specific food application, AND the conversion device (collector, burner, furnace).

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Define the term 'blanching' and explain its purpose in food processing."**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Blanching is a short-duration thermal pre-treatment in which food products (typically vegetables and some fruits) are immersed in hot water at **85–95 °C for 2–5 minutes** or exposed to steam for an equivalent duration.

**Purposes:**
1. **Enzyme inactivation** — Destroys heat-labile enzymes such as peroxidase, catalase, lipoxygenase, and polyphenol oxidase that would otherwise cause discoloration, off-flavor, and nutrient loss during storage.
2. **Texture modification** — Softens the tissue, facilitating packing and rehydration.
3. **Microbial load reduction** — Decreases surface microbial count before further processing.
4. **Air removal** — Expels intercellular gases, preventing oxidation and shrinkage in canned/frozen products.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse blanching with pasteurization. **Blanching** is a *pre-treatment* before drying/freezing/canning; **pasteurization** is a *preservation* treatment for liquid foods like milk and juice. Examiners deduct marks for this conflation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Choose ANY ONE — 1 × 14 Marks)

---

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**"With the help of a neat schematic, describe the construction and working principle of an indirect solar dryer. Discuss its advantages over open sun drying. Estimate the total thermal energy required to dry 500 kg of fresh mango slices from an initial moisture content of 85% (wet basis) to a final moisture content of 10% (wet basis), given that the drying temperature is 60 °C, ambient temperature is 28 °C, specific heat of mango solids is 1.6 kJ/kg·K, and latent heat of vaporization at 60 °C is 2356 kJ/kg." (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Construction, Working Principle, and Advantages (7 Marks)

**Construction of an Indirect Solar Dryer:**

An indirect solar dryer consists of two main sections:

1. **Solar Air Heater (Flat-Plate Collector):**
   - A **black-painted absorber plate** (steel or aluminum) with an integral air channel.
   - A **transparent glass/polycarbonate cover** to trap incoming solar radiation.
   - **Insulation** at the back and sides to minimize heat loss.
   - **Inlet** for ambient air at one end.

2. **Drying Chamber:**
   - A thermally insulated cabinet connected to the solar air heater via a duct.
   - **Wire-mesh trays** arranged horizontally or in a sloped configuration to hold the product.
   - **Outlet chimney/vent** for moisture-laden exhaust air.
   - Optional **fan** for forced convection.

**Working Principle:**
- Ambient air enters the solar air heater through the inlet.
- The air flows over the heated absorber plate, gaining sensible heat.
- The heated air (typically 50–80 °C above ambient) is ducted into the drying chamber.
- Hot air passes over the wet product, transferring heat to it.
- Moisture from the product evaporates into the air stream.
- The moisture-laden air exits through the chimney.

**Advantages over Open Sun Drying:**

| Parameter | Open Sun Drying | Indirect Solar Dryer |
|---|---|---|
| Drying rate | Slow (depends on weather) | 2–3× faster (forced hot air) |
| Hygiene | Exposed to dust, insects, birds | Enclosed, hygienic |
| Product quality | Uneven, discolored | Uniform color and texture |
| Losses | 20–40 % (birds, rodents, weather) | <5 % |
| Weather dependency | Total | Partial (with back-up heater) |
| Working hours | Daylight only | Extended with thermal storage |

#### Part (b) — Energy Estimation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $m_1 = 500$ kg (initial mass of mango slices)
- $M_1 = 85\% = 0.85$ (wb)
- $M_2 = 10\% = 0.10$ (wb)
- $T_p = 60$ °C (product/drying temperature)
- $T_a = 28$ °C (ambient)
- $c_{p,solid} = 1.6$ kJ/kg·K
- $\lambda_{60°C} = 2356$ kJ/kg

**Step 1: Dry solid mass**

$$ m_s = m_1 (1 - M_1) = 500 \times (1 - 0.85) = 500 \times 0.15 = 75 \text{ kg} $$

**Step 2: Final product mass**

$$ m_2 = \frac{m_1 (1 - M_1)}{(1 - M_2)} = \frac{500 \times 0.15}{1 - 0.10} = \frac{75}{0.90} = 83.33 \text{ kg} $$

**Step 3: Mass of water to be evaporated**

$$ m_w = m_1 M_1 - m_2 M_2 = 500 \times 0.85 - 83.33 \times 0.10 $$

$$ m_w = 425 - 8.333 = 416.67 \text{ kg} $$

**Step 4: Specific heat of wet product (using mass fractions)**

Mass fraction of solid initially: $x_s = 1 - M_1 = 0.15$
Mass fraction of water initially: $x_w = M_1 = 0.85$

$$ c_{p,product} = c_{p,solid} \cdot x_s + c_{p,water} \cdot x_w = 1.6 \times 0.15 + 4.186 \times 0.85 $$

$$ c_{p,product} = 0.24 + 3.558 = 3.798 \text{ kJ/kg·K} $$

**Step 5: Sensible heat required**

$$ Q_{sensible} = m_1 c_{p,product} (T_p - T_a) = 500 \times 3.798 \times (60 - 28) $$

$$ Q_{sensible} = 500 \times 3.798 \times 32 = 60{,}768 \text{ kJ} $$

**Step 6: Latent heat required**

$$ Q_{latent} = m_w \lambda = 416.67 \times 2356 = 981{,}594 \text{ kJ} $$

**Step 7: Total thermal energy**

$$ Q_{total} = Q_{sensible} + Q_{latent} = 60{,}768 + 981{,}594 = 1{,}042{,}362 \text{ kJ} \approx 1.04 \text{ GJ} $$

**Step 8: Express in kWh (optional for board presentation)**

$$ Q_{total} = \frac{1{,}042{,}362}{3600} = 289.5 \text{ kWh} $$

**Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Dry solid mass | 75 kg |
| Final product mass | 83.33 kg |
| Water removed | 416.67 kg |
| Sensible heat | 60,768 kJ |
| Latent heat | 981,594 kJ |
| **Total energy** | **1,042,362 kJ ≈ 1.04 GJ (289.5 kWh)** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse wet basis (wb) and dry basis (db) moisture.** KTU examiners frequently test this conversion. If the question gives moisture in dry basis, you must convert using $M_{wb} = \frac{M_{db}}{1 + M_{db}}$.
> 2. **Always show the dry-solid-mass equation** explicitly. Many students skip this and lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Use $\lambda$ at the appropriate temperature.** At 60 °C, $\lambda$ is ~2356 kJ/kg, NOT 2257 kJ/kg (which is at 100 °C).

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**"Explain the working of a biogas-based milk pasteurization plant with a flow diagram. Calculate the volume of biogas (at STP) required per day to pasteurize 2000 liters of milk from 4 °C to 72 °C, given that the calorific value of biogas is 22 MJ/m³, the burner efficiency is 60%, and milk specific heat is 3.93 kJ/kg·K. Assume density of milk = 1.03 kg/L." (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Biogas Pasteurization Plant (7 Marks)

**Process Flow Description:**

A biogas-based pasteurization plant consists of:

1. **Biogas Plant (Anaerobic Digester):**
   - Feed: Cattle dung, food waste, agricultural residues.
   - Retention time: 30–40 days (mesophilic) or 15–20 days (thermophilic).
   - Output: Biogas (60 % CH₄ + 40 % CO₂) + digestate slurry (fertilizer).

2. **Biogas Storage and Purification:**
   - Gas holder (floating dome or fixed-dome type).
   - H₂S removal unit (iron oxide scrubber) for corrosion protection.

3. **Burner Unit:**
   - Atmospheric or pressurized biogas burner (premixed with air).
   - Stainless-steel burner head for clean combustion.

4. **Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE):**
   - HTST (High Temperature Short Time) configuration.
   - Sections: preheating → heating → holding → regeneration → cooling.
   - Milk is heated to 72 °C and held for 15 seconds.

5. **Hot Water Circuit:**
   - Biogas burner heats water in a closed loop.
   - Hot water circulates through the PHE to heat milk indirectly (no contamination).

**Working Principle:**
- Raw milk at 4 °C enters the PHE regeneration section, where it is preheated by outgoing pasteurized milk.
- It then passes to the heating section, where biogas-fired hot water raises its temperature to 72 °C.
- The milk flows through the holding tube for 15 seconds (residence time for pasteurization).
- In the regeneration section, hot pasteurized milk transfers heat to incoming cold milk (heat recovery).
- Finally, milk is cooled to 4 °C and sent to packaging.

**Advantages:**
- Self-sufficient energy from farm waste.
- Carbon-neutral operation.
- Lower operating cost than LPG/electricity.
- Slurry by-product serves as organic fertilizer.

#### Part (b) — Biogas Volume Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Volume of milk = 2000 L/day
- Density of milk = 1.03 kg/L
- Initial milk temperature = 4 °C
- Final pasteurization temperature = 72 °C
- $\Delta T = 72 - 4 = 68$ °C
- Specific heat of milk $c_p = 3.93$ kJ/kg·K
- Calorific value of biogas (CV) = 22 MJ/m³ = 22,000 kJ/m³
- Burner efficiency $\eta_b = 60\% = 0.60$

**Step 1: Mass of milk per day**

$$ m_{milk} = V \times \rho = 2000 \times 1.03 = 2060 \text{ kg/day} $$

**Step 2: Heat energy required (sensible heating only)**

$$ Q_{required} = m_{milk} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 2060 \times 3.93 \times 68 $$

$$ Q_{required} = 2060 \times 267.24 = 550{,}514.4 \text{ kJ/day} \approx 550.5 \text{ MJ/day} $$

**Step 3: Heat energy that must be supplied by biogas (accounting for burner efficiency)**

The burner delivers only 60 % of the energy contained in biogas. Therefore, the energy content of biogas needed:

$$ Q_{biogas} = \frac{Q_{required}}{\eta_b} = \frac{550{,}514.4}{0.60} = 917{,}524 \text{ kJ/day} \approx 917.5 \text{ MJ/day} $$

**Step 4: Volume of biogas required**

$$ V_{biogas} = \frac{Q_{biogas}}{CV} = \frac{917{,}524 \text{ kJ}}{22{,}000 \text{ kJ/m}^3} = 41.71 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} $$

**Step 5: Final Answer with Units**

$$ \boxed{V_{biogas} \approx 41.7 \text{ m}^3 \text{ per day} } $$

**Summary Table:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Milk mass per day | 2060 kg |
| Sensible heat required | 550,514 kJ (550.5 MJ) |
| Energy input needed (with 60% efficiency) | 917,524 kJ (917.5 MJ) |
| Calorific value of biogas | 22,000 kJ/m³ |
| **Biogas volume required** | **≈ 41.7 m³/day** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT forget to divide by burner efficiency.** A very common mistake is calculating only $Q_{required}$ and dividing by CV. You must invert the efficiency because not all fuel energy is converted to useful heat.
> 2. **Unit consistency is critical.** KTU examiners strictly enforce kJ and m³ units. Mixing MJ and kJ within the same calculation leads to a 2-mark penalty.
> 3. **Assumed density** (1.03 kg/L for milk) must be stated explicitly in the answer; otherwise the examiner may deduct a half-mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision — Memorize These Points:**

### 🔑 Core Definitions
- **Renewable energy in food processing** = use of replenishable energy sources for heating, drying, and thermal preservation.
- **Heating** = sensible temperature rise ($Q = mc_p \Delta T$).
- **Drying** = simultaneous heat + mass transfer removing moisture.
- **Pasteurization** = mild heat (<100 °C) for pathogen destruction; HTST = 72 °C / 15 s.
- **Sterilization** = severe heat (121 °C / 15 min) for commercial sterility.
- **Blanching** = short pre-treatment (85–95 °C / 2–5 min) for enzyme inactivation.
- **Wet basis (wb) moisture** = water mass / total mass.
- **Dry basis (db) moisture** = water mass / dry solid mass.

### 🔢 Must-Know Formulas (Memorize!)
- $Q = mc_p \Delta T$ — sensible heat
- $Q = m\lambda$ — latent heat
- $\eta_c = (\tau\alpha) - U_L \cdot T^*$ — collector efficiency (Hottel-Whillier-Bliss)
- $\eta_d = \frac{m_w \lambda}{I_t \cdot A_c \cdot t}$ — dryer efficiency
- $F_0 = t \cdot 10^{(T - 121.1)/z}$ — sterilization value
- $V_{fuel} = \frac{Q_{required}}{\eta_b \cdot CV}$ — fuel volume

### 🌞 Renewable Sources & Applications
| Source | Food Process | System |
|---|---|---|
| Solar thermal | Water heating, drying, pasteurization | FPC, ETC, SAH, solar dryer |
| Biogas | Cooking, pasteurization, boiling | Anaerobic digester + burner |
| Biomass | Sterilization, parboiling, baking | Furnace, boiler, oven |
| Geothermal | Drying, space heating | Heat exchanger |
| Solar PV | Mechanical drying, cold storage | PV + electric heater |

### ⚖️ Numerical Constants (Memorize These Values)
- $c_{p,water}$ = **4.186 kJ/kg·K**
- $\lambda_{water, 100°C}$ = **2257 kJ/kg**
- Solar constant = **1367 W/m²**
- HTST pasteurization = **72 °C / 15 s**
- Sterilization = **121 °C / 15 min**
- Biogas calorific value ≈ **22 MJ/m³**
- Typical burner efficiency = **55–65 %**

### ⚠️ Common Pitfalls (Avoid These!)
1. Confusing **wet basis (wb)** vs **dry basis (db)** moisture.
2. Forgetting to **divide by burner efficiency** when calculating fuel requirement.
3. Using $\lambda$ at **100 °C** instead of at the actual drying temperature.
4. Confusing **blanching** (pre-treatment) with **pasteurization** (preservation).
5. Mixing **units** (kJ vs MJ, kg vs g) within a single numerical problem.
6. Skipping the **dry-solid-mass equation** in drying energy problems.
7. Forgetting to convert **mass fractions** correctly when computing $c_{p,product}$.

### 📚 KTU Examiner's Favorite Question Themes
- Compare open sun drying vs solar dryer (with table).
- Calculate drying energy for a specific food product.
- Biogas volume for pasteurization / cooking.
- Derive and apply the Hottel-Whillier-Bliss collector efficiency equation.
- Sketch and explain an indirect solar dryer or biogas plant.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Tip:** Always conclude numerical answers with a clear **boxed final answer** and a brief interpretive sentence (e.g., "Therefore, approximately 41.7 m³ of biogas is required per day."). KTU examiners award the concluding 0.5–1 mark for clear statement of the result.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies and Examples of Successful Implementations
Solar Photovoltaic Systems
PV Technology: Components, PV Arrays, and Grid-Tied vs Off-Grid Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT866 - APPLICATIONS OF RENEWABLE ENERGY IN FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** PV Technology: Components, PV Arrays, and Grid-Tied vs Off-Grid Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Photovoltaic (PV) Technology — Formal Definition

**Photovoltaic (PV) Technology** is the direct conversion of sunlight (photons) into electrical energy (DC electricity) using semiconductor materials that exhibit the *photovoltaic effect*. When photons with energy greater than the semiconductor bandgap strike a PV cell, electron-hole pairs are generated and separated by the built-in electric field at the p-n junction, producing a usable DC current.

In the context of **food processing applications**, PV technology is critically important because it offers:
- Decentralized, on-site power generation for cold chain storage (refrigeration, freezers)
- Solar drying of agricultural produce
- Water pumping for irrigation and post-harvest washing
- Powering sensors, IoT-based food quality monitoring
- Pasteurization, dehydration, and milling operations in rural/off-grid locations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Key Terms to Memorize**
> 1. **Photovoltaic Effect** — The physical phenomenon of photon-to-electron conversion
> 2. **PV Cell** — The basic semiconductor device (typically silicon)
> 3. **PV Module** — An encapsulated assembly of interconnected cells
> 4. **PV Array** — A combination of multiple modules wired in series/parallel
> 5. **BOS (Balance of System)** — All non-module components (inverter, mounting, wiring, protection)

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Solar Money Changer"

Imagine sunlight as a stream of foreign currency (photons). A PV cell is like a **currency exchange booth** at an airport. Each photon brings some "energy currency" to the booth (the p-n junction). If the photon carries *more energy* than the booth's "minimum transaction fee" (the **bandgap energy**, ~1.12 eV for crystalline silicon), the booth accepts it and gives you back electrical "money" (DC current + voltage). Photons that bring too little energy (below the bandgap) are simply rejected — they pass through or generate heat instead. Photons that bring too much energy don't give you extra money; the excess is lost as heat.

This is *why* silicon is the dominant material — its bandgap is perfectly tuned to the most abundant part of the solar spectrum.

### 1.3 Definition of PV Arrays

A **PV Array** is a structured assembly of two or more PV modules mechanically fastened, electrically connected, and wired to a common output bus. Arrays provide the voltage and current levels required to feed inverters, charge controllers, or DC loads.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchical Terminology (must not be confused in exams)**
> - **Cell** → smallest unit (≈ 0.5–0.6 V open-circuit)
> - **Module** → encapsulated group of cells (typically 36 or 72 cells)
> - **Panel** → colloquial term for module (often used interchangeably)
> - **Array** → multiple modules mechanically and electrically combined
> - **String** → modules connected in *series* within an array
> - **BOS** → Balance of System (everything except the modules)

### 1.4 Grid-Tied vs Off-Grid Systems — Formal Definitions

| System Type | Formal Definition |
|---|---|
| **Grid-Tied (On-Grid) PV System** | A PV system that operates in parallel with the utility grid, exporting excess generation to the grid and importing power during deficits. It **must** shut down during grid outages (anti-islanding). |
| **Off-Grid (Standalone) PV System** | A self-contained PV system that operates independently of the utility grid, relying on **battery storage** and/or backup generation to meet load demand continuously. |
| **Hybrid PV System** | A combination of grid-tied architecture with battery storage, offering both grid interaction and backup capability. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Test Conditions (STC)** used as universal benchmarks:
> - Irradiance: **G = 1000 W/m²**
> - Cell temperature: **T_cell = 25 °C**
> - Air mass: **AM 1.5**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle of a PV Cell — Layer by Layer

1. **Photon Absorption** — Sunlight strikes the semiconductor. If photon energy $E = h\nu \geq E_g$ (bandgap), it is absorbed.
2. **Electron-Hole Pair Generation** — A valence-band electron is excited to the conduction band, leaving a hole behind.
3. **Charge Separation** — The p-n junction's built-in electric field (depletion region) sweeps electrons to the n-side and holes to the p-side.
4. **Current Extraction** — Metallic contacts collect the separated charges. Connecting a load allows current to flow.
5. **Recombination Losses** — Some electron-hole pairs recombine before collection, reducing efficiency.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Shockley Diode Equation** governs the I–V behavior of a PV cell:
> $$I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{kT A}\right) - 1 \right] - I_{sh}$$
> where $I_{ph}$ = photo-generated current, $I_0$ = diode saturation current, $q$ = electron charge, $k$ = Boltzmann's constant, $T$ = absolute temperature, $A$ = diode ideality factor, and $I_{sh}$ = shunt leakage current.

### 2.2 Key Performance Parameters of a PV Cell/Module

- **Open-Circuit Voltage ($V_{oc}$)** — Terminal voltage when load is disconnected (current = 0).
- **Short-Circuit Current ($I_{sc}$)** — Current when terminals are shorted (voltage = 0).
- **Maximum Power Point (MPP)** — The unique (V, I) pair where $P = V \cdot I$ is maximized, denoted $(V_{mp}, I_{mp})$.
- **Fill Factor (FF)** — Ratio of maximum power to the product $V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$.
- **Conversion Efficiency ($\eta$)** — Ratio of electrical power output to incident solar power on the cell area.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Equation | Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Instantaneous Power Output | $P = V \cdot I$ | W (Watts) | Valid at any operating point on I–V curve |
| 2 | Maximum Power Point Power | $P_{mp} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}$ | W | Operating point where dP/dV = 0 |
| 3 | Fill Factor (FF) | $FF = \dfrac{P_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$ | Dimensionless (0 < FF < 1) | Typical range: 0.70–0.85 for good cells |
| 4 | Cell/Module Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{mp}}{G \cdot A} = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{G \cdot A}$ | % (or decimal) | $G$ = irradiance (W/m²), $A$ = module area (m²) |
| 5 | Series-Connected String Voltage | $V_{string} = N_s \cdot V_{module}$ | V | $N_s$ = number of modules in series |
| 6 | Parallel-Connected String Current | $I_{array} = N_p \cdot I_{string}$ | A | $N_p$ = number of parallel strings |
| 7 | Array Power | $P_{array} = N_s \cdot N_p \cdot P_{module}$ | W | Assumes uniform irradiance & no losses |
| 8 | Daily Energy Yield | $E_d = P_{peak} \cdot PSH \cdot \eta_{system}$ | Wh or kWh | $PSH$ = Peak Sun Hours |
| 9 | Battery Bank Capacity | $C_{bat} = \dfrac{E_{load} \cdot D_o}{V_{bat} \cdot \eta_{bat} \cdot DOD_{max}}$ | Ah | $D_o$ = autonomy days, $DOD$ = depth of discharge |
| 10 | Inverter Sizing | $P_{inv} \geq 1.25 \cdot P_{array,DC}$ | W | 25% safety margin standard |
| 11 | Charge Controller Sizing | $I_{cc} \geq 1.25 \cdot I_{sc,array}$ | A | Safety factor accounts for irradiance spikes |
| 12 | Temperature Effect on $V_{oc}$ | $V_{oc}(T) = V_{oc,STC} + \Delta T \cdot \dfrac{dV_{oc}}{dT}$ | V | Coefficient is *negative* (≈ –0.3 to –0.4 %/°C) |
| 13 | Temperature Effect on Power | $P(T) = P_{STC} \cdot \left[ 1 + \gamma_p (T_{cell} - 25) \right]$ | W | $\gamma_p$ = power temperature coefficient (≈ –0.4 to –0.5 %/°C) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Memorize the **Fill Factor** and **Efficiency** formulas verbatim. They appear in nearly every PV-related problem. Also remember: **$V_{oc}$ decreases** with temperature, but **$I_{sc}$ slightly increases** — the net effect is power *decreases* as cell temperature rises.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility in Food Processing

- **Cold Chain & Refrigeration** — Off-grid PV systems power milk chilling centers, fish cold storage, and vaccine refrigerators in remote Kerala villages.
- **Solar Drying** — PV-powered fans/blowers in tunnel dryers and cabinet dryers for turmeric, cardamom, fish, and spices.
- **Solar Water Pumping** — Direct-drive submersible pumps for irrigation, post-harvest washing, and cleaning-in-place (CIP) operations.
- **Food Processing Mills & Grinders** — Decentralized agro-processing (rice hullers, oil expellers) in tribal/rural belts.
- **IoT & Cold Chain Monitoring** — PV-powered wireless sensors (LoRa/NB-IoT) for real-time temperature/humidity tracking in transit.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Power Point (MPP) Condition

**Objective:** Find the voltage $V$ at which $P = V \cdot I$ is maximized for a PV cell.

**Starting Point — The Simplified PV Model (neglecting $R_s$ and $R_{sh}$ losses for analytical clarity):**

$$I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{kT A}\right) - 1 \right]$$

**Step 1: Express power output.**

The terminal power is:

$$P = V \cdot I = V \left\{ I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{kT A}\right) - 1 \right] \right\}$$

**Step 2: Apply the maximum power condition.**

At the maximum power point, $\dfrac{dP}{dV} = 0$.

$$\frac{dP}{dV} = I + V \cdot \frac{dI}{dV} = 0$$

**Step 3: Compute $\dfrac{dI}{dV}$ from the diode equation.**

Differentiating the current expression with respect to $V$:

$$\frac{dI}{dV} = -I_0 \cdot \frac{q}{kTA} \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{kTA}\right)$$

**Step 4: Substitute back and set to zero.**

$$I - V \cdot I_0 \cdot \frac{q}{kTA} \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{kTA}\right) = 0$$

**Step 5: Rearrange to obtain the MPP condition.**

$$\boxed{\; \frac{qV_{mp}}{kTA} + \ln\!\left( 1 + \frac{qV_{mp}}{kTA} \right) = \ln\!\left( \frac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1 \right) \;}$$

This transcendental equation has no closed-form solution and must be solved iteratively (Newton–Raphson). For practical KTU problems, however, $V_{mp}$ is given or read from the I–V curve.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: Module Efficiency & Annual Energy Yield

**Problem:** A PV module has the following specifications at STC:
- $V_{oc} = 45.0$ V
- $I_{sc} = 8.50$ A
- $V_{mp} = 36.5$ V
- $I_{mp} = 7.95$ A
- Module area $A = 1.65$ m²
- Peak Sun Hours $PSH = 5.0$ h/day (Kerala average)
- System efficiency $\eta_{sys} = 0.85$ (inverter + wiring + temperature losses)

**Find:** (a) Fill Factor, (b) Module efficiency, (c) Daily energy yield, (d) Annual energy yield.

---

**Step (a): Calculate Fill Factor (FF).**

$$P_{mp} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp} = 36.5 \times 7.95 = 290.175 \text{ W}$$

$$V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} = 45.0 \times 8.50 = 382.50 \text{ W}$$

$$FF = \frac{P_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \frac{290.175}{382.50} = 0.7587$$

$$\boxed{FF \approx 0.759 \text{ (dimensionless)}}$$

**Why this matters:** A high FF (~0.75–0.80) indicates a "square" I–V curve, meaning the cell has low series resistance and high shunt resistance — desirable quality.

---

**Step (b): Calculate Module Efficiency ($\eta$).**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{mp}}{G \cdot A} = \frac{290.175}{1000 \times 1.65}$$

$$\eta = \frac{290.175}{1650} = 0.17586$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 17.59\%}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** A modern monocrystalline silicon module with 17.59% efficiency is consistent with current commercial technology (typical range 16–22%).

---

**Step (c): Calculate Daily Energy Yield.**

$$E_d = P_{peak} \cdot PSH \cdot \eta_{sys} = 290.175 \times 5.0 \times 0.85$$

$$E_d = 290.175 \times 4.25 = 1233.24 \text{ Wh/day} = 1.233 \text{ kWh/day}$$

---

**Step (d): Calculate Annual Energy Yield.**

$$E_{annual} = E_d \times 365 = 1.233 \times 365 = 450.05 \text{ kWh/year}$$

$$\boxed{E_{annual} \approx 450 \text{ kWh/year}}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: Off-Grid Battery Sizing for a Solar Milk Chiller

**Problem:** A solar milk chiller located at a dairy cooperative in Wayanad consumes **2.5 kWh/day** continuously. Design an off-grid PV system:
- Autonomy $D_o = 2$ days (battery must last 2 cloudy days)
- System DC bus voltage $V_{bat} = 48$ V
- Battery: Lead-acid, $\eta_{bat} = 0.85$, $DOD_{max} = 0.60$
- Module $P_{module} = 290$ W (from earlier example)

**Find:** (a) Battery capacity in Ah, (b) Number of modules required (with 25% margin).

---

**Step (a): Battery Sizing.**

$$C_{bat} = \frac{E_{load} \cdot D_o}{V_{bat} \cdot \eta_{bat} \cdot DOD_{max}}$$

$$C_{bat} = \frac{2500 \times 2}{48 \times 0.85 \times 0.60} = \frac{5000}{24.48} = 204.25 \text{ Ah}$$

$$\boxed{C_{bat} \approx 205 \text{ Ah at C/20 rate}}$$

**Practical selection:** Round up to standard 220 Ah tubular lead-acid battery.

---

**Step (b): Module Count.**

Required PV power per day, accounting for PSH and system efficiency:

$$P_{required} = \frac{E_{load}}{PSH \cdot \eta_{sys}} = \frac{2500}{5.0 \times 0.85} = 588.24 \text{ W}$$

Applying 25% safety margin:

$$P_{design} = 1.25 \times 588.24 = 735.3 \text{ W}$$

Number of 290 W modules:

$$N = \left\lceil \frac{735.3}{290} \right\rceil = \lceil 2.535 \rceil = 3$$

$$\boxed{N = 3 \text{ modules (total array power} \approx 870 \text{ W)}}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** The oversized array (3 modules vs. ~2 minimum) compensates for cloudy-day deficit, dust accumulation, and seasonal irradiance variation during Kerala monsoons.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Grid-Tied vs Off-Grid Trade-off Matrix

The choice between grid-tied and off-grid systems is governed by the following decision parameters:

| Decision Parameter | Favors Grid-Tied | Favors Off-Grid |
|---|---|---|
| Proximity to grid (< 1 km) | ✓ | — |
| Reliability of grid supply | Poor grid → off-grid | Strong grid → grid-tied |
| Need for backup during outage | — (no inherent backup) | ✓ (with battery) |
| Net-metering policy available | ✓ | Not required |
| Net cost of electricity from grid | High (₹>8/kWh) | Low (< ₹5/kWh) |
| Load profile (24/7 critical) | — | ✓ |
| Initial capital budget | Lower | Higher (battery cost) |
| Payback period target | < 5 years | > 7 years acceptable |

$$\text{Payback Period} = \frac{\text{Total System Cost (₹)}}{\text{Annual Savings (₹/year)}}$$

**Food processing applications specifically:**
- **Dairy cold chain in rural Kerala** → Off-grid (grid unreliable, backup essential)
- **Solar drying unit near farm** → Off-grid
- **Industrial spice processing near grid** → Grid-tied (cost-effective)
- **Export-quality seafood cold storage** → Hybrid (premium reliability required)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 PV System Hierarchy — Cell → Module → Array

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["PV CELL 0.5 to 0.6 V 1 to 4 Wp"] --> A2["PV MODULE 36 or 72 cells 28 to 50 V 100 to 400 Wp"]
    A2 --> A3["PV STRING modules in series High voltage 600 to 1500 V DC"]
    A3 --> A4["PV ARRAY strings in parallel High current 1 to 10 kWp or more"]
    A4 --> A5["BOS Balance of System inverter mounting wiring protection"]
    A5 --> A6["FINAL SYSTEM AC or DC load for food processing"]
```

### 4.2 Off-Grid PV System Architecture (Standalone)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S["SOLAR ARRAY PV modules 1 to 10 kWp"] --> CC["CHARGE CONTROLLER MPPT or PWM 48 V DC"]
    CC --> BAT["BATTERY BANK Lead-acid or Lithium 48 V 200 to 1000 Ah"]
    BAT --> INV["INVERTER Pure sine wave 48 V DC to 230 V AC"]
    INV --> LOAD1["FOOD PROCESSING LOAD refrigerator chiller dryer"]
    INV --> LOAD2["ANCILLARY LOAD lighting fan pump"]
    CC -.-> DUMP["DUMMY LOAD diversion controller optional"]
    BAT -.-> BMS["BATTERY MANAGEMENT SYSTEM BMS protection"]
```

### 4.3 Grid-Tied PV System Architecture (On-Grid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S["SOLAR ARRAY PV modules string 1 to 10 kWp"] --> DC["DC COMBINER BOX fuses SPD DC isolator"]
    DC --> INV["GRID-TIE INVERTER MPPT DC to AC 230 V 50 Hz"]
    INV --> AC["AC DISTRIBUTION PANEL MCB RCCB metering"]
    AC --> LOAD["FOOD PROCESSING LOAD daytime consumption"]
    AC --> M["NET METER bidirectional import export kWh"]
    M --> GRID["UTILITY GRID Kerala State Electricity Board"]
    INV -.-> ISL["ANTI-ISLANDING PROTECTION grid outage shutdown"]
```

### 4.4 Grid-Tied vs Off-Grid — Comparative Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph GT["GRID-TIED SYSTEM"]
        G1["PV Array"] --> G2["DC Isolator"] --> G3["Inverter no battery"] --> G4["AC Load"]
        G3 -.-> G5["Grid Export Import"]
    end
    subgraph OF["OFF-GRID SYSTEM"]
        O1["PV Array"] --> O2["Charge Controller"] --> O3["Battery Bank"] --> O4["Inverter"] --> O5["AC Load"]
        O3 -.-> O6["Generator backup optional"]
    end
    subgraph HY["HYBRID SYSTEM"]
        H1["PV Array"] --> H2["Hybrid Inverter with MPPT"] --> H3["AC Load"]
        H2 -.-> H4["Battery Bank"]
        H2 -.-> H5["Grid with net metering"]
    end
```

### 4.5 Component-Level Functional Map — BOS for a Food Processing PV Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["PV MODULES crystalline silicon 290 Wp each"] --> B["MODULE LEVEL DC WIRING MC4 connectors 4 mm²"]
    B --> C["STRING COMBINER BOX fuses 15 A string diodes"]
    C --> D["DC SURGE PROTECTION DEVICE SPD Type II"]
    D --> E["INVERTER 3 phase 10 kW MPPT efficiency 98 percent"]
    E --> F["AC ISOLATOR manual switch"]
    F --> G["AC DISTRIBUTION BOARD MCBs RCCBs earth fault protection"]
    G --> H["NET METER bidirectional"]
    H --> I["LOAD Cold storage 5 kW Solar dryer 3 kW Lighting 1 kW"]
    J["MOUNTING STRUCTURE hot dip galvanized tilt 13 degrees Kerala latitude"] -.supports.-> A
    K["LIGHTNING ARRESTER class II"] -.protects.-> E
    L["EARTHING pit resistance less than 5 ohm"] -.grounds.-> G
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the following terms with one-line significance: (a) Photovoltaic effect, (b) Fill Factor, (c) Peak Sun Hours (PSH).**

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Photovoltaic Effect** — The physical phenomenon in which light photons incident on a semiconductor p-n junction generate electron-hole pairs, which are separated by the built-in electric field to produce a DC voltage and current. *Significance:* Forms the fundamental operating principle of all solar PV cells.

(b) **Fill Factor (FF)** — The ratio of the maximum obtainable power ($V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}$) to the product of open-circuit voltage and short-circuit current ($V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$). *Significance:* A direct measure of the "squareness" of the I–V curve and the quality of the cell.

(c) **Peak Sun Hours (PSH)** — The equivalent number of hours per day during which solar irradiance averages **1000 W/m²** such that the total daily insolation equals $G_{daily} = PSH \times 1000$ Wh/m². *Significance:* Used as a standard unit to estimate daily PV energy yield. For Kerala, typical annual average PSH ≈ 4.5–5.0 h.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any six components of a typical grid-tied PV system's Balance of System (BOS).**

**Model Answer:**

The six key BOS components of a grid-tied PV system are:

1. **Grid-Tie Inverter** — Converts DC to 230 V AC synchronized with the grid
2. **DC Combiner Box** — Combines parallel strings with fuses and DC isolators
3. **AC Distribution Panel** — Houses MCBs, RCCBs, and metering
4. **Net Meter** — Bidirectional meter for grid export-import measurement
5. **Mounting Structure** — Galvanized frames holding modules at fixed tilt (~13° for Kerala)
6. **Surge Protection Devices (SPDs)** & Earthing — Protects against lightning and over-voltage faults

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

**QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an **off-grid (standalone) PV system** for a rural food processing unit. Identify and explain the function of each component.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 290 W PV module has $V_{oc} = 45.0$ V, $I_{sc} = 8.50$ A, $V_{mp} = 36.5$ V, $I_{mp} = 7.95$ A, and area $A = 1.65$ m². Calculate (i) Fill Factor, (ii) Module efficiency at STC, and (iii) daily energy output assuming PSH = 5 h/day and overall system efficiency = 85%.

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

An **off-grid PV system** is self-contained and does not depend on the utility grid. It must have sufficient generation, storage, and load management to operate 24/7.

**Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram for graphical representation).**

**Component Functions:**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **PV Array** | Converts sunlight to DC electricity; sized based on daily load |
| **Charge Controller (MPPT/PWM)** | Regulates charging current/voltage to battery; prevents overcharge; MPPT extracts up to 30% more energy than PWM |
| **Battery Bank** | Stores excess energy for night/cloudy-day use; sized for required autonomy (typically 1–3 days) |
| **Inverter** | Converts 48 V DC (or 12/24 V) battery output to 230 V AC, 50 Hz for AC loads |
| **Distribution Panel & Protection** | MCBs, RCCBs, fuses, and earthing for operator safety and equipment protection |
| **Backup Generator (optional)** | Diesel/biogas genset for extended cloudy periods |
| **Monitoring System** | Tracks battery SOC, PV voltage, load consumption |

**Why off-grid for food processing?** Rural food processing units (dairy chilling, drying, milling) often face unreliable grid supply. Battery-backed off-grid systems ensure **24/7 operation** critical for cold chain and hygienic processing.

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**Step (i): Fill Factor**

$$P_{mp} = V_{mp} \times I_{mp} = 36.5 \times 7.95 = 290.175 \text{ W}$$

$$FF = \frac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \frac{290.175}{45.0 \times 8.50} = \frac{290.175}{382.50}$$

$$\boxed{FF = 0.759}$$

**Step (ii): Module Efficiency**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{mp}}{G \cdot A} = \frac{290.175}{1000 \times 1.65} = \frac{290.175}{1650}$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 17.59\%}$$

**Step (iii): Daily Energy Output**

$$E_d = P_{module} \times PSH \times \eta_{sys} = 290 \times 5.0 \times 0.85$$

$$E_d = 290 \times 4.25 = 1232.5 \text{ Wh/day}$$

$$\boxed{E_d \approx 1.23 \text{ kWh/day per module}}$$

---

**QUESTION B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model Paper — Internal Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **grid-tied** and **off-grid** PV systems in terms of at least **eight** parameters. State the role of **net metering** in grid-tied systems.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A solar milk-chilling plant in Palakkad requires **3.0 kWh/day** for 24/7 operation. Design the off-grid system:
- Module rating: 250 W, $V_{mp}$ = 30 V
- Battery: Lead-acid, $V_{bat}$ = 48 V, $\eta_{bat}$ = 0.85, $DOD$ = 60%
- Autonomy: 2 days
- PSH = 5 h, $\eta_{sys}$ = 0.85

Calculate (i) battery capacity in Ah, (ii) number of modules required, (iii) suggest inverter rating with 25% margin.

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Grid-Tied System | Off-Grid System |
|---|---|---|
| Grid Connection | Mandatory (parallel operation) | Not required |
| Battery Storage | Not required (in basic version) | Essential |
| Cost | Lower initial | Higher (battery cost) |
| Anti-Islanding | Mandatory (safety) | Not applicable |
| Net Metering | Yes (export excess to grid) | Not applicable |
| Backup During Outage | **No** (shuts down per regulation) | **Yes** (battery powers load) |
| Efficiency | Higher (no battery round-trip loss) | Lower (battery η ≈ 80–90%) |
| Maintenance | Low (no battery care) | Moderate (battery replacement every 3–5 yr) |
| Typical Payback | 3–6 years | 5–10 years |
| Best Suited for | Urban/semi-urban with reliable grid | Remote/rural/unreliable-grid areas |

**Role of Net Metering:**
- A **bidirectional** electricity meter records (a) energy imported from the grid and (b) energy exported to the grid separately.
- At the end of the billing cycle, the **net exported kWh** is **credited** to the consumer's account (at an agreed feed-in tariff or through net-off billing).
- In Kerala, the **KSEB (Kerala State Electricity Board)** allows net metering under the **Kerala State Electricity Regulatory Commission (KSERC)** regulations for systems up to 1 MWp.
- This dramatically improves the **economics of grid-tied PV**, often achieving bill neutrality for daytime loads.

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**Step (i): Battery Capacity**

$$C_{bat} = \frac{E_{load} \cdot D_o}{V_{bat} \cdot \eta_{bat} \cdot DOD} = \frac{3000 \times 2}{48 \times 0.85 \times 0.60}$$

$$C_{bat} = \frac{6000}{24.48} = 245.10 \text{ Ah}$$

$$\boxed{C_{bat} \approx 246 \text{ Ah (round up to 250 Ah standard rating)}}$$

**Step (ii): Number of Modules**

Required PV array power:

$$P_{required} = \frac{E_{load}}{PSH \cdot \eta_{sys}} = \frac{3000}{5 \times 0.85} = \frac{3000}{4.25} = 705.88 \text{ W}$$

Applying 25% design margin:

$$P_{design} = 1.25 \times 705.88 = 882.35 \text{ W}$$

$$N = \left\lceil \frac{P_{design}}{P_{module}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{882.35}{250} \right\rceil = \lceil 3.53 \rceil$$

$$\boxed{N = 4 \text{ modules (total array} = 1000 \text{ W)}}$$

**Step (iii): Inverter Rating**

$$P_{inv} = 1.25 \times P_{array,DC} = 1.25 \times 1000 = 1250 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Inverter rating} \approx 1.25 \text{ kW (prefer 1.5 kW standard)}}$$

**Practical note on module configuration:**
- Each module $V_{mp} = 30$ V
- To achieve 48 V battery bus under charging, modules are typically connected in **2-series × 2-parallel** configuration (2S2P), giving string voltage ~60 V (well above 48 V battery to allow charge controller regulation).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the system efficiency term (η_sys) in energy yield problems** — a 4–5 mark penalty is common. Always state η_sys = 0.80–0.90 explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing "module" with "array"** in numerical answers — Module is one unit, array is the whole set. Examiners deduct marks for this terminology slip.
> 3. **Ignoring Depth of Discharge (DOD) in battery sizing** — never use full battery capacity; apply DOD ≤ 0.80 for lead-acid.
> 4. **Using $V_{oc}$ instead of $V_{mp}$ in efficiency calculation** — efficiency must use $P_{mp} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}$, not $V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$.
> 5. **Forgetting to apply the 25% safety margin** for inverter and charge controller sizing.
> 6. **Anti-islanding not mentioned** in grid-tied system answers — this is a compulsory safety point worth 2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision — Must Memorize Before Exam**

- ☐ **Photovoltaic effect** = photon + p-n junction → DC electricity. Bandgap $E_g$ is the threshold photon energy.
- ☐ **Hierarchy:** Cell → Module (28–50 V) → String (series) → Array (series × parallel).
- ☐ **STC:** 1000 W/m², 25°C, AM 1.5. Always quote this when stating module ratings.
- ☐ **$V_{oc}$** decreases with temperature; **$I_{sc}$** slightly increases. Net result: **Power decreases** as temperature rises (~–0.4 %/°C).
- ☐ **Fill Factor** = $V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp} / (V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc})$, typical 0.70–0.85. Higher = better cell quality.
- ☐ **Efficiency** = $P_{mp} / (G \cdot A)$. Always use $P_{mp}$, not $P_{rated}$.
- ☐ **Series connection** → adds voltage; **Parallel connection** → adds current.
- ☐ **Daily energy** = $P_{peak} \times PSH \times \eta_{sys}$. Kerala PSH ≈ 4.5–5.0 h.
- ☐ **Battery sizing:** $C_{bat} = (E_{load} \cdot D_o) / (V_{bat} \cdot \eta_{bat} \cdot DOD)$. Typical lead-acid: DOD ≤ 0.6–0.8.
- ☐ **Inverter sizing:** $P_{inv} \geq 1.25 \times P_{DC,array}$. Charge controller: $I_{cc} \geq 1.25 \times I_{sc,array}$.
- ☐ **Grid-tied** = no battery, **must** have anti-islanding, **net metering** enables export credits.
- ☐ **Off-grid** = battery mandatory, **24/7 operation** possible, higher initial cost, suitable for rural food processing.
- ☐ **Hybrid** = best of both — battery backup + grid export. Increasingly preferred for commercial food plants.
- ☐ **Food processing applications:** Cold storage, solar drying, water pumping, pasteurization, milling, IoT monitoring.
- ☐ **Always include η_sys ≈ 0.80–0.90** in yield calculations to account for inverter, wiring, temperature, and dust losses.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Integration with Food Processing Facilities: Electricity Generation and Energy Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT866 - APPLICATIONS OF RENEWABLE ENERGY IN FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Integration with Food Processing Facilities: Electricity Generation and Energy Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Integration with Food Processing Facilities** is defined as the systematic design, interconnection, and operational coordination of renewable energy conversion systems (solar PV, biomass gasifiers, wind turbines, geothermal heat pumps, solar thermal collectors) with the electrical, thermal, and process energy demands of food processing plants such that net energy input from conventional grid/fossil sources is minimized while maintaining product quality, throughput, and food safety standards.

The integration is **bidirectional** in modern food-industry parlance:
- **Power Side Integration** — electricity generated from renewables (PV arrays, biomass cogeneration, small wind) feeding the plant's switchgear/MCC.
- **Process Heat Integration** — thermal energy from solar thermal, biogas combustors, geothermal exchangers supplying boilers, dryers, pasteurizers, and steam cookers.

> [!NOTE]
> The governing technical concept is **"Energy Vector Coupling"** — recognizing that food processing has a unique signature: simultaneous demand for **low-grade heat (40–90 °C)**, **medium-grade heat (90–250 °C for sterilization)**, **mechanical shaft power (chopping, milling, pumping)**, and **electrical power (refrigeration, controls, lighting, drives)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Multi-Panel Restaurant Kitchen"

Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen. A chef needs **gas (steam)**, **electricity (mixers, ovens, refrigerators)**, and **hot water (washing)** all at the same time. Now imagine installing **solar panels on the roof** (electricity for mixers and fridges), a **biogas plant in the backyard** from food waste (gas for the burners and steam), and a **solar water heater** (hot water for cleaning). This is *exactly* what integration in a food processing facility means — every renewable source is matched to a specific "appliance" inside the food factory, so the kitchen runs on **homegrown, sustainable energy** instead of only LPG and grid electricity.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following baseline constants and metrics are universally used in KTU examinations:

| Metric / Constant | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Standard solar insolation (peak) | $G_{STC}$ | **1000 W/m²** |
| PV cell reference temperature | $T_{STC}$ | **25 °C (298.15 K)** |
| Air specific heat (energy management) | $c_{p,air}$ | **1.005 kJ/kg·K** |
| Water specific heat (thermal loads) | $c_{p,water}$ | **4.186 kJ/kg·K** |
| Lower calorific value of biogas | $LCV_{biogas}$ | **18–25 MJ/m³** |
| Net calorific value of rice husk | $NCV_{husk}$ | **12–14 MJ/kg** |
| Power factor reference (industrial) | $pf$ | **0.85–0.95 lagging** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Food processing facilities are classified as **"Class B Industrial Consumers"** under KSEB tariff schedules, and typically operate at a power factor mandate of **≥ 0.90** to avoid penalties.

### 1.4 KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping

This topic directly addresses:
- **CO2:** Design and analyze renewable energy systems suited to food processing operations.
- **CO3:** Evaluate energy management strategies in food industries using renewables.
- **Bloom's Levels:** Apply (L3), Analyze (L4), Evaluate (L5).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Pillars of Integration

#### Pillar A — Electricity Generation Pathway
Renewable → DC/AC conversion → Step-up/down transformers → Plant Switchgear → Loads

The electrical integration involves:
1. **DC Bus Architecture** (for PV + Battery): PV array → MPPT charge controller → DC bus → Bidirectional inverter → AC bus.
2. **AC Bus Architecture** (for biomass genset + wind): Prime mover → Synchronous/Asynchronous generator → Protection panel → AC bus.
3. **Grid-Interactive Mode** (Net metering): When generation > load, excess is exported; when load > generation, deficit is imported.

#### Pillar B — Energy Management Pathway
The **Energy Management System (EMS)** is the "brain" that continuously balances:
- **Load-side variables:** Real-time plant demand $P_{load}(t)$, reactive demand $Q_{load}(t)$, steam demand $M_{steam}(t)$.
- **Supply-side variables:** PV output $P_{PV}(t)$, biomass gasifier output $P_{bio}(t)$, grid import $P_{grid}(t)$, battery state-of-charge $SOC(t)$.

### 2.2 Why This Topic Demands Rigorous Analysis (The "Why" Behind Each Step)

| Step | Why It Matters in Food Processing |
|---|---|
| Load profiling | Food plants have **seasonal peaks** (e.g., paddy season for rice mills, sugarcane season for sugar mills) — RE systems must be sized for these. |
| Power quality control | **VFDs, refrigeration compressors, and motor starters** create harmonics; RE inverters must comply with **IEEE 519**. |
| Steam-thermal coupling | A dairy needs 4–6 kg steam per kg of milk processed; biogas is the natural fit. |
| Cold chain linkage | Solar PV → refrigeration → cold storage forms a **direct thermal-electrical coupling** critical for fish, dairy, and horticultural processing. |
| Net metering | Surplus daytime PV in a flour mill can offset night-time grinding loads. |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the high-yield formula bank for Module 2 questions.**

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{PV} = \eta_{PV} \cdot A_c \cdot G \cdot (1 - \beta (T_c - T_{STC}))$ | PV array power output (with temperature derating) | W |
| 2 | $E_{PV,annual} = P_{PV,peak} \cdot PSH \cdot 365$ | Annual PV energy yield | kWh/yr |
| 3 | $P_{biogas,elec} = \dfrac{\eta_{gen} \cdot Q_{biogas} \cdot LCV_{biogas}}{3.6}$ | Biomass/biogas electric power | kW |
| 4 | $Q_{biogas,daily} = \dfrac{M_{waste} \cdot Y_s \cdot V_{CH_4}}{f_{CH_4}}$ | Daily biogas production from waste | m³/day |
| 5 | $\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{E_{useful}}{E_{input,primary}}$ | System overall efficiency | dimensionless |
| 6 | $P_{grid,import} = P_{load} - P_{PV} - P_{bio} - P_{bat,disch}$ | Power balance equation (positive = import) | kW |
| 7 | $SOC(t) = SOC(t-1) + \dfrac{\eta_{ch} \cdot P_{ch} \cdot \Delta t}{C_{bat}} - \dfrac{P_{disch} \cdot \Delta t}{\eta_{disch} \cdot C_{bat}}$ | Battery state of charge | % |
| 8 | $C_{d} = 0.8 \cdot C_{tot}$ (diesel); $C_{f} = (1 - f_{d})$ (diesel displacement) | Diesel displacement fraction in hybrid systems | dimensionless |
| 9 | $E_{saved} = E_{RE,used} \cdot C_{grid} \cdot (1 + \tau_{esc})^n$ | Lifecycle cost savings with energy escalation | ₹ or $ |
| 10 | $LCOE = \dfrac{\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}}{\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{E_t}{(1+r)^t}}$ | Levelized cost of energy | ₹/kWh |
| 11 | $CF = \dfrac{E_{annual}}{P_{rated} \cdot 8760}$ | Capacity factor of RE plant | dimensionless |
| 12 | $P_{demand} = \sum P_i \cdot (1 + d_m + d_s)$ | Maximum diversified demand with diversity | kW |
| 13 | $PF = \cos \phi = \dfrac{P_{real}}{\sqrt{P_{real}^2 + Q_{reactive}^2}}$ | Power factor (critical for KSEB penalty) | dimensionless |
| 14 | $\eta_{CHP} = \dfrac{P_{elec} + Q_{th}}{Q_{fuel,in}}$ | Combined Heat and Power efficiency | dimensionless |
| 15 | $M_{steam} = \dfrac{Q_{process}}{h_g - h_f}$ | Steam mass flow from heat demand | kg/h |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Dairy Industry**: India operates ~1,000 dairy processing plants. The **AMUL model** integrates biogas from cattle dung with chilling centers, demonstrating ~30–40% energy self-sufficiency.
- **Rice Mills**: The combination of **rice husk gasification + solar PV + dryer integration** is a textbook KTU case study.
- **Sugar Mills**: **Bagasse cogeneration** is one of the largest industrial RE applications in the world.
- **Spice/Coffee Processing**: Solar thermal dryers in Kerala and Karnataka (Coorg coffee) represent clean integration of solar heat with processing.

> [!NOTE]
> According to the Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) framework, food processing units with RE integration qualify for **CFA (Central Financial Assistance)** of 30–50% of project cost under the **Cogeneration Program** and **Solar Power for Industries**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Power Balance Equation for an Integrated Food Plant

**Statement of Principle:** In any instant, the algebraic sum of all power entering and leaving the AC bus must equal zero (Kirchhoff's Power Law applied to industrial busbar).

$$
P_{PV} + P_{bio} + P_{wind} + P_{bat,disch} + P_{grid,import} = P_{load} + P_{bat,ch} + P_{grid,export}
$$

$$
P_{grid,import} = P_{load} + P_{bat,ch} + P_{grid,export} - P_{PV} - P_{bio} - P_{wind} - P_{bat,disch}
$$

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

Step 1 — Identify the AC bus as the reference node where all generation and consumption meet. The KTU treatment assumes the AC bus is at a fixed voltage of 415 V (3-phase) or 240 V (single-phase).

Step 2 — Sum all **power sources** (incoming terms). PV delivers $P_{PV}$ via inverter; biomass gasifier via synchronous generator; wind via doubly-fed induction generator; battery via bidirectional inverter (during discharge); grid via import feeder.

Step 3 — Sum all **power sinks** (outgoing terms). Plant load consumes $P_{load}$; battery consumes $P_{bat,ch}$ (when charging); grid receives $P_{grid,export}$ (when exporting under net metering).

Step 4 — Apply conservation. The two sums must be equal at every time instant. Rearranging isolates the grid import, which is the variable the plant operator wants to **minimize** to reduce the KSEB bill.

Step 5 — Introduce sign convention. Let $P_{bat,disch}$ be positive during discharge, and $P_{bat,ch}$ positive only when battery is absorbing energy. The equation now governs the **Energy Management System (EMS)** logic in real time.

**Numerical Illustration (typical KTU problem):**

Given:
- $P_{PV} = 250$ kW; $P_{bio} = 100$ kW; $P_{wind} = 50$ kW
- $P_{load} = 600$ kW; $P_{bat,ch} = 30$ kW (battery charging)
- $P_{bat,disch} = 0$ (battery idle)
- $P_{grid,export} = 0$ (no export allowed in this case)

Compute $P_{grid,import}$:

$$
P_{grid,import} = 600 + 30 + 0 - 250 - 100 - 50 - 0
$$

$$
P_{grid,import} = 630 - 400 = 230 \text{ kW}
$$

**Interpretation:** The plant self-generates 400 kW but still needs to import 230 kW from the grid. The RE penetration fraction is:

$$
f_{RE} = \frac{P_{PV} + P_{bio} + P_{wind}}{P_{load}} = \frac{400}{600} = 0.6667 = 66.67\%
$$

This is a strong candidate **KTU 14-mark problem**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Annual Energy Yield of an Integrated Solar-Biomass-Diesel Hybrid System

**Objective:** Derive the total annual electrical energy generated by a hybrid RE-Diesel system for a typical food processing facility (e.g., a rice mill or dairy plant).

**Step 1 — Solar PV contribution:**

$$
E_{PV,annual} = P_{PV,peak} \cdot PSH \cdot 365
$$

where $PSH$ = Peak Sun Hours (Kerala average ≈ 4.5–5.0 h/day).

**Step 2 — Biomass gasifier contribution (with derating):**

$$
E_{bio,annual} = P_{bio,r} \cdot CF_{bio} \cdot 8760
$$

where $CF_{bio}$ = capacity factor of biomass plant (typically 0.70–0.80 because feedstock is seasonal).

**Step 3 — Diesel generator contribution (only for peak deficit):**

$$
E_{diesel,annual} = P_{diesel,r} \cdot H_{operation} \cdot CF_{diesel}
$$

where $H_{operation}$ is the actual running hours per year and $CF_{diesel} \approx 0.85$ (derated for partial load).

**Step 4 — Total annual generation:**

$$
E_{total,annual} = E_{PV,annual} + E_{bio,annual} + E_{diesel,annual}
$$

**Step 5 — Diesel displacement factor (key metric for food plant sustainability):**

$$
f_{diesel,displaced} = 1 - \frac{E_{diesel,annual}}{E_{diesel,business-as-usual}}
$$

**Numerical Illustration:**

Given:
- $P_{PV,peak} = 200$ kW, $PSH = 4.8$ h/day
- $P_{bio,r} = 150$ kW, $CF_{bio} = 0.75$
- $P_{diesel,r} = 250$ kW, $H_{operation} = 2000$ h/yr, $CF_{diesel} = 0.85$

Compute:

$$
E_{PV,annual} = 200 \cdot 4.8 \cdot 365 = 350{,}400 \text{ kWh/yr}
$$

$$
E_{bio,annual} = 150 \cdot 0.75 \cdot 8760 = 985{,}500 \text{ kWh/yr}
$$

$$
E_{diesel,annual} = 250 \cdot 0.85 \cdot 2000 = 425{,}000 \text{ kWh/yr}
$$

$$
E_{total,annual} = 350{,}400 + 985{,}500 + 425{,}000 = 1{,}760{,}900 \text{ kWh/yr}
$$

If the business-as-usual scenario (diesel only) would have required $E_{diesel,bau} = 1{,}900{,}000$ kWh/yr, then:

$$
f_{diesel,displaced} = 1 - \frac{425{,}000}{1{,}900{,}000} = 0.776 = 77.6\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This 77.6% diesel displacement qualifies the food plant for the **MNRE Category-I RE Industrial incentive** under Indian renewable policy.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE) for RE Integration

The LCOE is the most defensible economic metric a KTU examiner uses to compare a renewable-integrated food plant against a grid-only plant.

**Derivation from First Principles:**

Step 1 — Define the present value of all costs over project life $n$:

$$
PV_{cost} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}
$$

where $C_t$ is the year-$t$ total cost (capital + O&M + fuel), and $r$ is the discount rate.

Step 2 — Define the present value of all energy produced:

$$
PV_{energy} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{E_t}{(1+r)^t}
$$

Step 3 — Form the ratio:

$$
LCOE = \frac{PV_{cost}}{PV_{energy}}
$$

Step 4 — For uniform $C$ and $E$ across years, simplify to:

$$
LCOE = \frac{C}{E} \cdot \frac{r(1+r)^n}{(1+r)^n - 1}
$$

**Numerical Illustration:**

Given:
- Initial cost $C_0$ = ₹50,000,000 (5 crore for a 500 kW hybrid system)
- Annual O&M = ₹1,000,000 (escalating at 3%)
- Discount rate $r$ = 8%
- Project life $n$ = 25 years
- Annual energy $E$ = 1,500,000 kWh/yr (assuming mild degradation in PV)

Compute capital recovery factor (CRF):

$$
CRF = \frac{0.08 \cdot (1.08)^{25}}{(1.08)^{25} - 1} = \frac{0.08 \cdot 6.848}{6.848 - 1} = \frac{0.5478}{5.848} = 0.0937
$$

Compute annualized cost:

$$
C_{annual} = 50{,}000{,}000 \cdot 0.0937 + 1{,}000{,}000 = 4{,}685{,}000 + 1{,}000{,}000 = ₹5{,}685{,}000/\text{yr}
$$

Compute LCOE:

$$
LCOE = \frac{5{,}685{,}000}{1{,}500{,}000} = ₹3.79/\text{kWh}
$$

**Comparison with KSEB industrial tariff (~₹7.50/kWh in 2024):**

$$
\text{Savings ratio} = \frac{7.50 - 3.79}{7.50} = 0.495 = 49.5\%
$$

The food plant saves ~50% on electricity cost — a direct boost to **margin and competitiveness**.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Energy Management Strategy (Load Following)

In an integrated plant, the EMS typically employs **load-following strategy** rather than **cycle-charging** because food processing has continuous critical loads (refrigeration, conveyors).

**The Load-Following Rule:**

$$
P_{RE,total}(t) \geq P_{critical,load}(t) \Rightarrow \text{Deficit covered by grid/diesel}
$$

$$
P_{RE,total}(t) > P_{critical,load}(t) \Rightarrow \text{Surplus charges battery or exports}
$$

Define a **net load**:

$$
P_{net}(t) = P_{load}(t) - P_{PV}(t) - P_{bio}(t)
$$

If $P_{net}(t) > 0$: the grid/diesel must supply $P_{net}(t)$ or battery discharges.
If $P_{net}(t) < 0$: surplus exists — charge battery or export.

This logic is implemented in real industrial EMS through **Programmable Logic Controllers (PLC)** and **SCADA systems**.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently tests the **dispatch priority** in numerical problems. The standard industrial priority is: **PV → Wind → Biomass → Battery → Grid → Diesel**, with battery as a transient buffer only.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Key Performance Indicators for Integrated Food Plants

| KPI | Formula | Target | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| RE Penetration | $f_{RE} = E_{RE}/E_{total}$ | ≥ 50% | Indicates sustainability |
| Capacity Factor | $CF = E_{annual}/(P_r \cdot 8760)$ | 0.20–0.80 | Indicates asset utilization |
| Diesel Displacement | $f_d$ | ≥ 70% | CO₂ reduction indicator |
| Energy Self-Sufficiency | $ESS = 1 - E_{grid}/E_{load}$ | ≥ 60% | Resilience indicator |
| LCOE | $LCOE$ | < grid tariff | Economic viability |
| Payback Period | $PB = C_0/\text{Annual Savings}$ | < 7 years | Investment decision |
| Carbon Avoided | $CO_2 = E_{RE} \cdot EF_{grid}$ | — | Sustainability metric |

where $EF_{grid} \approx 0.82$ kg CO₂/kWh for the Indian grid emission factor.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

### 4.2 Energy Management Strategy Flow — Decision Logic Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ST1([Start Every 1 min Loop]) --> A1[Read P_PV from PV Inverter]
    A1 --> A2[Read P_bio from Gasifier]
    A2 --> A3[Read P_wind from Wind Inverter]
    A3 --> A4[Read P_load from Plant Bus]
    A4 --> A5[Read SOC from Battery BMS]
    A5 --> B1[Compute P_RE_total]
    B1 --> B2[Compute P_net equals P_load minus P_RE_total]
    B2 --> C1{P_net greater than 0}
    C1 -->|Yes| C2{SOC greater than 30 percent}
    C2 -->|Yes| C3[Discharge Battery at min P_net eta_bat]
    C2 -->|No| C4[Import from Grid at P_net]
    C1 -->|No| D1[Surplus P_neg]
    D1 --> D2{SOC less than 95 percent}
    D2 -->|Yes| D3[Charge Battery with Surplus]
    D2 -->|No| D4[Check Net Meter Export Allowed]
    D4 -->|Yes| D5[Export Surplus to Grid]
    D4 -->|No| D6[Reduce RE Output Curtailment]
    C3 --> E1[Log Data to SCADA]
    C4 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    D5 --> E1
    D6 --> E1
    E1 --> ST1
```
