#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Analyse the case of **"Synthite Industrial Chemicals, Kerala"** (or any well-known Indian spice-processing unit) — describe how they integrated solar thermal drying and biomass boiler systems using ANERT and MNRE incentives. What were the key financial and environmental outcomes? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Critically evaluate the **challenges and limitations** faced by food processing industries in India when trying to access and stack multiple renewable energy incentives. Suggest three policy reforms to improve access. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (Outline):**

**(a) — Synthite Case (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Company** | Synthite Industrial Chemicals, Kadayiruppu, Ernakulam, Kerala |
| **Sector** | Spice oleoresin extraction (value-added spice processing) |
| **RES Deployed** | 1 MW solar PV (rooftop + ground) + 8 TPH biomass boiler (rice husk + wood chips) |
| **Investment** | ~₹8.5 crore |
| **Incentives Used** | (1) MNRE 30% subsidy on biomass boiler — ₹1.5 crore; (2) Kerala ANERT 25% subsidy on solar PV — ₹0.5 crore; (3) KSEB Net-Metering; (4) Section 80-IA tax holiday; (5) REC trading on PXIL |
| **Financial Outcome** | Payback reduced from 6.5 → 3.2 years; IRR rose from 14% → 28% |
| **Environmental Outcome** | 4,800 tCO₂/yr mitigated; SEC dropped 38% (from 412 → 256 kWh/tonne oleoresin) |
| **Strategic Impact** | Enabled Synthite to meet EU REACH & USFDA carbon disclosure norms, securing premium export contracts |

> **Key takeaway:** Stack of **state + central + market** incentives is the game-changer.

**(b) — Challenges and Reforms (7 marks):**

**Challenges (4 marks):**
1. **Procedural complexity:** Multiple agencies (MNRE, state SNA, DISCOM, CERC) have different portals, paperwork, and timelines — typical sanction takes 9–18 months.
2. **Subsidy cap vs. capex inflation:** Capped subsidies (e.g., ₹30 lakh max for biomass) haven't been revised since 2014, eroding real value.
3. **Documentation burden:** DPR, baseline audit, third-party verification cost ₹3–5 lakh — prohibitive for MSMEs.
4. **Incentive exclusivity clauses:** Some state schemes *prohibit* stacking with central subsidy, reducing attractiveness.
5. **Differential GST treatment:** Solar service GST ambiguity (5% vs. 18%) creates ITC reversal issues.

**Reforms (3 marks):**
1. **One Nation, One Portal (ONOP)** for unified application, sanction, and disbursement of all renewable incentives.
2. **Auto-indexed subsidy caps** tied to CPI-IW (Consumer Price Index for Industrial Workers).
3. **Pre-approved DPR templates** for FPI clusters (rice, dairy, spice) to lower transaction cost.
4. **Green Credit Fund** for MSMEs that successfully complete a renewable transition.

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose 3–5 Marks in This Topic:**
> 1. **Conflating REC price** — REC floor price on power exchanges is ≈ ₹1,000/MWh, NOT per kWh. Writing "₹1,000/kWh" is a *lethal* error.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract O&M** when computing payback — most students only divide capex by savings, ignoring recurring costs. The model answer **must show** the O&M subtraction.
> 3. **Mixing up AD rate** — the **current** AD is **40%** (post-2017 amendment), not 80%. Writing 80% in 2024–2025 exam is a *frequently observed error*.
> 4. **Confusing FiT vs. PPA** — FiT is the *regulated tariff*; PPA is the *contractual agreement*. Many students write "PPA price" when they mean "FiT".
> 5. **No mention of discount rate** in NPV — losing 1 mark. Always state **r = 10%** explicitly.
> 6. **Writing "MNRE gives 30% subsidy on all renewable projects"** — false. Subsidy percentages and caps differ by technology (solar thermal, biomass, biogas, etc.). Be precise.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **8 Incentive Types — FORM-CRIB:** Fiscal, Open-access, REC, Metering → Capital, Refinance, Income-tax, Buyback.
- ✅ **Big 5 FPI Sectors** with documented success: **Dairy, Rice, Sugar, Spices, Beverages.**
- ✅ **Key Agencies:** MNRE (central), SECI (solar tendering), BEE (PAT for industries), NABARD (rural refinance), MoFPI (PMKSY scheme), State SNAs (ANERT, TEDA, GEDA).
- ✅ **REC** = 1 MWh; price ≈ ₹1,000/REC; traded on IEX/PXIL.
- ✅ **AD** = 40% in Year 1 (NOT 80% — post-2017).
- ✅ **CII Formula:** $\eta_{incentive} = \frac{\sum I_i}{C_{capex}} \times 100\%$. A "good" stack yields 30–50%.
- ✅ **LCOE post-incentive** must be compared to grid tariff; rule of thumb — if LCOE < 0.7 × grid tariff, project is **highly bankable**.
- ✅ **Stacking** is legal & encouraged; verify with each scheme's guidelines.
- ✅ **DPR quality** is the single biggest determinant of subsidy sanction — invest in a competent consultant.
- ✅ **Carbon Credit revenue** is *additional*, often 5–10% of total incentive value.
- ✅ **Always state** project life (15–25 yr), discount rate (10% benchmark), and grid EF (0.716 kg CO₂/kWh) when computing NPV/IRR/LCOE in the exam.
- ✅ **Payback = Net Capex / Net Annual Savings**, where Net Capex already deducts *capital* incentives only.
- ✅ The **Stacking Engine** is the conceptual heart of any case study — show that ≥2 incentives are combined.
- ✅ **Mnemonic for LCOE formula components:** *PV-of-Costs divided by PV-of-Energy* — both discounted at the same rate.
- ✅ **International analogy:** Germany's *Energiewende*, Denmark's wind cooperatives, and Brazil's PROINFA sugar-ethanol bagasse are textbook international parallels worth mentioning in 14-mark answers for extra marks.
- ✅ **Remember the KTU 2024 NEP alignment:** Course Outcomes (CO4) emphasise "**analyse the policy-economics** of RE in FPI" — case studies are the *primary* assessment vehicle.
